output: Mr President, I would like to apologise once again for my delay.
This year marks the European Year of Intercultural Dialogue.
The involvement of all and the perception that education, knowledge and the intermingling of different cultures with each other is crucial is a given.
For this reason, multiple initiatives associated with this year have been carried out by the different areas of action and intervention.
In this context, the Erasmus Mundus programme also plays its part and the new programme is already present in this new perspective.

The Erasmus Mundus programme makes an important contribution to the creation of centres of excellence in the EU, which will in a way help to stop the brain drain to countries outside the EU, and it also plays a significant role in

in promoting European values to nationals of third countries who come here to study and who find in the Old Continent an unparalleled cultural and linguistic diversity, which acts as an attractive pole and is different from models in the rest of the world.
However, intercultural dialogue has suffered a lot in order to survive.

The problem of student visas is a global one, and the frequent difficulties in obtaining and renewing them mean that they often live in a situation of semi-illegality in the European Union, attending master's courses but with tourist visas, and sometimes being expelled.
I believe that the search for a solution that ensures solidarity, transparency and efficiency in issuing visas for students is of the greatest relevance and urgency.
Knowledge of the language, which allows cultural understanding and mutual coexistence beyond strictly academic mastery, is also crucial, and we must guarantee this as an absolute.
To conclude, a word of thanks to Mrs Désir for the way she conducted the whole process and for the balanced report she produced.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, authorisation for the granting of State aid for the modernisation and renewal of fishing fleets in outermost regions should be possible only until the end of 2009 or should it apply indefinitely?
The particularities of fishing activities in these regions justify that these aids be granted beyond 2009 without the marine environment being harmed in the slightest.
State aid for the modernisation or renewal of fleets in outermost regions for an indefinite period does not mean permanent and eternal aid.
would be immediately suspended if the studies and the evaluation by the Scientific Committee and the regional advisory committees indicated that this was necessary.
This is the solution that would best suit the very specific reality of fisheries in the outermost regions.
The same reasons that justify exclusive fishing reserves for each group of fishermen up to 12 miles also justify differential treatment with regard to aid for modernisation and renewal of the fleet.
Fortunately, the best solutions that can meet the particularities of fisheries in the outermost regions were found in the seat of the Committee on Fisheries.

Mr President, Commissioner Almunia, President of the Eurogroup, I would like to congratulate not only the rapporteurs, but also the Commission, for the initiative of producing this text, the assessment of ten years of Economic and Monetary Union.
This is a strategic text and this analysis was fundamental.
The euro, as was said today, is an undeniable success.

From Lisbon to Helsinki, from Dublin to Bratislava, the euro has shown its strength and resilience, even during the severe turbulence we have experienced, and it is clearly important that the borders of this club be widened.
However, the times in which we live are daily testing the solidity of the European project in many other dimensions.
As I said, the euro is a central instrument for the functioning of the financial system.
However, the Commission's own text clearly points to the fundamental conclusion that neither real economic growth nor convergence, in social and spatial terms, have accompanied this success.
On the contrary, the Commission clearly concludes that disparities have worsened violently during the decade of operation of the single currency.
This divergence affects regions of my country in particular, in particular northern Portugal, but also other regions of Member States.

The impacts of the single monetary policy, particularly the very high exchange rate, have hit hardest the regions that are most exposed to international competition and the most export-oriented regions.
Today, the crisis resulting from the deregulation of financial markets is violently affecting the real economy and worsening the situation of many citizens and many regions.

These regions, which are exposed to international competition, which depend on small and medium-sized enterprises, and where access to credit is more important, are today becoming victims of this process and are even threatened with depression.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Eurogroup, the success of the euro depends on the confidence that European citizens place in it.
The most powerful European countries have already begun the process of relaunching their national economies.
We have the particular case of initiatives relating to the car industry.
But Europe is more than that.
It must be much more than a more or less coordinated sum of national policies.
It is time to transform the euro into a central tool for Europe, supporting not only a strong currency but also a strong real economy, and to ensure that the euro works in tandem
of mechanisms that guarantee the central objective of achieving social and regional convergence, because that is at the heart of the European project.
The debate on the coordination of economic policies in the euro area is today of reinforced and unquestionable impact and relevance.
However, coordinating national policies is not enough.
We need more.

We have a Lisbon Strategy that is a sum of national initiatives and a Stability and Growth Pact that differentially squeezes the different countries that use it and that have to submit to it, and these are limitations to which we must add the limited budget we have.
Crises generate opportunities.
Monetary union can only be sustained by convergence in the welfare of the Member States that make it up, and of their citizens, regardless of where they were born.
What other way would we have of mobilising citizens for the single currency?

The Commission must live up to its own diagnosis, and that is why we are eagerly awaiting the concrete proposals that the Commission will bring to this plenary and present to the European citizens on 26 November.

It is essential that, from this crisis, a new phase of the Union is launched, in which the consolidation and strength of the financial system are accompanied by real prosperity based on cohesion and also on economic citizenship.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, this oral question tabled by Mr Galiotto on behalf of the Committee on Regional Development has every justification and relevance.

In fact, the Council needs to make clear the reasons why it is blocking the reform of the Solidarity Fund, as it is unable to reach agreement on a common position that will allow the legislative process to move forward.
It is difficult to fathom the reasons underlying the Council ’ s position, except perhaps for petty financial reasons.
Does the Council not want a more agile and immediate response to the natural disasters that occur?
I do not want the Council to give this type of Seveso-type response to other types of disaster, such as serious industrial accidents, terrorist attacks or serious public health situations.

It is important that these questions receive a clear answer, as it is also important to clarify what the European Commission thinks of this situation and whether it intends to take any measures to unblock the legislative process.
The value of solidarity must also be fully implemented in this area.
The citizens would not understand at all if this were not the case.
I would like to say that I would like to protest against the statement made by Mrs Lulling, which attempts to condition the vote of the Members in this House, and this conditioning makes no sense.

The interpretation you are giving is one that we do not share, and it is precisely in this sense that I would like to ask colleagues who know what they are dealing with, who know that we are defending other interests, namely the interests of public health and the interests of citizens and the interests of countries, to vote in favour of the report. Thank you very much.
Mr President, Commissioner, for two months, the political and media agenda has been dominated by the financial and economic crisis.
The referendum in Ireland or the difficulties in the Czech Republic over the Treaty of Lisbon are hardly mentioned in the media.

Do you not think that the Commission's information and communication strategy should now be reinforced, given the need for and urgency of the entry into force of the Lisbon Treaty, as a political response to the crisis we are experiencing?
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the world powers have the international financial system in their hands at the moment.
They are looking for a German date, and it was no longer a matter of time.
Perhaps it is time, then, to solve another problem that we have been talking about for decades.
For decades, we have been hearing this music of pillage and plunder of resources, as many times as now in the Congo, on the blood of the populations.

I believe that it is also time to pursue these companies, to pursue this ignoble trade, to freeze the accounts through which the shameful profits of this exploitation of minerals at the cost of the blood and suffering of the populations circulate and are deposited.
I do not understand why these companies are not listed in the same way that we already have a blacklist of terrorist organisations.
I do not understand why the international community is not able to pursue these entrepreneurs, who are not entrepreneurs at all, but real bandits, who endanger regional and global security.
I encourage the French Presidency and the Commission to take an international initiative in this respect. Thank you very much.
We have heard here today several times that a crisis normally creates an opportunity, and that is true.
If we want to take advantage of the opportunity that is open to us, the agenda is easy to know.
Firstly, we know at European Union level that we need to introduce transparency, regulation and supervision mechanisms in the financial markets.
Commissioner McCreevy does not need to launch more consultations, nor does the Presidency need to analyse and call for more studies and
more proposals, just look at the proposals that were made in good time by the Socialists and many of which were taken up by this Parliament.
Secondly, internationally, Europe cannot be passive on the international stage.
It must be an active agent in building the new international order; it must be the defender of a new architecture that does not leave black holes in the system, such as tax havens at present.
Thirdly, today we have heard the President of the Commission say that there is reluctance to coordinate strategies at the level of the different Member States.
It is true that if we had not heard from many members of the Commission for so long that markets self-regulate and that the State should stay out of it,
that financial markets do not have an impact on growth or on employment, perhaps there would be a more active and more effective spirit of solidarity between the different countries.
However, the proposal we have before us today is only a first step, if we compare it with the programme of China and the United States. On the other hand, it
does not clearly define what the objective is, and the objective has to be employment, and thirdly, because a European programme cannot be a summing-up of national agendas.
It is time for us to show European citizens that Europe is a network of rights, including economic rights, that protect them in times of crisis, in terms of

solidarity, of the growth programme and of support for small and medium-sized enterprises, which at the moment are not reaping the benefits of the interventions that have been made in the banking sector.
The real economy is just that, and we are here, as Europeans, to show the citizens that Europe is here too, and above all, because of them. Thank you very much.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I am particularly pleased to present to you the work done in this own-initiative report, which was done in cooperation with you all.
For me and for all those who collaborated in the development of this process, we can say that we have done our job.

to everyone, particularly the shadow rapporteurs from the various political groups, who expressed their willingness to reach compromises for the future, to the staff of the Committee on Regional Development, who were always available, especially Miguel Teles Cremades and Elisa da Faria.

to the staff of the PSE Group, to LiLà and to Petros, to the European Commission, which, always represented by the many and varied Directorates-General that a rapporteur's report requires, followed the work closely and constantly and made every effort to see it through to a successful conclusion.

to my office and, in particular, to Joana Benzingo, and to all of you, my very sincere thanks, on behalf of more harmonious and structured development for coastal regions and for tourism in the European Union.
The 27 EU Member States have a combined total of more than 89 000 km of coastline.

This region has a variety of very particular characteristics that, depending on their location, distinguish it either by cosmopolitanism, if we talk about cities such as Lisbon, Copenhagen or Stockholm, or

due to the difficulties of being peripheral or outermost regions, with problems of connections to major urban areas or with accelerated desertification, such as the Algarve, Liguria, the Canary Islands or Madeira.
In one way or another, we all identify with the concept of coast and coastal zone and live daily with the advantages and disadvantages associated with them.
According to the data available, by 2010, about 75% of humanity will live in coastal areas. These areas
According to the circuits and relationships between sites, we defined the 50 km in a straight line from the coastline to the inland areas.
Yes, regions and not maritime strips, detached from an integrated vision, which these matters so lack and which our ancestors would also endorse.
All these people will be drawn here in search of opportunities and economic synergies, and in many cases, they will only expect tourism to be the focus.
Thus, the obvious need for a pragmatic, integrated vision of the impact of tourism on coastal areas took hold of our minds and led us to get to work.

In the current financial crisis, in which the impact on the real economy is becoming increasingly insurmountable, tourism appears as a sector with enormous potential to be affected directly or indirectly in a brutal way.

Regions that are overwhelmingly or totally dependent on this for their development see their activities threatened and look to the future with mistrust, especially because tourism is not currently an EU competence.
However, no measures can or should be taken, and the spirit in which the Treaty of Lisbon was drafted reflects this same tendency.
However, waiting until the Treaty comes into force before starting to act is like waiting until after the milk has been spilt to start crying.
The political and tourist activity that is present, and the fragile reality of the regions that depend on it, require urgent and effective action from us.

The fact that these are areas that are structurally dependent on tourism as a source of employment, despite the fact that this is often seasonal employment, means that this labour-intensive activity cannot be overlooked in a context of pressure on urban areas and unemployment.

That is why this report that we are presenting here now, which was already of the greatest importance and urgency when the Development Committee of this House decided to promote it, now assumes that it must be seen as a priority by the Commission and by the European Council.

Priority should be given to the numerous initiatives it includes, as well as to those it takes up and expresses, and which have already been put forward by other institutions, alongside the measures adopted in the emergency plan.

of the Commission, of which I would highlight the review of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund, in which the issues associated with this sector, and the shock to which it is currently subject, must obviously be covered.

It is crucial to ensure the development of new sectors of the economy in these coastal areas, guaranteeing their social and environmental sustainability and promoting the real integration of the different sectoral policies, such as the maritime sector, transport,

energy, the cohesion instruments already in place, the new quality products policy incorporated in the CAP reform, the announcement of the Elleschek report and the new tourist products in these coastal areas, bearing in mind their crucial contribution to the European economy.
The implementation of a suitable and holistic vision for this policy must soon become a reality in the non-EU countries.

Finally, Mr President, only through a clear articulation of these instruments and through swift and effective action involving all the partners and stakeholders on the ground will we be able to guarantee that we have a sustainable coastal tourism sector with a future in the European Union. Thank you.
Mr President, Commissioner, Minister, e-Justice is an important step towards access to the law, to justice and to courts.
I would like to congratulate the initiative, the rapporteur and the French Presidency.

e-Justice was one of the projects under discussion, and Portugal, during its Presidency, will lead the development and implementation of the pilot project, which will allow all citizens to access services in other Member States in a simpler, cheaper, more comfortable way and in their own language.
This multilingual portal should be designed to serve citizens and businesses seeking legal assistance and basic legal advice on cross-border legal problems.
Eurojust should be coordinated and managed by a responsible body, which will also coordinate the contributions from the various Member States and link them to this responsible body.
e-Justice will serve the citizen and strengthen our democracy.
I welcome the launch of the portal in December 2009. Thank you.
Mr President, Mr Barrot, ladies and gentlemen, I congratulate Carlos Coelho, Ms Martin and the other shadow rapporteurs on their work.
In December 2004, the Council adopted a regulation establishing standards for the security features and biometric data of passports and travel documents issued by the Member States.
Now we need to make new headway in combating child abduction and trafficking.
The use of passports by children, in accordance with the principle of one person, one passport, could be a fundamental way of winning this difficult and important battle.
In my country, Portugal, collecting fingerprints from children from the age of six is already a long-established practice, and perhaps for that reason, it does not raise any objections from me.
As a defender of the European cause, I believe that harmonisation is important in this area.
I am reassured to know that Member States such as Portugal, which have long defined a minimum age of six years for fingerprint collection, will not have to change their national legislation.
It should be stressed that the security of the passports being strengthened today does not end with the existence of the passport.

The passport alone corresponds to an increase in the level of security, ranging from the presentation of the necessary documents for issuing the passports, followed by the collection of biometric data, to verification and matching at border control posts.
This report is another step towards affirming people ’ s rights and guaranteeing their safety.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Alexander Lambsdorff, and the shadow rapporteurs, with particular mention of Barbara Weiler.
Ladies and gentlemen, I believe it is essential that we move forward towards the integration of national defence markets and even the strategic coordination of their production.

We must move forward, effectively, with the specificities and precautions necessary in applying the essential rules of the internal market to the defence industries, increasing transparency in the tenders that are organised and in the contracts that are signed involving EU Member States.
At the same time, however, we must help to develop the conditions that will allow European equipment and products to be more competitive on world markets.

Commissioner McCreevy, I believe it is important that this directive should guarantee progress in the legal framework for public contracts in the defence and security sectors, moving forward with the construction of the internal market in

internal market, while respecting, of course, the interests and rights of Member States, particularly in the area of security and specifically in the manufacture of weapons, ammunition and security equipment, such as my own country.

Mr President, I would also highlight the provisions that make it easier for small and medium-sized enterprises to access this market, particularly through the improvement of sub-contracting rules and the prospect of making greater progress with greater impact and depth in the decision

industrial, to create a real European defence market, which could also contribute to strengthening research and development, not only in the defence industries but also in a range of European industries that benefit from the development of the defence industries.
To conclude, I think we can refer to the obvious link between the defence industry and the Union ’ s foreign policies.
However, I would like to quote a recent speech by Mr Dettmer, translating a classic Roman saying: 'If you want peace, prepare for war'.

The European Union, as it is almost a peaceful superpower, calls these industries 'defence industries' and not 'war industries', because we want peace and not war, and that is why we also need defence industries.
Vice-President Barrot, I agree with you, I agree with you on the dissatisfaction.
In fact, we have to be satisfied with the set of measures, which is vast.
The truth is that irregular immigration continues to Europe, human trafficking continues, there are areas where there is a great deal of
migration, particularly in the Mediterranean and towards Italy. France has advanced with a set of important bilateral agreements, but other Member States have not done so.
The Commission must continue, without losing enthusiasm, with this policy of cooperation and aid to the Member States.
Mr President, Commissioner, I am going to try to ask the questions in a very concise way.
First point: the issue of the special monitoring mechanism of the European Union for the Western Balkans, which ended, as was said here, on 31 December 2008.
Madam Commissioner, we need the timely and routine publication of statistics on imports, exports and prices, just as happens in the United States.
Without this data, the European Union is unable to react to any unfair practices, and the Commission itself is unable to define its strategy.
I would like to draw attention to this, and I agree with what other Members have already said.
Secondly, producing within the European Union increasingly requires compliance with safety standards, social standards and environmental standards.
The RITS, for example, is a recent initiative that creates even more constraints for us.
It is important that the Commission has a clear strategy, which is perceptible and capable of being monitored, so that imported products have identical requirements.
How are these aspects being properly safeguarded in the free trade agreements currently under way?
And what about Maiduguri? Can Maiduguri really help solve this issue?
Thirdly, the European Union has launched an economic recovery plan to combat the current crisis.
What role will trade policy play in this context?

At this time, what is the Commission preparing to do when a number of other countries, such as China, begin to proliferate the introduction of non-tariff barriers, increasingly so that our European imports do not gain access to the Chinese markets?

What proposals does it have for updating or adjusting the Globalisation Fund, as well as the support available under the Structural Funds, to improve the immediate situation of the European textile industry?
Last point, finally, will the crisis we are going through finally make the Commission sensitive to the devastating impact of an overvalued euro on the European economy?
How can the Commissioner sensitize her colleagues and the bodies dealing with European monetary policy to rebalancing?
It is clear to us that we should applaud Mr Obama ’ s decision to close Guantánamo, to ban torture and illegal interrogations and to suspend military trials.
However, Mr Posselt, who is no longer here, Portugal and the Portuguese Socialist Government do not feel guilty in any way.
The Portuguese socialist government did not collaborate with the Bush administration; on the contrary.

It was precisely now, with the Portuguese administration, with the Portuguese Government, with the initiative of Minister Luísa Amaral, that the possibility and duty of the European Union and democratic countries to welcome and receive those detained without charge in Guantánamo was triggered.

That is what is truly important, and that is what should be highlighted as an example for the other Member States of the European Union to follow. It is important that tomorrow the resolution be adopted by all the political groups in this House, so that, united, the decision can have greater force.
Mr President, I would like to thank Ms Napoletano for her excellent report.

Fourteen years after Barcelona and five years after the first UfM Parliamentary Assembly, we have the Union for the Mediterranean, with economic power, with parliamentary power, with civil society, with Heads of State and Government deciding together.
We all want to do more and better in a region where 720 million citizens represent a potential for development and peace for the world.
To limit ourselves to the economic and business side is clearly wrong.

Responding to growth crises naturally requires a political response, but responding to humanitarian crises, emergency situations and military crises, such as those experienced at the end of 2008 and the beginning of 2009, is crucial.
Politicians and citizens alike must provide answers to the world's problems.
The world asked for answers, and months later we are still slow to react, both citizens and politicians, particularly in a platform such as the Union for the Mediterranean.

I hope that at the very least this moment has served to learn and move forward, because only those who make mistakes learn, but moving forward in terms of speeding up the reaction is absolutely crucial.

The interest in the adoption of this directive, of this proposed directive, lies essentially in the way it can contribute to boosting economic activity at local level and supporting social initiatives.
The application of a reduced rate is very important in the housing sector, both in construction and in renovation, allowing Member States that adopt it to reduce the cost for the end user.

The provision of local services, particularly by small enterprises, may also benefit from this reduction in the VAT rate, securing and generating more and better jobs through the encouragement of consumption of goods and services, thus stimulating the economy itself.
The restaurant trade is vital for the proper functioning of local economies, and tourism itself can gain a lot at local level from this reduction in VAT.

Ladies and gentlemen, these services should not be allowed to circulate on the European internal market, which will help to reduce the reserves of some Member States already verified in the Council plan.
I therefore cannot agree with the extension of this reduced rate to equipment, whatever the pretext.

The prudent way in which this proposal should be treated in the European Parliament should not give governments that have some reservations about the Commission proposal an excuse to use the veto right that European legislation gives them in fiscal matters.
We therefore have to prudently reject amendments that make little sense and only make it more difficult to accept the text.
As has already been said here, not only is the best the enemy of the good, but he who wants everything loses everything.
This proposal for a directive is important not only for boosting local economies, but also because it corresponds to the ambitions repeatedly expressed by various Member States.
We therefore support the Commission proposal as it was presented. That was, in fact, the sense of the vote, as well as the agreement of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection.

I welcome the work of the rapporteurs and call for the adoption of this proposal in the exact terms in which it was tabled by the Commission and adopted by the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection. Thank you very much.
Mr President, I have taken the floor to disagree with my colleague, Mrs Estrela, and it was precisely that which prompted me to speak, when she caricatured the intentions of the Czech Presidency.
Nobody wants to send women home; the problem is that there are women who want to or would like to stay at home.
There are even associations that try to defend women who are ignored, despised and discriminated against because they want to devote themselves to their families, and we do not listen to them.
This is a matter of freedom, a matter of freedom of choice, which the left so likes to invoke, but which it forgets here.
It is a question of respecting or not the freedom of orientation of couples.
It is about being able to provide those who want to do so with a better quality of life, better paternity and better maternity leave.
That is what our society also needs to do.
We will not solve people's fertility and birth problems with prejudice.
We solve them with policies adapted to reality and to people ' s spontaneous desires.
We are not going there with an obsession with the State and with the market.
Let us take a fair look at the family.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the difficult situation at Qimonda has been made worse by the emergence of the financial and economic crisis.
The Portuguese Government has done everything it could to help resolve the situation.
We would like the Bavarian Government to act responsibly in this area as well.
Saving the Munich facilities will have a decisive impact on the Vila do Conde and Dresden units.

The Portuguese Minister, Manuel Pinho, himself expressed great interest in making this company viable, as it is so important for Portugal and for Europe, saying that Qimonda could be a competitive company worldwide.
Mr President, the Portuguese Government must continue its efforts to find a solution that guarantees the viability of the company.
We hope that the German governments, both federal and state, will effectively engage in resolving this problem.
As for me, I would like to stress once again the strategic importance of maintaining this type of industry in Europe.
No national or state executive should make the mistake of allowing this company to close plants and liquidate jobs in the territory of the Union.
Ladies and gentlemen, we understand the need to help restructure the large automotive groups, but the Union funds and the energies of the European Commission cannot be exhausted on these bail-outs.

Commissioner, we are calling on you to commit the Commission to supporting Qimonda, overcoming any possible communication failures. We are making this appeal to Commissioner Špidla, with whom we share links of work and political solidarity, and to President Barroso, who will never forget that he is our compatriot and that a significant number of Qimonda workers are also our compatriots.

We should stress, before concluding, that the Portuguese President, Cavaco Silva, recently stated in Germany that he saw new hope for Qimonda, and we should also mention the importance, as other Members have already done, of the economic sector in which Qimonda operates.

We therefore take into account the fact that the Portuguese Government has expressed its willingness to support Qimonda with whatever is necessary, bearing in mind the economic dimension of the country.
Ladies and gentlemen, let us help to save Qimonda: time is running out.

Madam President, Commissioner, we have been talking about the Lisbon Strategy for years, and the times in which we live may not be the most appropriate for talking about the Lisbon Strategy, but there is no doubt that we need a strategy.
a strategy that responds to the difficulties and challenges that the crisis currently poses to us.
That is what we also expect from the Commission.
It is essential that the Commission does not shy away from this issue and instead has the ability to coordinate actions with the Governments of Portugal and Germany and the German Länder.

It is essential to bear in mind that this is an industry, as has already been said, which is of interest to Europe, because of its quality and value, because of the research it carries out and because of the environmental quality associated with it, and it is essential that the Commission does not turn its back on it.
I support the invitation made by my colleague, Mr Peneda, for the Commissioner and the Commission to visit Portugal.

The Commission must bear in mind that, at this time, Europeans are looking to Europe and expect answers from the European authorities; they need to feel that the European authorities are close to them.
European citizens do not understand a Europe that washes its hands; they want a Europe that has the courage to get its hands dirty and to roll up its sleeves to help them overcome their difficulties.
Mr President, I would also like to add my tribute to Mr Monney, but I asked for the floor to welcome the President for his statement on the European Day of Remembrance for Victims of Terrorism.
It was my proposal that five years ago we adopted this approach, which the Council enshrined in a meeting on 25 March, following the tragic terrorist attacks in Madrid.
But my speech was a plea.
Parliament has always been faithful in marking this date, but unfortunately this date has not yet had the visibility it should have among the European institutions and the Member States.
I believe that marking this date is one of the most important tools we have for paying tribute to the victims, as the President has just done, but also for raising public awareness.
I know that there are celebrations in Madrid today, but nothing more.
My appeal, therefore, to the Commission and to the Czech Presidency is that all the Member States celebrate this date with dignity in the future.
Ladies and gentlemen, the crisis is deeper than we expected and unemployment will be higher than we forecast.
There are good reasons to suppose that the European stimulus package is not sufficient, but it is clear already that it is taking too long to reach the citizens.
Parliament ’ s position is strong and clear, and has been, and I hope will continue to be.
Our objective is to maintain employment and to create new jobs, with cohesion, with solidarity, both territorial and social.
At this time of crisis, citizens cannot be content with a Europe that does not respond to them, with a Europe that is impotent in the face of the problems they are experiencing.
What is Parliament going to ask the Commission?

It naturally calls, through these reports, for national interventions to be coordinated, for it to use all the means currently at its disposal to intervene, and for it to be given all the conditions as a budgetary body to make this happen.

I ask you to launch a clear European initiative for employment and tell you that it is important to have an agenda with a calendar to implement the measures to regulate the financial market, as well as to get credit to the real economy.
But what is being asked of the Council?
I ask the Council to rediscover, above all, the political will that lies at the heart of the European project.
The European Union is competition, but it is also cohesion and solidarity.
There cannot be a single market without this guarantee of solidarity and cohesion.
That is why we all delegated at European level the national autonomy that we had before joining this project.

Madam President, Commissioner, anyone who visits sub-Saharan Africa can easily see the enormous weakness of the health systems in most of the countries there and the extremely negative impact this has on the lives and health of the populations that those services should serve.
The indicators continually published at international level never cease to confirm this.
In this respect, it is absolutely disturbing to think that simple gestures and practices, not even very elaborate or particularly expensive, could be sufficient to save many lives.

European financial support could be crucial in this respect, and we must always bear in mind that cooperation in the health sector is truly strategic and directly affects not just one of the Millennium Development Goals, but many of them.

The Court of Auditors concluded that, and I quote: 'Community funding for the health sector has not increased since 2000 in proportion to total development aid', despite the commitments made by the Commission in relation to the Millennium Development Goals and the health crisis in sub-Saharan Africa.
End of quote.

It acknowledged – I quote again – that the Commission had made a significant contribution, through its funding, to the establishment of the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, but had not paid the same attention to strengthening health systems, which was expected to be one of its priorities.
End of quote.

According to the Court, this will happen again because the Commission did not have sufficient experience in health matters to ensure that the funding granted by it to this sector was used in the best way.
End of quote.
This is therefore a strong challenge that the Court of Auditors is launching directly at the European Commission, and which I support.
On our side, I would like to reiterate this same challenge, now supported by the objectivity of these data and this assessment.
Health services are already part of our development aid priorities, but they should be increasingly so, and they deserve increased funding.

Optimising the way in which aid is provided, bearing in mind the apparently antagonistic needs of coordination in its management and proximity to the beneficiary populations, is providing a service that can save many lives.
The European Commission cannot fail to respond positively to this appeal, and I urge it to do so.
Mr Bowis just now made a moving speech here and was able to put human faces on the cold, hard figures of the Court of Auditors.
The challenge for us, Commissioner, is for our cooperation to be able to put a look of joy and hope on these very same faces.
To do this, Commissioner, it is essential that we have different figures in the area of health in our cooperation.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, once again, for sad reasons, we are discussing here the situation in Guinea-Bissau, which is a truly tragic situation.
It is a country that has been living with chronic instability for many years, and that tried to embark on a path towards democracy in the early 1990s.

Nothing went well, there was a coup, a small civil war, and they live in great instability: political, military, tensions, deep rivalries, and now, recently, also the presence of the drug trade, which is very worrying and increasingly evident to all observers.
We strongly condemn these attacks, the bombing death of Chief of Staff Tagme Na Wai and also the assassination, in terms that are particularly barbaric, if not savage, of President Nino Vieira.
Regardless of his past, we express our solidarity with his family, with the people of Guinea-Bissau, and we regret and strongly condemn these attacks.
We hope for a return to normality, and the lesson that I draw and would like to highlight in the resolution is that impunity is not the answer.
In the past, with regard to the murder of Suma Mané and of the journalist Lúcio Siavira, we turned a blind eye to the fact that the culprits were not found and brought to justice.
It is clear that this is not the solution.

We therefore have to make it clear to the State of Guinea-Bissau that those responsible have to be found, the guilty parties have to be brought to justice and we have to provide all the help that is necessary.

I would also like to draw attention, to conclude, to the concern we have about the presence of narco-traffickers throughout the region, the risk that this represents for the European Union and the shocking presence of this phenomenon in Guinea-Bissau.
I would also like to call for us to strengthen our relations with Cape Verde in this context.

We have established a special partnership with Cape Verde. Cape Verde has very close relations with Guinea, it has a great deal of knowledge, but it is also very vulnerable, and this is essential for our own European security.
Therefore, stepping up the special partnership with Cape Verde is also very important in this context.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to thank everyone for their hard work.

The three lines defined in the Green Paper – concentration, interconnection and cooperation – can provide solutions to some of the obstacles to the Union's harmonious development, particularly the negative effects associated with

concentration of economic activity, inequalities in access to markets and services due to distance, and the divisions imposed by borders between Member States, particularly those that are less well-off, but also between regions.
We must therefore promote the improvement of synergies between these policies with effective methods for assessing their territorial impact.

For this reason, I have always advocated the definition of supplementary qualitative indicators, with the aim of improving the implementation and execution of the relevant policies on the ground, taking into account the different territorial specificities.
For the time being, GDP remains the only criterion for eligibility to receive support from the Structural Funds.

However, the definition of supplementary indicators and the carrying out of territorial evaluations should not give rise to more bureaucracy or delays, but rather to simplification in the application of new policies and actions to support territorial cohesion.

The fifth report by Mr Kallas – I am about to finish, Mr President – makes a specific reference to the transition regions, located between the convergence regions and the competitiveness and employment regions.
We need to pay attention to the fact that these regions need to have a clearer status, with greater security and stability for their development.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in the context of this major debate on territorial cohesion and the future of economic and social cohesion policy, I cannot fail to mention the very specific issue of the outermost regions.

Regions that, due to the combination and permanence of a number of geographical factors, are particularly vulnerable economically and socially, especially in the context of a serious international crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.
I would therefore like to appeal to the European Commission and, in particular, to Commissioner Hübner, to pay very special attention to the effects of the current crisis on the outermost regions.

An assessment of these effects in each of the outermost regions, particularly in tourism, construction and unemployment, would be important in order to allow a specific European response for these regions.

I therefore challenge the European Commission to prepare a European response to the crisis for the outermost regions that goes beyond the measures already announced under the economic and social cohesion policy for the whole of Europe's regions.
A specific European response to the crisis for the most vulnerable.

Mr President, in response to the global financial crisis, the European economic recovery plan states that cohesion policy makes a significant contribution to public investment by Member States and regions, and should act as a means of recovering from the current crisis.
It specifically suggests adopting actions in priority areas of the Lisbon Strategy in order to achieve growth and employment.
All the instruments used aim to achieve this purpose and speed up the attainment of results.

In this respect, the extension of the scope of the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund and the streamlining of the European Social Fund have a particular responsibility to respond to the multiple situations of social and economic emergency that require support.
I am certain that, today as in the past, its action will be all the more effective the more complementary its actions and areas of activity are.

However, as no new funds or new action lines in this area have been provided, it is particularly important that the European Social Fund (ESF) be used to its full potential in combating unemployment and the rapidly increasing pressure on competitiveness in the European economy, resulting from the current financial crisis and economic downturn.

I would like to stress that the Committee on Regional Development has repeatedly identified simplification as essential for improving the management and implementation of the Structural Funds, as the European Commission is now proposing.
Urgency was therefore dismissed in this matter and we took this into account when adopting this package of regulations, and this is why the House never shirked its political responsibilities.

Therefore, despite the many serious views that the particular nature of the moment raises, in order to guarantee the smooth running of the process and the real benefit for citizens that this proposal aims to achieve, let us refrain from tabling any new proposals for amendments at this stage.
However, we are warning that we need to start a process of evaluation of this fund right away, with a view to carrying out a complementary review as soon as possible.
Madam President, among other things, the crisis has highlighted the serious imbalances between the countries of the euro area, perhaps the main
conclusion of the Economic and Monetary Union report, but also the extreme vulnerability of countries that belong to the European Union but not to the euro area.

That is why it is absolutely essential to increase the resources available at European level to support balance of payments crises, and that is why we welcome the increase from EUR 12 to EUR 25 and then to EUR 50 million in the necessary resources.
However, more important is to attack the root causes of the problems, not just their most obvious manifestations.
Today, the enlarged Europe and the euro area must reinterpret the meaning of European solidarity and the objectives of real cohesion and convergence.
We need to look at the capacity of European horizontal policies in that light, but we also need to revisit new financing instruments such as Eurobonds.
Financial assistance to the balance of payments is necessary, but is a long way from being sufficient to guarantee the sustainability of the European project and the single European currency.
Madam President, President of the Commission, President of the Council, we are in an unprecedented crisis.

It was not the time to discuss internally how we should organise ourselves. Therefore, my votes are in favour of the Treaty of Lisbon being ratified as quickly as possible so that we can face the world scene united and in solidarity as Europeans.

As regards the economic crisis, the report that I drafted and which this Parliament adopted by a large majority acknowledged that the recovery plan presented by the Commission so far is not sufficient.
Today, we are certain that it is not sufficient.
A crisis that began in the financial sector is spreading to the real economy, to jobs and to social issues.
It is not sufficient and is vague.

Every crisis brings with it an opportunity. This is the opportunity for the Commission to respond to the real problems of Europeans through serious financial regulation, not the lightest and most superficial that could be found.

possible, as is currently the case, not through individual national plans, but through a genuine European initiative; not through scattered means, but by focusing on the real challenge, which is employment.

In every crisis there is an opportunity. This is the opportunity for the Commission and for Europe to respond to the real problems of European citizens, and I hope that this will happen through a new approach to the recovery plan.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to express, not only on behalf of the EPP, as many Members have already done, but particularly on behalf of the Portuguese Members of the EPP, our great satisfaction with the result of the referendum in Ireland.
We believe that this clearly strengthens the prospects for the development and evolution of the European Union, creating the conditions for a new stage.

We would like to congratulate all those who contributed to the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular the Commission and the Vice-President of the Commission who is here present, and also, of course, the Portuguese Presidency of the Union, which

had a very important role here. And that is why we are also very concerned to see that, despite all these vicissitudes, and still keeping our expectations for the position of the Czech Republic, the Portuguese will see with great joy the name of their capital linked

a decisive step in the evolution of the European Union, and I would like to say that we can also count, in this perspective, on another banner, which will always be a banner of the Portuguese Members of the EPP Group, which is the banner of national parliaments.
We believe that the Treaty of Lisbon takes a decisive step towards incorporating the presence of national parliaments into European democracy.

We therefore believe that this dimension, which is the most important dimension of democracy, the representative dimension, and not, as we often hear in this Chamber, the referendum dimension, what is actually

fundamental to democracy is representation, not direct voting. The Treaty of Lisbon is also a big step towards increasing the participation of national representative democracies in the European process.
We are therefore very pleased with this decision by the Polish Presidency.

Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to make a brief speech to welcome the historic and resounding result of the referendum in Ireland and to highlight the decisive role played by Fine Gael and its leader and next Irish Prime Minister, Enda Kenny, in taking the lead in this process.
It was a process in which it became very clear that the European Union needs to get much closer to its citizens.
They are the main beneficiaries of all the EU ’ s actions, as are we all.
It is essential that everyone is aware of the impact and benefits that Europe has on our lives every day.
Europe is our vital space.
What is good for Europe is good for each Member State, and good for its citizens.
This is certainly the case with the Treaty of Lisbon.
With this treaty, it becomes much clearer that you can be at the same time a proud and committed national citizen and a fervent supporter of the European project.

The European Union, having concluded a debate that has lasted too long on its organisation and functioning, will be able to concentrate much more, and with much more effective new instruments, on the real problems facing Europeans: competitiveness, growth and employment.
I will conclude, Mr President, Minister, with a proposal for future generations.

Given the lack of information on the values, powers, objectives and way in which the European Union operates, the European Parliament should propose, at the end of compulsory schooling in each Member State, a new compulsory subject on European studies.
In an objective, true and concrete way, young Europeans will learn what our principles are, who we are, what we do and where we want to go.

It is through the knowledge of these young people that they will understand throughout their lives the potential and enormous usefulness of European identity, of this unique project of voluntary sharing of sovereignty on a continental scale.

Mr President, Mr President of the Commission, let me begin by congratulating you on this first debate, on what this new instrument of political control means for the advancement and development of parliamentary democracy in Europe.
It is the European citizens, through their representatives, who stand to gain.

Taking into account the most recent developments, the Irish referendum, the ratification by Poland and the latest statements by the President of the Czech Republic, I would like to ask what your assessment is of the process for the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon?
The President of the Commission has taken action.
and what is the forecast for a possible date for the entry into force of the treaty, and on the other hand, to know whether, at this moment and in view of this forecast, the Commission
some transitional measures have already been taken, from the Treaty of Nice to the Treaty of Lisbon, or whether we are still in a phase of, let us say, waiting, therefore a phase of waiting to see.
Mr President of the Commission, having heard this, I would like to know the following: at this stage, when everyone is awaiting the formation of the Commission, what is your outlook?
Will we now have a Commission in caretaker status until the Czech Republic completes its final ratification procedures, or will the procedures for appointing the new Commissioners go ahead?

Mr President, it is essential that the primary objective of the creation of the European External Action Service is met by the efforts of the European institutions: to ensure the creation of an effective service, capable of coordinating, formulating and implementing a European response to today ’ s international challenges.
It must represent more than the sum of its parts; it must be an added value compared to the current efforts of the Member States and the European Union.
In this respect, it seems essential to me that what makes the Union unique should be at the heart of this new reality, and that uniqueness is the community method.
That is why I fully support the Brok report, which defends and safeguards close cooperation between the Commission and the future Service.

I would like to highlight two aspects in this regard: the importance of close coordination between the President of the Commission and the High Representative, who is also Vice-President of the Commission, in order to ensure its effectiveness and smooth running.
Finally, there is a need for an alliance between Parliament and the Commission to combat the intergovernmental drift that is looming and that could compromise the effective operation of the Service.
Parliament should therefore be vigilant and ensure that the European External Action Service is a centre of excellence, representative of the best expertise in foreign policy.
Mr President, Commissioner, first of all, I would like to stress the importance of this issue raised by Mr Florenz, because it is a matter of the most basic justice.

We all know perfectly well that in a situation of flu pandemic, it will be the countries of the South that suffer most from this problem and from the consequences of the proliferation of the virus, and not the countries of the North.

Every year, we stand by and do nothing as millions of citizens die as a result of pandemics that have already been eradicated or have treatments in the countries of the North: a long list, including malaria, dengue, yellow fever and tuberculosis.
We have no limits to this list and we are passively witnessing this problem.

We effectively use the countries of the South as suppliers of free samples of new virus strains so that we can improve treatment in the countries of the North, but we still have no effective response to these problems. Therefore, I would like to add a few more questions to those that have already been asked.
Do we really want to treat this problem as a public health problem or as a business?
Because if it is a public health problem, we have to guarantee equal access to treatment in all regions of the world, and for this, efforts have to be more effective.
On the other hand, I would like to ask, and I hope this is not the case, whether we are waiting for the vaccine stocks in Europe to become so overstocked that
The secondary effects are so negative in this respect that we need to show generosity and suddenly start sending the necessary vaccines to the countries of the South.

Finally, I would just like to ask whether the European Union, whether we Europeans and whether the Commission feel comfortable with a system or a model where countries in the north deal with pandemics through a prevention model and countries in the south deal with them through palliative care or just with the leftovers.

Mr President, first of all, I would like to express my full support for the question raised by Mr Rübig and, above all, to highlight the fundamental issue, the underlying political issue in this matter, which is precisely joint programming of research.

What happened with the joint programming on neurodegenerative diseases and, in particular, on Alzheimer's was that, in the middle of the process, the rules changed: we stopped having a report and started having a resolution, thus eliminating our co-decision-making capacity in this matter. We stopped being MEPs and became consultants.
I would therefore like to know whether at least what we recommend will be taken into account or not.
The programming of research activities, whatever the subject, is a political choice, not a technical one, and in this respect Parliament's role should be valued and strengthened.
It seems to me that defining political priorities means that they must be scrutinised, that they must be transparent and that they must be democratic.
The process that took place with regard to the joint decision and joint programming of research on Alzheimer 's disease is one that must not be repeated.

I would like to repeat, please, that you should give us advance warning. I hope, therefore, that the last thing that can happen is that the decisions and recommendations that Parliament has put forward in this matter are not taken into account.
Mr President, in the last month, Europe ’ s regions have been in the spotlight.
The European Week of Regions and Cities has consolidated the regions ’ position as a fundamental part of the European response to global challenges.
That is what President Barroso, Commissioner Siimes and the hundreds of debates that have taken place have concluded, leaving no room for doubt.

Likewise, at a more specific level, the important meeting of the Conference of Presidents of the Outermost Regions of Europe, which for the first time included the participation of Members of the European Parliament, was a milestone in regional policy in support of European integration.
Global challenges and European responses represent an excellent opportunity for a new strategy for the EU.

These regions, such as the Azores, in addition to the permanent constraints that we are all used to hearing about, have a range of potential that gives us clear opportunities to achieve our progress and give Europe a clear advantage in new areas essential to responding to global challenges.

That is why the President of the Commission must put a clear end to the palace coup that the Commission's ' no paper ' indicates was being prepared, while we, Members and European citizens, were praising the ratification of the Lisbon Treaty and its importance in defending territorial cohesion.

First of all, I would like to welcome the rapporteurs, Mr Martin and also Mr Corbett, and say that, as a member of the PPE Group, I basically support the amendments proposed to the rules of procedure.
I would essentially like to highlight what seems to me to be an important point that has not yet been raised here this evening, which is the point of national parliaments.

The democratic reinforcement of the European Parliament undoubtedly results from this regime and from the Treaty of Lisbon, but European democracy also results from the link with national parliaments.
What this system, or what this proposal is now doing, is precisely to create these two democratic legitimacies, to strengthen democracy.

On the one hand, the democracy resulting from the European Parliament's own instruments, from its control instruments, from its legislative capacity, and, on the other hand, democratic cooperation from national parliaments through different instruments.
For this reason, I am here, let us say, totally in tune with the proposals made.
I do not agree with Mr Duff when he thinks that we should leave for later the introduction of mechanisms for relations with national parliaments.
I think that, under the Treaty of Lisbon, we can already do something, and this is very important.
I would also like to say that I support the proposal by my colleagues Mr Szájer and Mr Brok on representation in the JPA and, finally, I am
I am entirely in agreement with the observations made here by Mr Brok and other Members on Parliament and Members ’ primacy over Parliament ’ s services.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur on the work done on this report and on her speech a short while ago.
This new microfinance instrument will make it possible to grant microcredits to small businesses and to people who have lost their jobs and want to create their own business and their own job.
This is very important at a time when the economic crisis is expected to result in the loss of three and a half million jobs in the European Union alone.
As a result of the economic downturn, banks have stopped granting loans for the creation of jobs and businesses, and access to credit is more difficult at a time when it should be more accessible.
This new microfinance instrument will counteract the current trend of restricting access to credit, facilitating the acquisition of funds necessary for launching new businesses and creating new jobs.
The Commission proposal aims to re-allocate EUR 100 million from the budget of the Progress programme to this financing instrument.
We cannot agree with this proposal.
The financial and economic crisis is also a social crisis.
Therefore, reallocating the PROGRESS resources, which target the most vulnerable groups, is not the most appropriate solution.
For this reason, the creation of a separate budgetary heading to finance this instrument is supported, as well as an increase in the budget to EUR 150 million.

We also agree with the need to clarify in the legislation itself that the target group is made up of vulnerable groups who have difficulty entering or re-entering the labour market and who face the threat of social exclusion.
Specific groups should therefore be removed.
Finally, let us stress that it is essential that people have financial support and also adequate training.

Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, Prime Minister Reinfeldt, both on behalf of the EPP and on behalf of the Portuguese delegation of the EPP.
Our assessment of the Swedish Presidency is one of near-total success, essentially in four fundamental areas.

The first is in the institutional field: the contribution of the Swedish Presidency to the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon and to the election, which was very successful, of the President of the Commission.
particularly on all the issues relating to ratification, was extremely professional and was on a par with what is expected of best practice in the European Union.
Naturally, for a country like Portugal, which played a decisive role in the adoption of the Treaty of Lisbon, this contribution by the Swedish Presidency is invaluable.
My second point concerns the climate agenda, where the Commission ’ s work has naturally been extremely important.
We must say that, from my point of view and from the point of view of many Members of the PPE, it is in the area of climate change that the European Union has had most success.
is at the forefront of the global fight, and this is thanks to the efforts of the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, the Commission Presidency.
We consider this report to be very positive.
Thirdly, financial regulation, which, especially with this last Council, took what we consider to be a decisive step and which could have a very significant impact on getting us out of the crisis.
I would therefore also like to congratulate you on having reached an agreement in this area.
Finally, I would like to say a few words about an area that means a lot to me personally, namely the Stockholm Programme and, therefore, the area of security, justice and freedom.

I believe that, having followed both the Tampere process and, in particular, the Daejeon process, this Stockholm programme is absolutely essential, and I would like to congratulate the Swedish Presidency and Prime Minister Reinfeldt on this.

Mr President, as we know, fifteen directives on the recognition of professional qualifications were consolidated into a single legislative act, which has given rise to various problems in practice in the application thereof in the different Member States.

The difficulties are known, and the Commission should inventory the critical points and carry out an analysis of the problematic areas arising in the application of this important internal market instrument.
The protectionism that is evident at various levels is an obstacle not only to the free movement of people, but also to the internal market.
We must try to identify the ambiguous points so that the effectiveness of the directive is not undermined by mistrust.
The Union cannot be asked to do what Member States should do, but there is a facilitating effort to be made to help break down professional corporatism.

Improvement of the application of this directive should involve the dissemination of information on the recognition of qualifications on the SOLVIT network and the EURES network, and should include incentives for
common platforms, the implementation of effective single points of contact and, no less importantly, the articulation of this directive with the Services Directive.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, it is urgent that we promote a debate on the EU budget and on how we can, in the future, contribute to its sustainability and transparency.

The post-2013 financial framework must be built on the basis of solidarity and territorial cohesion, values that are crucial for regions such as Madeira, which face permanent difficulties and therefore require permanent support.
I would like to share with you some ideas that I believe should always be present when we think about and build cohesion policy.

The Commission has mentioned on several occasions certain aspects that seem essential to me, such as the need for greater flexibility, simplicity, efficiency and a greater focus on results in defining and implementing this policy.
However, there are issues that concern me, particularly when we take into account the needs of the outermost regions.
Allow me to make three brief points on this matter. First, the current approach in terms of regions should prevail over alternative approaches, such as value-added sectors.

Such a reallocation could jeopardise Objective 2, which currently benefits around two thirds of the European regions, and could potentially be counterproductive in regions with their own economic profile and competitive advantages. Secondly, it seems to me that maintaining the proximity criterion in the cohesion policy is vital.

The allocation and management of the Structural Funds should continue to be given priority on a regional basis, rather than a national one, or even a centralised one at European level. Third and lastly, but not least, I would like to recall and underline Article 349
349 of the Treaty of Lisbon, provides for special treatment of the outermost regions with regard to access to the Structural Funds, precisely because of their economic and social situation.
aggravated by constraints and unique features of a permanent nature, which result in such constant difficulties and therefore require the permanent aid requested.
Mr President, regarding the urgency, what we saw in Copenhagen was several steps backwards.

It is true that Copenhagen mobilised more Heads of State than others, but it also created more divisions, and as a result, we now have a situation of each for himself, where each State can define its own targets and, on top of that, on a voluntary basis.
mentioning that we have a target of two degrees Celsius or targets for financing, without any clarification, is also not of much help.
Therefore, I think that we have to act in terms of the future, and to act in terms of the future is to stop talking and start acting.
The European Union has always wanted to be a leader. I have always said so.
However, when we got to Copenhagen, it ended up hesitating, not defining or defending the targets that it said were its targets and that it would not abandon.
So I ask: in future, will we not be more just if we continue to allow the poorest to continue paying the heaviest price for this failure and for the decisions of others?
Mr President, I would like to denounce what is currently happening in Portugal, where it is clear that the media
has brought to light a government plan to control newspapers, television stations and radio stations, which calls freedom of expression into question.
Just this week, a well-known journalist, Mário Crespo, had one of his columns censored, again on the suggestion or apparent suggestion of the Prime Minister.

In the light of this, Prime Minister José Sócrates must give substantial explanations to the country. He must explain that he is not going to dominate, to muzzle, to censor freedom of expression in Portugal.

The way we are going, Portugal is no longer a State of Law, but a formal State of Law, where the Prime Minister limits himself to formalities, procedures and formalisms, and does not want to give substantial explanations.
For Portugal, we want a material rule of law.

Mr President, the Green Paper on the reform of the common fisheries policy, whose rapporteur is my colleague from the Azores, Marisa Matias, is a unique opportunity for a debate on a sector that is of great importance for the balance of local development in outermost regions.

The S&D Group is following the ongoing discussion with particular interest and considers it imperative that a distinction be made between artisanal and industrial fleets, which should benefit from different fishing regimes.
It is essential to defend a principle of positive discrimination in limiting access to territorial waters within the exclusive economic zone to local artisanal fleets only.
In Madeira, support for the modernisation of vessels is a priority, as is management that guarantees the long-term conservation of a wide range of species.
Only in this way will we be able to avoid unsustainable situations for economic, environmental and social balance.
Finally, we will call for permanent, not temporary, support for fisheries in outermost regions.
This report on the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) aims to extend the eligibility of housing interventions to the benefit of marginalised communities in our Member States.
Until now, this support was only available to marginalised communities residing in urban areas of the new Member States.
In other words, since the vast majority of these communities live in rural areas and in shelters, they could not benefit from support to replace low-quality housing.
It is essentially about closing a gap and giving Member States and their regions greater opportunities in the policy of mitigating the specific difficulties of these communities.
I also welcome equal treatment for all Member States, as marginalised communities in need of support and integration deserve our concern, regardless of the Member State to which they belong.

By concluding this important report on the fight against poverty and for human dignity at first reading, we also ensure its swift application within the Economic Recovery Plan, responding in a timely manner to the crisis affecting us.

Mr President, Commissioner, let me begin by highlighting the role of the rapporteur, Mr van Nistelrooij, for his work in seeking consensus on this matter and also for the willingness he has always shown to incorporate other contributions into his report.
Tomorrow, we will vote on amendments to the FEADER Regulation, which are particularly important for the so-called old Member States and for Portugal too.

These amendments will allow the use of the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) in the housing sector to be extended to the benefit of marginalised communities, as currently this fund can only be allocated to urban development operations.

The amendments that I proposed, together with other colleagues, and which were adopted by a large majority in the Committee on Regional Development, will mean that the former Member States, and not just the new ones, as the original proposal provided, can also benefit from this funding opportunity.
This was done in an attempt to avoid setting a precedent that I consider dangerous and that would exclude all the former Member States of the East and probably other additional uses of Community aid.
That would not make any sense, as housing problems, particularly in marginalised communities, exist in both the new and the old Member States.

I would like to reiterate that the length of time that a country has been a member of the European Union should not be a criterion for allocating Structural Funds, and that this criterion should be abandoned completely.

Cohesion policy negotiations after 2013 must be based on solidarity, with a view to territorial cohesion, and must be oriented towards rewarding, rather than punishing, regions that have been exemplary in using Community funds.
Mr President, I would like to take this opportunity to underline to the Commission and to its President the way in which you have worked with Parliament over these months of investiture.
There is no body in the world with executive and legislative initiative powers that is required to present a programme before Parliament by its President, to be heard by all the groups

Members, a vote by an expressive absolute majority to bring all the Commissioners here to be heard one by one, with their own programme, with three to four hours of direct questions and questions with answers.
someone who agreed to negotiate a framework agreement with Parliament, in which he agreed, first of all, to justify and expand Parliament ’ s powers in the legislative initiative.

that accepted the principle of total access to information, whether in legislative and political action or in international negotiations; that accepted taking into account Parliament ’ s opinion on the Commissioners and on the reshuffle of the Commissioners.
This means, once and for all, and from my point of view, that the Commission has shown, from July until now, that it is willing to have a close relationship with Parliament and that it is

demonstrated clearly, vividly and unequivocally that the strategic alliance of the Treaty of Lisbon, the 'community method' that Mr Saryusz-Wolski spoke of, is the alliance between Parliament and the Commission.
The Commission and the framework agreement therefore deserve the full support of the EPP.
Madam President, five years after the Orange Revolution, Ukrainians seem to look back on this period as a lost opportunity.
And it is not only the fault of their political elites.
It is true that they have been too consumed by power and influence struggles.
Many reforms remain to be implemented and many have not gone beyond paper.
It is inconceivable that, in any democracy, electoral laws should be changed between the two rounds of an election.
It does not mean those who proposed them, those who voted for them or those who enacted them.
It reflects the not very praiseworthy and recommendable intentions in its agenda.
There is much to be changed regarding the lack of independence of the judiciary, which is subject to excessive influence not only from the political power but also from economic agents.
Without a free and independent judiciary, there is no rule of law, human rights are not guaranteed, there is no foreign investment and there is no progress.
However, the blame for this disenchantment also lies heavily with this Parliament and, essentially, with many Member States.
I would like us to put ourselves in the shoes of a citizen of a European country that, due to the vicissitudes of history, is still not a member of the European Union.
What would we expect from the Union?
Solidarity.

However, many chancelleries, with the sole concern of not upsetting or offending Moscow, have used and abused the constant internal struggles in Kiev to avoid clearly stating that Ukraine is an independent and sovereign country.
As such, if that is the freely expressed will of the majority of its people and if it meets the established criteria, Ukraine could aspire to become a member of the European Union in the future.

I would like to point out that in island regions, air transport is sometimes the only way of access and that its operating conditions are determined by the volume of its passenger and freight traffic.

While there are insular regions where the abundance of these flights is profitable for many airlines, attracting competition and low transport prices, thus improving accessibility, there are others, such as the Azores, where these flights are still limited and unattractive to operators.

As a result, air fares are higher, which constitutes an obstacle to population mobility and a limiting factor in attracting tourists, preventing this enormous potential for development and diversification of their economic base from flourishing and being built.
This framework is particularly punitive in times of crisis such as the one we are going through.

It is therefore essential to provide temporary support to boost flows so that the market can start to function, and also to include these regions with high tourism potential in the objectives of the trans-European networks in bringing Europe's outermost regions closer together.
