Mr President, I rise today in this House with a voice choked with the dismay of someone who lived through and personally witnessed the tragedy that struck Madeira last Saturday.

My words are obviously to associate myself with the feeling of pain and mourning that has affected the families of the victims, which now stands at 42, to whom I pay my tribute and respect for their losses.

The true scale of this disaster is still to be revealed, as the search and rescue teams, who have been working non-stop since Saturday in a Herculean effort that I salute, are only now beginning to reach the most isolated communities.
We fear that the number of victims may increase.

The situation is one of widespread destruction, with considerable material damage to access routes, roads and bridges completely destroyed, and to essential services such as water and electricity supplies.
Some 600 people have been left homeless, having lost their homes and personal belongings.
The economic and social impact of this disaster is preventing people from returning to a normal life.
But we now need to look forward.
We also need to send a message of solidarity, hope and confidence to people.
We need to help, and it is in this area that the European Union and Parliament in particular have a role to play. They are vital in the process of mobilising the Solidarity Fund.
When we are called upon to intervene, we must do so quickly, because we cannot ask these people to wait, especially when they need us most.
I call on the Commission and its President, Mr Barroso, and especially on the Commissioner for Regional Development, Mr Hahn, and I welcome his initiative to visit Madeira in the near future.
I ask him to also carry this message of hope, of help and of support for reconstruction, because now it is time to rebuild what nature has unfortunately taken away from us once again.
But I am sure that we will, because as the Madeira anthem taught me, the Madeiran people are humble, stoic and brave.
It is the people who, with pick and shovel, plough the land, the hero of work in the mountainous interior.
Madeira will honour its history and, through hard work, will fight for and achieve its prosperity and glory.
We are beginning this plenary session today with a minute 's silence.

It was a minute of silence with two meanings: solidarity with the victims of this disaster that struck Madeira and their families, and a tribute to all the men and women who have tirelessly helped these victims.
However, a minute of silence should make us reflect, and should also lead to a reflection that goes beyond the one we are having here.
What we saw in Madeira is a phenomenon that will repeat itself more and more.
Climate change means that extreme events are repeated and we need to have responses to them.
We cannot avoid natural disasters, but we should not be condemned to always having such dramatic effects on people's lives.
That is why we must fight for planning and coordination policies that are oriented towards the public good.

But, Mr President, I would like to end with what is most important and what brought us here, which is that the European Commission, the European Parliament and the European institutions must be able to respond urgently to an emergency situation, and for that we need the necessary financial resources and the necessary speed.
A tribute of solidarity to the families and friends of the victims of the tragic events of Saturday in Madeira.
As a Madeiran, a Portuguese and a European, I cannot fail to applaud Parliament and the Union for their active solidarity with the Autonomous Region of Madeira and its people.
It is in the regions that the presence of the Union needs to be felt, and it is precisely at times like these, when its solidarity is most needed, that it is also most important that it be felt.

Mr President, I too would like to express my solidarity with the people of Madeira and to insistently ask the European institutions, and in particular the Commission, to make available all the means at their disposal to provide this assistance.
I would also like to stress that when a disaster of this kind occurs, it is much more difficult to tackle its consequences when it happens in poor countries and poor regions.

However, in the case of Madeira, we also have to take into account the fact that the island's economic base is directly affected by the fact that, as it depends on tourism, it has a double consequence when its natural beauty, its access and its quality of life are all destroyed.
Therefore, there is in fact a different situation when we have problems of this severity in poor countries and in poor regions, and particularly in mountainous and tourist regions, such as Madeira.
It is therefore absolutely essential that all the adaptations that were foreseen and requested and that were mentioned here with regard to the Solidarity Fund should not wait any longer.

they should be implemented immediately, because, due to climate change, these processes will unfortunately be repeated, particularly in Europe's poorest regions, which are those that experience torrential downpours in terms of water and extreme drought in summer. Thank you once again, Madam President.
Mr President, Commissioner, President-in-Office of the Council, we are here talking about reinventing the energy system at European level through investment in low-carbon technologies.
I think that these are very important challenges, as mentioned in the communication.
This is one of the challenges that we have to face head on, but there is no clarification here, and that is why it is important to raise these questions and to request clarification.
It seems that there is not only a lack of clarification of the funding, but also a lack of political clarification.
Energy efficiency, carbon capture, nuclear power, biofuels, solar energy and energy efficiency are all mentioned as being cheaper ways of reducing emissions.
I am surprised that there are other, much cheaper ways of reducing carbon emissions that are not even mentioned in this communication, such as, for example, reducing consumption.
energy, micro-generation, distribution and different forms of distribution and different projects that do not have to go through large structures, and access to energy.
I think that this would certainly help to clarify the financial aspects and make the plan cheaper.

Therefore, within this political clarification and within this financial clarification, we still need to talk about what seems to me to be an absolutely crucial point, which is the constant emphasis on public-private partnerships as if they were the solution to everything.
When asked where the money comes from, the Commission tells us that it comes from where it is.
I confess that I am not any clearer when I am told that the money will come from where it is, without being told exactly where it is.

Therefore, this commitment to public-private partnerships, which is systematically made when we talk about low-carbon technologies, leaves us with one certainty: we do not know where the money will come from, but, knowing that they are public-private partnerships, we know from the outset who will pay.
Who will pay? The taxpayers at the beginning, the consumers, who by chance are also taxpayers, at the end.
We know that those who pay less are, in the first place, the institutions and private organisations that make the investments and are paid for them, but in the end end up multiplying the profits.
Without this clarification, it seems to me that, once again, we are systematically passing on to future generations the bill for changing the energy model.

Madam President, the European Union recently celebrated, within the framework of the World Trade Organisation, an agreement with the countries of Latin America, under which it committed itself to substantially and progressively reducing tariffs on banana imports from those countries.

I draw your attention to the fact that such an agreement has had directly negative effects on European banana producers, particularly on banana producers in Madeira, in Portugal, but also, even more intensely, on those in the Canary Islands, in Spain.

I would like to recall that the situation of producers in these regions is already seriously hampered by the physical and geographical characteristics of the islands and by the size of their farms, which means that they have to face permanent difficulties that penalise their development.

It is therefore urgent to take measures to defend banana producers in Madeira and the Canary Islands, bearing in mind the serious and immediate impact that the new Geneva Agreement will have on the European import market and, consequently, on the production and marketing of bananas from these outermost regions.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, as rapporteur for the opinion of the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs, I would like to begin by thanking the Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety, the Committee on Industry, Research and Energy and the Committee on International Trade for their cooperation.
I would like to congratulate Mr Kľouček, even in his forced absence, and to highlight the way in which he managed to draft this important report, thus facilitating the main objective.
I would also like to congratulate Mrs Krehl on the presentation she has made to us.

The main objective is, without further delay, to simplify procedures and speed up the implementation of programmes funded by the Cohesion Fund, the Structural Funds and the European Regional Development Fund.
In the context of the current financial, economic and social crisis, pressure on national financial resources has been increasing.
This pressure will be reduced by better use of Community funding, and by getting these resources to the most affected beneficiaries more quickly.
More than 20 million Europeans are unemployed, that is, more than four million more than a year ago, and, regrettably, the trend is to increase.

This situation requires that the cohesion programmes be properly implemented, as they represent the most powerful and relevant lever for supporting the real economy, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises and employment.
SMEs are the driving force behind the European economy and the actors of sustainable growth and job creation in quantity and quality.

The continuation of simplification and clarification of the rules governing cohesion policy will undoubtedly have a positive impact on the pace of implementation of the programmes, particularly because it will be
national, regional and local authorities the possibility of using clearer and less bureaucratic rules, which will allow greater flexibility in adapting programmes to new challenges.
Mr President, of course this crisis was unforeseen and of course the precautionary principle had to take priority.
However, lessons need to be drawn, and the first is that, for five days, we did not hear a sufficiently strong and politically responsible European voice.
This voice was missing in terms of defending passengers' interests, clarifying their rights, seeking alternative transport and coordinating solutions.

My second conclusion is that the way out of the crisis must not appear to the ordinary citizen to be a power struggle between those who want to avoid losses and those who want to defend the precautionary principle.
There must be clear transparency and clear objectivity regarding the conditions under which we leave a situation of total closure of European airspace.
Therefore, improving scientific tests and coordination at EU level is also necessary.

Mr President, I would also like to begin by congratulating my colleague, Mrs Krikorová, on her work and persistence on this important dossier and on her work to improve the energy performance of buildings and to create a more sustainable Europe.
As we know, energy consumption in the European Union is far above what is acceptable, and that is why it is so important to move forward with proposals of this kind.
It is more than time that we had an ambitious European policy and that we added complementary measures to this ambitious policy, particularly in the crisis situation that we are experiencing.
The construction sector is considered to be one of the sectors, one of the markets with the greatest potential in terms of the energy economy, but that is not all.
That is not all, and that is why it is the ideal sector for the implementation of public policies, and it is in this sense that I would like to conclude, Mr President.

We therefore have to take advantage of this opportunity, which is being offered to us, to invest in improving the energy efficiency of buildings and its potential to create millions of jobs in the coming years.
And it is not just about new buildings, but also about the rehabilitation and recovery of existing, degraded or inappropriate buildings.

Let us therefore take the opportunity to use this legislation as a fundamental contribution to the revival of the European economy, and we cannot continue to waste investment that could and should be strategic for recovery and counteracting the crisis. We hope that the Commission will invest and that the Member States will know how to take advantage of this investment.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating Mr Manders on the work he has done on this report, as well as thanking the colleagues who collaborated with him on this file.

In fact, the very fact that this proposal simplifies and improves the current legislative framework by bringing together into a single regulation all the existing legislation – three basic directives on the labelling and packaging of textiles – is in itself an important fact.

However, beyond this, this proposal will have a positive impact on private operators and public authorities and will also allow new fibres to be included, thus encouraging innovation in the textile and clothing sector.
This revision will also increase the transparency of the process for adding new fibres to the list of harmonised fibre names.
There seems to be no doubt that the agents involved will benefit from this regulation.
Let us see: the textile industry will benefit from savings in administrative costs and the possibility of marketing new fibres earlier.
On their side, the authorities of the Member States will not need to transpose the changes into national legislation, which will allow for a significant cost saving for those Member States.
Consumers will have the guarantee that the fibres so named meet specific characteristics, and will also benefit from the fact that the new fibres will reach the market earlier.
The introduction of these new fibres is important for European industry as a way of promoting innovation, while also increasing the level of information provided to consumers.
In today's globalised world, with emerging economies and markets that are increasingly large, where millions of products produced by millions of companies circulate,
Investing in innovation and the speed with which that innovation reaches consumers is absolutely crucial for businesses, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises.
that the application of this regulation will increase their competitiveness.
SMEs will certainly benefit from this new market share regulation and will thus contribute to increasing employment in Europe.
Madam President, Minister, Commissioner, the shortage of organs for transplantation is a growing problem.

This has already been mentioned here several times, and it is a major problem that raises issues for public health and issues associated with the strengthening of the black market and organ trafficking, which does nothing to improve our health systems or our citizens.

We therefore have to combat this crime, and I think that doing so through the adoption of proposals such as the ones we are discussing here is more than important and more than relevant. I would therefore like to focus on the most important aspects and issues raised by these two proposals.
The system we have so far, without these two proposals, is a system of enormous inequalities.
inequalities between Member States, inequalities between existing systems, inequalities in access to organs, inequalities between citizens, which it seems to me that we cannot tolerate and
It is therefore important to ensure that these inequalities are reduced, and these proposals aim to do precisely that.

I think that a European system that maintains, after all, some of the specific features of certain countries, so that they are not forced to have more restrictive systems, is the answer to this problem.
The work carried out by the rapporteurs, Mr Mikolášik and Mr Pedro Rodrigues, is of the utmost importance and makes a contribution to this problem that we are facing.
We are talking about proposals based on and supported by issues such as charity, volunteering, gratuitousness, confidentiality and data protection.

Traceability and responsibility are important here, and I think that Parliament is going as far as the recovery of the patient, and not just as far as donation, and cooperation between Member States. I think we are on the right track.
I would just like to mention, however, that with regard to the notion of 'living beings', I am pleased that inclusion has been extended and that this extension has been made with rights.

Of course, all rights and conditions must be guaranteed, but it is very important that we go beyond the restricted circle of the conventional family, because otherwise it would be just another way of curtailing other forms of equality.

Therefore, promoting data exchange as a main source of transparency and protecting data and patient safety seem to me to be more than enough reasons for us all to come together tomorrow and vote in favour of these two proposals, which are so important for the future.
Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, the ability to replace damaged human organs with those of another person, without rejection, is one of the great successes of contemporary medicine.

The European benchmark reveals differences, it reveals that medium-sized countries such as Portugal do very well in such comparisons and that larger countries such as Spain are now recognised as leaders in organisation and best practice.
Some principles and practices to safeguard.

Firstly, the reinforcement of scientific knowledge and information, which leads more and more donors to donate while alive and to generalise post-mortem donation; secondly, the prohibition of the commercialisation of

of organ donation, not only in the Member States but also outside them, preventing transplant tourism and combating the trafficking of organs; thirdly, the proactive promotion of donation in hospitals as a way of overcoming shortages.

Finally, Madam President, we must guarantee equal access to transplantation for all those who need it and qualify on the basis of harmonised clinical criteria, in accordance with the principle of universal access to healthcare, the paradigm of the social Europe of which we are proud.
I congratulate the rapporteurs on their work.
I would like to stress that it is with great satisfaction that the European People ' s Party and the Portuguese Members of the European People ' s Party
European Parliament, this accession process to the European Convention on Human Rights is beginning.

I would, however, like to draw attention to the fact that we consider it very important that the European Parliament accompany the accession negotiations of the European Union to the European Convention on Human Rights every step of the way.

We believe that this aspect, from a technical, legal and political point of view, is fundamental, because Parliament is also a house of human rights, and a house where human rights are very important. I would just like to point out that we consider it very important

For us Members of the European Parliament, who are in contact with many countries outside the European Union, it is very important, it is a very good signal that we are sending to the members of the Council of Europe, the European Union accession countries and the Council of Europe countries that are not members of the European Union.
For these members of the Council of Europe, it is a sign of the European Union's commitment to their situation and to their levels of democracy and respect for fundamental rights.
Madam President, Commissioner, social housing is an economic service of general interest with an important role in the market, creating positive externalities to the benefit of society as a whole.
According to Protocol 26 annexed to the Treaty of Lisbon, it is the responsibility of national, regional and local authorities to provide and organise this type of service.
It is therefore difficult to understand this restriction of the target group to families with incomes of less than EUR 33 000.
This is a measure of poverty based on absolute and purely financial terms, which does not take into account the diversity of the 27 Member States, reducing the issue to a simple argument of solvency.

Social housing responds to cultural issues, social issues, proximity to the place of work, the important redefinition of the role of women in the market and the mobility of workers in the European space.
The struggle that the European Union is waging to emerge from the current crisis involves economic growth, which brings with it an increase in immigration and in ethnic minorities.
economic and cultural, and the need for their integration, even with the risks of ghettoisation associated with this, which we must combat with a fair balance.
Is the Commission not entering the field of competence of the Member States?

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, allow me to point out that we are debating the first report from the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development under the new ordinary legislative procedure provided for by the Treaty of Lisbon.

This is a historic moment, and by confirming this House's vote, I propose that we will begin this new period of sharing competences in agricultural matters in the European Union in the best possible way.
I agree with the Irish on their ultra-peripheral regions, in a practical demonstration of the will to build a Union that strengthens itself through cohesion,
in the spirit of active solidarity, respecting the specificities and differences of its parts, as the only viable way of building our common European project.

I am also introducing fundamental regulation to support the sugar industry in the Azores, which has been around for over a century and which I am particularly pleased to represent, thus contributing to a
healthy agricultural diversification in a region where the dairy sector, as we know, has a unique national scale and is irreplaceable, and which we must make sustainable and consolidate.
Madam President, I would like to thank Commissioner Siilasmaa and the other Commission officials with whom we have worked and negotiated for their openness and flexibility.
The Spanish Presidency of the Council also spared no effort to turn our shared desire to reach an agreement at first reading into reality.

This thanks obviously extends to the shadow rapporteurs from the other political groups, as well as to the excellent staff of the Secretariat of Parliament's Committee on Agriculture, whose collaboration enabled us to reach a result that I consider to be very positive.

I would also like to highlight the decisive contribution made by the Agricultural Federation of the Azores and by the regional and national governments in the way in which they became involved and committed to obtaining a solution for Azorean sugar.

The presentation of the Council's draft regulation on 2 October in the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development took place at a time when there were no prospects for the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, which was still awaiting ratification by all Member States.
In this context, the initial idea of the Commission and of the Council was that the proposal be adopted under the simplified procedure, without any amendments, to take effect from 1 January 2010.

With the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and despite the fact that at the time the prevailing opinion among other political groups, as well as the Council and the Commission, was that the proposal should continue in simplified procedure, because they thought that

proposal did not require amendments and should enter into force quickly. Despite this opinion prevailing, I and the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament believe that Parliament should have already expressed itself within the framework of the Treaty of Lisbon, assuming its co-legislator role.

We did so at a good time, because since then new possibilities have opened up, first with the contribution from the Committee on Regional Development and the opinion from Mr Nunez Queiró, whom I would like to take this opportunity to thank,

to thank them for their constant cooperation throughout this process, and then with my report introducing new possibilities for sugar in the Azores, as well as with the support and input from my colleagues on the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development.

Lastly, in this first speech, I would like to say that, as regards the amendments proposed by the Committee on Regional Development and voted in the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development, and supported by me, we were unable to have them accepted in the compromise reached in the trialogue.

It was essential to obtain a compromise at first reading, so as to safeguard the improvements already achieved and the need for entry into force, without committing to its retroactivity from January this year.
However, as the Commission is already preparing a review document for the Common Organisation of the Market in Wine, we will certainly have the opportunity to debate this again soon.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, we are facing an acceleration of global change, which has devastating consequences for our economic, political and social systems and, consequently, for all our citizens.
Today, we are witnessing an unprecedented weakening of the States ’ response capacity.
The European Union therefore has to identify common causes and allies and act clearly and united on the world stage.
Extraordinary situations require joint action to be lucid.

If we do not take the strict and collectively responsible measures that are required, Europe will be condemned to marginalisation and impoverishment, and only a strong Europe that respects its common rules will be able to respond appropriately to the new times.
Fragile and indebted States do not have the capacity to protect their citizens.

We therefore need to be able to win back the confidence of citizens, win the battles of stability, budgetary rigour, job creation, stability of the monetary union, globalisation and strategic choices.
Otherwise, our future will be compromised.
It will gain its future by investing in social cohesion, ensuring peace and building a new model based on the values of freedom, social justice and responsibility.
The Europe 2020 strategy and the integrated guidelines for Europe 2020 are therefore essential instruments for a new cycle of growth and employment in Europe.
In order to implement and achieve this properly, we need to define clear, quantifiable objectives in employment, education and poverty reduction.

It is also crucial for the success of the strategy that everything possible be done to facilitate transposition by the Member States of their national objectives and to ensure the proper implementation of this strategy.
When the Economic and Monetary Union completed ten years, the Commission considered the euro the most successful project of the Union.
Today, we would have difficulty affirming it without limitations, because the crisis has clearly shown that Economic and Monetary Union is an unfinished project.
The minimum rescue measures have been taken in a climate of emergency, but let us not deceive ourselves: the pressure conditions, the direct interests of the main
Member States, the painful nature of the decisions and their slowness have weakened the image of the euro and of the European Union in the eyes of citizens and of the world.
We have to face the facts.
The European Union and the euro are a process of integration, not mere inter-country cooperation.
It is an unfinished process that, at this time, either strengthens itself or runs a very real risk of disappearing.
There are three issues that need to be addressed. First, it is important to consolidate the instruments that were launched in this climate of emergency. We need to create a monetary fund.

European, we need to move forward with Eurobonds, we need to move forward with a European supervisory and regulatory architecture, and we need to move forward with a European dimension to the management of banking crises.
Secondly, it is important to understand that there is no strong currency if the economy is weak. The 1% growth forecast for the European Union is unsustainable in terms of
both in terms of its demographic composition and in terms of combating unemployment. The Europe 2020 strategy is nothing more than a set of good ideas if we do not have the means to implement them.

Thirdly, Commissioner Olli Rehn, fiscal consolidation is important, but it cannot coexist with increasing disparities between the Member States that make up the Union. The convergence policy urgently needs to be reviewed.
Mr President, several of my fellow Members of the Group of the European People ' s Party (Christian Democrats) have already given a very comprehensive picture of the situation.
I think that we will now have to refer to the role of the Member States.
In fact, national governments also have a role to play here, in that they must impose the requirements
and the conditions for the single currency to be strengthened and for this project of monetary and economic integration to be taken forward.
I would like to say that our party, the party linked to the EPP, which is the PSD in Portugal, is supporting the government in its austerity measures, because we believe that

that the errors that the Socialist Government has committed in Portugal over these 15 years, and which today, unfortunately, are obvious to everyone, need to be corrected and will have the support of the PSD in order to be corrected.

But if we are supporting the austerity measures, we are also criticising the Portuguese Government, which is a good example for other similar cases, because it is not cutting spending. And what is fundamental is not just

increase taxes to reduce the deficit, but to reduce expenditure, to demand that the government have the courage to take measures that reduce expenditure, that reduce the deficit and that reduce public debt.
This applies to Portugal and to other Member States that are in a difficult situation today. As Members of the European Parliament, we have a responsibility to do the same in our countries.

apology for the measures necessary to ensure that the euro emerges strengthened and that this process of integration and this dream, which is a dream of the EPP and a dream of all of us, can be fulfilled.
We therefore criticise those governments that do not have the courage to take the necessary measures in their countries.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by saying that the report on the evaluation of the results of the 2006-2010 gender equality strategy, which aims to influence future strategies on this matter, could, in our opinion, have been more accurate and less controversial.

In fact, the way in which several ethically sensitive issues were approached, particularly the issue of women ’ s sexual and reproductive health, should have been different if the objective had really been to obtain the greatest possible consensus.

That said, I would like to stress that, in times of so-called normal economic growth, concern for women's rights, and particularly for the disparity between the situation of men and women at work, in society and in the family, is great, but these problems are greatly aggravated by an economic and social crisis such as the one we are experiencing.

This is why we need a reinforced strategic commitment from Member States, local and regional authorities and Community institutions in terms of protecting the environment and public health, and in terms of promoting sustainable development and the transition to a low-carbon economy.

say that, in line with what President Daul said and what Mr Langen said, it is essential that there is an economic government of the Union, but that this economic government should be centred on the Commission.
This is the first point I wanted to stress. Having said that, I would like to say that we should not just, as has been done this morning, have our debate focused on immediate issues, but
also in those that are part of the 2020 strategy. I would particularly highlight the issue of education and training of the young, the issue of innovation, research and development.

because these are the niches that will allow Europe to be competitive in the global arena. Today, I must say that, regrettably, the European education model has failed, and it has failed when compared with the model of the emerging countries.

like India, China and Brazil, is today a less demanding and less rigorous model. Therefore, I also ask that, in the face of this idea of innovation, development, research and education, there be rigour and demands in the context of European education systems.
Only with this rigour and this demand will we prepare our young people to compete in the global market.

We cannot just think about this year's crisis or the crisis of the next two or three years; we have to think about the strategy for Europe over the next 10 to 20 years, and it depends on a rigorous and demanding education system.
Mr President, Commissioner, never before has the European project, built with so much effort over the last fifty years, been so threatened.

The frustration of European citizens is enormous: the financial system, which was supposed to serve them, has destroyed their savings, their pensions, their investment projects, their jobs, in short, their future.
As regards the necessary strengthening of the whole financial services market – banking, insurance and securities – the way forward is clear and lies in the architecture we are discussing here today.
We really need a strong European supervisory architecture, covering all products and actors, equipped with methodologies and instruments
effective interventions and that it is able to respond to the reality of the market, which, particularly in the European context, does not recognise national borders.

Risk cannot and should not be eliminated from the financial market, but we need regulation that makes this risk more transparent and prevents the accumulation of bubbles whose bursting affects the whole system and weighs down the real economy.

However, if, despite these defences, new crises occur in the future, it is crucial to have mechanisms in place to limit the spread, particularly with regard to the banking system, because this is vital for the functioning of the economy and for safeguarding citizens' savings.
It is precisely in the powers and competences of the Banking Authority that the ability to discuss a new strategy on the management of cross-border banking crises lies, the subject of my report.

We cannot prevent banks from failing, but we can ensure that their reorganisation or eventual liquidation is carried out in a way that protects the public interest and that does not place the burden on the public purse.
in an orderly manner, limiting the collateral effects on the rest of the system and, above all, avoiding the taxpayer having to pay the costs of the crises they generate.

To this end, a set of new instruments is needed: rules, definitions, methodologies, instruments and terminology common to all those involved in restructuring, reorganisation or liquidation operations.
We need to harmonise the relevant national legal frameworks for crisis management, particularly insolvency and liquidation laws.
Supervisors need to be given wider powers of intervention.
We need to draw up clear criteria, not only quantitative but also qualitative, which will allow us to begin interventions in institutions in difficulty.
Contingency plans need to be prepared.
All this process for 27 Member States takes time.
Meanwhile, about 50 banks out of the 12 000 operating in Europe represent 70% of the assets.
This means a huge concentration of risk, but also an opportunity to focus on them primarily.

We have created the conditions to propose a special and urgent process for the rescue of banks which, being cross-border, by their size and relationships with other banks, may have a systemic impact on the financial system.
For these banks, it is possible to anticipate special supervision by the new European Banking Authority, even though it operates within the College of Supervisors of the Member States.
It is possible and urgent that they start making contributions to a fund that finances any eventual interventions, depending on the risk they generate.
necessary, and it is important that the banking authority create a specialised unit to coordinate interventions in the event of difficulties in these banks.
Taken as a whole, the proposed elements ensure that interventions in transnational systemic banks are consistent and coherent, and that they are
different countries and, above all, that a principle so dear to Europe is respected, namely that the costs of the pollution caused by these banks should be borne by the polluter.
This proposal fits perfectly into what we are discussing, especially regarding the banking authority ’ s powers. I would even say that it is the most obvious test of its effectiveness.
In the vote in the Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs, there was a very large majority in favour of this proposal: 87% of the votes.
We also count here in plenary on the overwhelming support of this House for this proposal, which is visionary, realistic, but also pragmatic.
Let us take urgent and necessary action to rekindle Europeans ' hope in Europe, in its financial system and, in particular, in the banking system.
Briefly, to highlight just four points.
The first of these is that it has been an extraordinary experience to work in such a united way with my colleagues from the other political groups.
Thank you very much for including it in the supervisory package and, in particular, thank you to Mr García-Margallo y Marfil for all the cooperation that we were able to obtain.

Secondly, I would like to thank my personal assistants and also the colleagues who contributed to the specific report that I am responsible for, which enabled me to incorporate 90% of their proposals.
A special word for the secretariat, for Zuzana Brabcová and for the Commission services.

Finally, I would like to thank the Commissioner and his services, and express my very clear hope that, on the basis of the report that I hope will be adopted tomorrow, we will be able to build a solid, truly European foundation that protects citizens.
Mr President, Commissioner, I would also like to start by thanking the rapporteurs for this excellent work.

Deforestation, as we know, is one of the main environmental and social problems on the planet, and every year we lose 13 million hectares of forest, thus contributing to 20% of global carbon emissions and constituting a huge threat to biodiversity.
Deforestation's contribution to climate change is undeniable and, as such, it is largely responsible for extreme weather events, floods and even desertification of vast areas of the planet.
Deforestation puts all of humanity at risk, but this risk and this impact are not shared equally by all of us.
Communities with fewer resources and those that depend most on forests and nature to survive are the worst affected.
The discussions are about our collective existence, the scarcity of natural resources and their distribution, the rights of local and indigenous communities, and social justice.
The main cause of deforestation is illegal logging, which accounts for 20% to 40% of the world ’ s industrial wood production.
We are therefore also talking about a transfer of wealth from the timber-producing countries to the timber-consuming countries.
This illegal practice jeopardises the sustainable exploitation of timber and the companies that carry it out.
Nice words are not enough.
Parliament must adopt strong and consistent measures in this fight.
This report takes a very significant step in that direction by defining the ban on the trade in timber.
Good intentions are not enough.
Effective means are needed, particularly sanctions, which in this case could be of three types.

Firstly, fines that actually take away the economic benefit of the illegal practice, increased in the case of repeat offenders; secondly, confiscation of the products in question; and thirdly, immediate suspension of the authorisation to carry out commercial activity.

It is therefore the case that anyone who exploits or markets a natural resource must be socially responsible, and it is also the case that anyone who first places timber or timber products on the European market must be obliged to exercise due diligence to ensure that the timber has been legally harvested.
That is why all the following operators must provide basic information on this timber, namely its origin and destination.
The public should also have the right – and I am finishing now, Mr President – the right to information on who is complying and who is not.
Mr President, Europe needs to renew confidence and hope.
In a crisis that has not yet been overcome, we had the firefighters, as Mr Delors said.
We hope that the Belgian Presidency will bring the architects.
The first test, of course, will be in September: the Council's approach to Parliament's proposals on financial architecture.

Today, however, one of the greatest European challenges is to ensure that the European currency does not collapse, whether due to a lack of monitoring instruments, a lack of growth in the euro area or, above all, due to the increasing divergences and internal tensions caused by the economies that make it up.

Cutting the Structural Funds, as the Commission proposes, funds that were created to stimulate convergence, to help economies that diverge, is like putting out fires by starting a fire that has not yet been extinguished.
We clearly need architects.

One last question: I would like to know about the external dimension of the European Union ’ s action, in particular, I would like to know a little more about what the Belgian Presidency proposes for Cancún and for Doha.

Mr President, this own-initiative report that we are debating today, drawn up by Mr Bové, whom I congratulate for the way in which he conducted it and for the good final result, and which I had the privilege of shadowing and negotiating the seven compromises obtained, is absolutely essential at a time when the

Agricultural policies are increasingly market-oriented, farmers in Europe are facing very low income levels and millions of consumers, as a result of the crisis that affects us all, are in urgent need of keeping food at affordable prices.
That is why, if farmers' incomes are going to depend more and more on the value generated in markets and on the price of food, improving the functioning of the food supply chain
It is absolutely necessary to ensure a fairer sharing of the added value, from agricultural producers to final consumers, guaranteeing fair incomes for the former and fair prices for the latter.

To this end, it is necessary to rebalance the relationships established along the food supply chain, to give them transparency and to guarantee the existence of a framework of fair and competitive practices.
In this respect, we urge the Commission to take into account the proposals supported by a large majority in the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development.
A few days ago, in Ericeira, Portugal, all the workers in a shoe company were dismissed by a simple text message on their mobile phones, with no further explanation.
I am raising this case because it is, unfortunately, a glaring example of abuse of collective redundancies in Europe.
It goes against all European standards and violates any sense of common decency, respect and dignity.
Europe has had eight million new unemployed people since the crisis began, making a total of 25 million. How many will be dismissed in such a shameful way as this?
I would say more and more, unfortunately.
The European Union is a champion of a long tradition of safeguarding workers ’ rights.
However, the current legislation has not been effective.
What additional measures will the Commission take?
That is why this issue is so pertinent.
There is no justification for dismissals such as those at Eurocar.
It will be an innovation introduced by the EIB, but it is not a good innovation; it is certainly a terrible one.
The environmental disaster currently under discussion, although occurring outside the geographical area of the Union, concerns all of us, European citizens.

The notion of sustainable development, which we believe in, is a concept that has no administrative borders or religious creeds, and which looks at the planet as a whole and not as the sum of the parts that make it up.
The Jordan River is therefore a problem for all of humanity, and not just for the populations or communities directly affected by the scarcity or poor quality of their water.
'Act locally, think globally', common sense tells us.
That is what we are doing here today: thinking globally.
As the European Union, we must help to act locally as a way of minimising and reversing this permanent degradation of the flow and quality of the waters of the Jordan River.
To act or help to act, the Union already has a legislative, institutional and instrumental framework at its disposal.
I refer to the Union for the Mediterranean, its secretariat and the European Investment Bank - managed FEMIP.
This is obviously an issue that the delegation of this Parliament, which I chair, will follow closely in its relations with the countries of the Maghreb.

It is obvious that the action leading to the resolution of this environmental tragedy should, first and foremost, be taken by the Member States and local authorities whose populations are directly affected by it.
In this context, I would like to highlight two ideas in the resolution.
Firstly, the creation of a commission for the management of the entire Jordan River basin, made up of representatives of the states or authorities that use those waters.
Europe can help here by sharing, for example, the experience of the International Commission for the Protection of the River Rhine.
Secondly, support for the dissemination of best practices in relation to joint community projects involving Jordanian, Israeli and Palestinian citizens.
particularly those promoted by the Friends of the Middle East, which Mr De Rossa has just mentioned, on the efficient and correct management of the water resources of the Jordan Valley basin.
Finally, Mr President, in an even broader context, I would like to highlight the example of cooperation and peaceful coexistence that this initiative represents.

At a time when, although regretting the absence of the European Union from the process, we welcome and encourage the resumption of a new process of direct dialogue between Israel and the Palestinian Authority, we want to welcome this direct involvement of the two parties.

Growth and employment need to be priorities alongside compliance with the Stability and Growth Pact.

Madam President, today we are discussing another case of human rights violation: the imprisonment of the Syrian activist Aytan Al-Malih, an 80-year-old lawyer accused of, they say, spreading false and exaggerated news that weakens national feelings in his country.

Beyond the violation of the rights enshrined in numerous treaties and international conventions, the imprisonment of this human rights defender is also contrary to the country's own legislation, which stipulates that military courts should not have jurisdiction to try civilians.
The decision taken in relation to this man and to other Syrian prisoners detained for similar reasons, the various detentions, the restrictions on freedom of movement and the arbitrary measures taken
Syrian refugees, are practices that contradict Syria ’ s important role in the whole region and do not reflect the efforts that have been made in this country to improve the social situation.
I repeat: today we are discussing yet another case of human rights violations.
It cannot be separated from the various discussions we have had here this week.
I would like to highlight two of them. The first is the rejection of the death sentence by stoning imposed on Sakineh Mohammadi Ashtiani.
Many voices joined together and there have already been some results.

It is not yet time to celebrate, but it is certainly not time to keep silent either. Secondly, today we are adopting an important resolution against the abuses committed by the Sarkozy Government against Romanian and Bulgarian citizens.
Human rights have no borders and this is not a fight that can be waged just over there.
Today we have given a good example of how we also work behind closed doors.
The unjustified imprisonment of Daitan Almaleh concerns us all here today.
He is a human rights defender.
Human rights defenders must be free people, and that is what we demand of the Syrian authorities.
We, human rights defenders, will not leave him alone.

Mr President, the fact that there is an increasing tendency for the safety profile of new medicines to only become fully known after they have been placed on the market makes it necessary to have a pharmacovigilance system that is more effective than the one we have had until now.
We are facing a typical risk management problem, but at macro and supranational level, and we must now bring it closer to citizens.
New medicines have played a fundamental role in public health and in the advances in healthcare over the past few decades.
Adverse reactions caused by medicines continue to be the fifth leading cause of death in hospital.
The proposals presented strengthen the contribution of medicines to modern public health through a Community pharmacovigilance system, improving coordination and transparency.
It aims to monitor, detect early, communicate and assess adverse reactions.
New medicines will have a pictogram indicating that they are still under review.

Users are encouraged to participate in monitoring, national efficacy studies are encouraged, new awareness of environmental consequences is opened up and the Community monitoring institutions are improved.
That is why I welcome, Mr President, the proposals
I congratulate the rapporteur and also the Commission for their spirit of cooperation, as well as the Spanish Presidency.
And so, with the proposals presented in this legislative package, I welcome the final document that will be voted on tomorrow. Thank you, Mr President.
Mr President, I would like to begin by thanking and congratulating the rapporteur, as well as all the Members involved in this process.
We know that adverse effects of medicines are the fifth leading cause of death in hospital, and that a strong and transparent pharmacovigilance system is fundamental.
We now have the opportunity to strengthen the safety of medicines already on the market, going beyond laboratory tests, which do not always anticipate problems.

If concrete examples were necessary to justify the importance of a new system, we need only look at what happened with the H1N1 flu vaccine: speed in getting the vaccine to market and neglect in investigating the side effects that have been detected and which are delaying a full investigation.
That is why we need to continue down this path.
This directive ensures that health professionals and patients are more actively involved in identifying difficulties.
In fact, cooperation between countries and the Union can be a positive sum game.
Madam President, Commissioner, a Europe without economic, social and territorial cohesion is not a Union.
Mountainous regions, islands and sparsely populated areas have their own, very difficult challenges, which must be overcome in the name of a Europe of the peoples.
However, we need to go beyond statements of intent, otherwise we will have the opposite effect, that is, we will reinforce inequalities.
These regions are vast reservoirs of important resources.
Biodiversity, forests, wetlands and green areas provide an essential service for everyone, yet they suffer the consequences of limitations on their economy.
We must repay them.
The populations of these regions must be supported by all in a spirit of solidarity.
I have two proposals.
Firstly, the potential contribution to science and development.
Many of these regions are authentic open-air laboratories, given their richness, but they do not have equal access to research resources.
Why not then to create excellence research centres, generating quality employment and revitalising their economies?
The second proposal concerns energy.
These regions can and should be the embryo of projects that demonstrate the potential for energy self-sufficiency and sustainability, particularly through micro-generation from local sources.
It reduced dependence, increased equality and created jobs.
Finally, I would like to say that using GDP as the only or main indicator for allocating funds and aid can only result in more injustice.
It is therefore very important to use the supplementary data, particularly that which reflects the social and economic reality of these regions.
I cannot fail to make a special reference here to the peoples of the Azores, Madeira, the mountainous areas and the depopulated inland areas of Portugal.
With their efforts, they make a major contribution to development and to the quality of life of all Europeans.
These regions, and all regions, have their own context.
Social, economic and territorial cohesion should not be seen as charity.
This is where we define whether or not we want a real European redistribution policy.
I therefore consider this resolution to be an important first step.

Mr President, Commissioner, the objective of territorial cohesion, now enshrined in the Treaty of Lisbon, embodies the value of European solidarity and the commitment of the Member States and the Union to reducing the disparities that exist between the various regions.
However, it is not enough to simply state the objectives to be achieved in the text of the treaties.
There are regions in the Union which have permanent constraints which strongly condition their economic and social development.

It is therefore necessary to implement specific programmes that effectively and truly overcome the lag of these regions by promoting an adaptation of their development model to their potential and resources, and contributing to achieving the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy.
This incentive to these regions should result not only from the regional policy, through its instruments, the Structural Funds, such as the Cohesion Fund and the four Structural Funds, but also
and the different use of the various sectoral policies, which have a significant territorial impact in each region and can therefore have a stimulating effect on their economies.
As regards the determination of European assistance to these regions, it is true that we should maintain GDP as the main eligibility indicator.

However, taking into account their natural disadvantages, it is appropriate to use other measurable criteria capable of providing a more realistic picture of their level of development, such as the unemployment rate, population density or level of education.
Only in this way will it be possible to obtain a more complete picture of the complex reality of the less-favoured regions.

Let us not forget the effect on the outermost regions, whose status has been recognised by the new treaty and which are a group of regions of the European Union that have specific characteristics and also deserve special treatment.
The outermost regions, united by their particular characteristics, also differ in their levels of development.

Likewise, with regard to these, complementary indicators to GDP should be used, capable of providing a more complete description of their specific reality and thus contributing to a more accurate determination of their level of development.
Mr President, I welcome this motion for a resolution, which calls for the need for a European strategy that makes it possible to translate the principle of cohesion
The Treaty of Lisbon gives the outermost regions opportunities to allow the populations of the territories that suffer from various permanent natural handicaps to participate in the European project.

I come from a region, the Azores, where these constraints accumulate and intensify, as in other regions known as Europe's outermost regions, in terms of Article 349 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union.
Following the Commission communication entitled 'Regions on the periphery: a triumph for Europe in 2008' and after the extensive debate in which the regions themselves participated,
actively, we also now await our version of this strategy, which will have to take into account not only our constraints but, above all, our potential.
I therefore call on the Commission to be prompt in its presentation, so that we can establish the best strategy before the debate on the new perspectives.

financial perspectives, because we must first define the content of policies before allocating the resources needed to implement them, as has been happening with the Community policies currently under review.

Mr President, Commissioners, ladies and gentlemen, after four consecutive years of reduction in the unemployment rate, from 2004 to 2008, the current economic crisis has wiped out many of the advances made.
Unemployment rates in the European Union have increased enormously. For example, in Portugal, we have unfortunately reached a record unemployment rate of 11%.
Another negative phenomenon is youth unemployment in the European Union.
Europe is therefore going through a period of transformation and is facing enormous challenges.
The crisis has wiped out years of economic and social progress and has exposed the fragilities of the economies of its Member States and, consequently, of the European Union itself.
The 2020 strategy points out the paths to follow.
The European Union must intensify all its efforts to promote employment and social inclusion as an integral part of its strategy to combat the economic and social crisis.

I therefore welcome the motion for a resolution on the future of the European Social Fund (ESF), which is being debated today, as this fund plays a crucial role in achieving the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy.

In fact, this fund is the main financial instrument that enables the European Union to achieve the strategic objectives of its employment policy, improving the levels of education and training of its citizens.

The European Social Fund (ESF) should be strengthened as the main engine of the Europe 2020 strategy and should be used by Member States to invest in training, employment, education and professional retraining.
Finally, I would like to stress that the reform of the European Social Fund, to be carried out jointly with the reform of the other Structural Funds, should facilitate and simplify its management and
to strengthen transparency, as well as to focus the funds on achieving results and on achieving objectives that are effectively felt by citizens in their daily lives.
The European Social Fund is probably the most structuring cohesion instrument.
It should therefore be closely linked to the European Regional Development Fund.
We need a global approach to the issues to be resolved, that is, a global vision for local action.
Local and regional authorities are best placed to know their realities, their investment needs, the creation of jobs and the most appropriate measures to promote employment in their regions.
We must take into account the positive experiences of the regions.
In the Azores, an outermost region where unemployment usually reaches record levels, the unemployment rate of 6.5% is the lowest in Portugal.

This is largely due to the strategy of forward-looking planning used for employment, defining the factors on which to act, how to achieve the desired results and also the use of the European Social Fund as an operational instrument of this strategy, acting on education, training and employment promotion.
The horizontal nature of cohesion policy is the only way to ensure the success of the Europe 2020 strategy.
Cohesion policy is the main pillar of European solidarity.
It is through cohesion that the objective of reducing disparities between regions is achieved and the harmonious development of the European Union as a whole is encouraged.
For the next period, 2014-2020, we must define a framework capable of strengthening the effectiveness of regional policy.

Governance at various levels should be encouraged, with greater involvement and participation by regional and local authorities, in line with the principle of subsidiarity, as provided for in the new Lisbon Treaty.
The special situation of the outermost regions must not be forgotten.
This group of regions faces very important challenges and should continue to negotiate specific provisions in the application of European policies in the future.
GDP should continue to be the main eligibility criterion, but we should take into account other indicators that can provide a more complete picture of regional reality.
ageing and with very low birth rates.
These factors represent enormous challenges for the European Union, to which we must provide concrete answers.
This observation has been widely accepted during this debate between us, despite the different views that this discussion has brought to light.
In Portugal, for example, the birth rate does not ensure the renewal of generations and reveals a reality that compromises the future.
This situation occurs in my country, as it does in most of the Member States of the European Union.
I am sure that more flexible policies on maternity leave will help to reverse these trends.
We must send families a consistent message of support for motherhood, with concrete measures to improve the reconciliation of professional, private and family life.
Women must be protected so that they can choose to have children without having to leave the labour market.
Achieving this challenge is essential if we want to meet the economic and social objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy and combat demographic ageing.
In Portugal, for example, maternity leave is already fully paid for 120 days, in an attempt to counteract the low birth rate we suffer from.
I therefore support the idea that women's salaries should be guaranteed during maternity leave, in the ways presented in the report under discussion.
Allowing each Member State to create the conditions to guarantee full pay during maternity leave by 2020 seems a sensible procedure.
Finally, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs Estrela, for her persistence in defending measures aimed at protecting families, which also help to counteract population ageing.
Firstly, with regard to these two reports, I would like to pay tribute to and publicly acknowledge the remarkable work done by the two rapporteurs,
Hélène Flautre, Sophie in 't Veld, Roberta Angelilli, Diana Wallis and Rebeca Harms, who were part of Parliament's delegation of MEPs who negotiated this framework agreement with the Commission.

I would also like to record the great openness and highly constructive spirit of cooperation of the President of the Commission and, after the new Commission was appointed, of Commissioner Šefčovič and their teams.
I believe that a remarkable job has been done, which has led to a historic agreement, which is an agreement that represents the first framework agreement after the signing of the Treaty
Lisbon and the first framework agreement that has a direct basis, an express basis in the treaties, more specifically in Article 295 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union.
I would like to say that this agreement does create a relationship of great transparency and great dynamism, even of a certain intimacy between
Parliament and the Commission, and essentially represents a compromise, a balance between the two institutions ’ views on the new role of each of them after the Treaty of Lisbon.

I believe that our negotiating team did very positive work, because it succeeded in translating into this framework agreement the new powers and the strengthening of powers resulting from the Treaty of Lisbon.
This applies to the legislative procedure and to programming or planning, for example, just to give two examples, in involving Parliament in
Commission work programme, or for example on issues related to the use of soft law in areas that are legislative competences of Parliament, as laid down by the Commission.

Secondly, a very important aspect is strengthening Parliament's powers of control and scrutiny, whether in terms of detailing and specifying the rules for electing the President and the College of Commissioners, or, for example,

the presence of Parliament in issues such as the reshuffle or possible dismissal of a Commissioner, or, for example, the hearing of the directors of regulatory agencies, or the monitoring of international negotiations.
In all these dimensions, there has in fact been a consolidation of powers, powers that result from the Treaty of Lisbon.
Access to information is also very important, and in particular access to information that is classified or information that is
confidential. Furthermore, the exchange of information and the exchange of views between the people responsible in Parliament and the people responsible in the Commission.

For example, we know that today it is already possible for the Commissioner responsible for employment and social affairs and the President of the Commission to take part in the relevant parts of the Conference of Presidents.

Or, for example, that there are various dialogue platforms between the Conference of Presidents, the Conference of Committee Chairs, the President of Parliament, the President of the Commission and the College of Commissioners, all of them expressed.
The Commission's presence in Parliament was also strengthened, particularly through the application of the ' question hour ' to not only the President of the Commission but also to the Commissioners.
I would therefore say that, out of deep respect for the Treaty of Lisbon and its new balance of powers, and out of deep respect for the idea of separation of powers,
We have here a clarifying agreement that will make the Treaty of Lisbon current, alive and applicable.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur on her work, as well as all those who collaborated with her on this report, which made it possible to reach this agreement at first reading.

This directive, which reformulates the previous one from more than ten years ago, aims, as everyone has acknowledged here, to put an end to the widespread problem of payment defaults in the European Union.
processes, which in some Member States are unreasonably long when they involve the public administration.

The negative effects of this practice on economic life are enormous, both at the level of small and medium-sized enterprises and at the level of the internal market, and these negative effects can no longer be tolerated, especially as we are living through an economic and financial crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.
This legislative amendment will give coherence and meaning to the plan to relaunch the European economy and to the implementation of the Small and Medium-sized Enterprises (SME) instrument.
surely this directive will improve the liquidity of businesses.
Creditors will have an effective and efficient instrument to exercise their rights against defaulting debtors.
With this directive, censorship by public bodies will be the exception.
The public administration bodies should set an example of good citizens and pay the bills for goods and services within one month.
It is now up to the institutions involved, to the Council and to the Member States, to allow the transposition of this directive to be carried out as quickly as possible.
Mr President, today there is a great disparity in many Member States as regards payment conditions to companies, especially when the State, SMEs and large companies are involved.

We who are familiar with businesses know that, in this situation of economic crisis and increased financing difficulties, companies are increasingly struggling with liquidity problems and, in many Member States, are even at increased risk of bankruptcy.
It seems clear that the current directive, which attempted to regulate this matter, has had limited effects, so the proposal we are now analysing represents an important step towards compliance
payment terms, not only in relations between companies and public bodies, but also, and this is essential, in relations between companies themselves.

We now have legislation that brings us greater added value, with a clear system of penalties for late payment, and that also brings us an effective improvement in the balance of abuse of dominant positions that the States and large companies exercise.
Madam President, Commissioner, allow me to talk about the unspoken.
I listened very carefully, Commissioner, and from what I heard, it seems to me that so far the most effective action by both the national and European authorities has been silence.
Industrial accidents can and must be avoided. European legislation exists for this very purpose and it seems obvious that the law was broken.
There was ineffectiveness, a late response, and abandonment of the affected people, particularly the most disadvantaged.
People have died and many are still threatened today by what may happen to their health.
It is impossible to calculate the total costs, and it is immoral not to care for the victims.
How is it possible, I ask you, Commissioner, that we learn of the news through reports from Hungarian citizens, while the authorities see nothing or tell us nothing?
That is why I would like to ask a few questions.
How can we allow banks to pressure citizens who have mortgaged homes in the affected area, asking them to pay what they owe, when their homes have lost value?
How can we allow telephone operators to cut off the only means of contact for people in disaster areas?
How come the company responsible for this disaster has already resumed operations?
Finally, Commissioner, you spoke about mitigating the effects of the mud spill.
Ask him what the reasons are for continuing to downplay the true scale of this human tragedy.
It seems that we are always starting from scratch.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we all recognise that the control of macroeconomic variables has failed in the European Union, both in the short and long term, particularly in terms of budgetary frameworks and accumulated national debt.
The crisis has underlined the need to strengthen the single market, bearing in mind the Monti and Greco proposals.

It is essential to develop cross-border e-commerce, simplify online payment processes, standardise products and services and harmonise tax instruments in order to create consumer confidence and boost the economy.
The Union must emerge from the crisis in a sustainable way, ensuring solid growth and responsible budgets, but also employment objectives.
Indicators such as the unemployment rate and the employment rate of the working-age population must be included in the surveillance system.
The indicators that will measure the progress of the 2020 strategy must not be forgotten either.
The feasibility study on issuing common European bonds could provide an opportunity to deepen and put into practice financial instruments to defend against speculation.
We would have liked to have gone further than a mere study.

The creation of the European Monetary Fund deserves our support, and we support it not only as a disciplinary instrument, but above all as a means of reducing speculative manipulation of sovereign debt markets.
The cooperation with the rapporteur, Mr Feio, was fruitful and we managed to achieve a comprehensive, balanced and rich text.
Madam President, Mr President of the Commission, I would like to begin by thanking Mr Feio for his work on the compromise and for the spirit of compromise he has shown in his report.
However, we concluded that Parliament, in certain cases – and this also happened in financial supervision –
able to unite and take a strong stance defending the interests of citizens, and that this strong stance should be recognised by the Commission and the Council.
This aspect is particularly relevant at a time when a legislative package of six proposals, which are extremely sensitive, is being launched, and in many of which Parliament has co-decision powers.
We will also be active, but without concessions.
Our spirit is one of dialogue, but not at the expense of the objectives we wish to defend.
In this respect, the Feio report highlights some framing aspects.
One of them is that economic governance is more than a package of sanctions.
Growth and employment need own resources.
We need initiatives to combat the growing internal disparities in the European Union.
We need concrete proposals on the European Monetary Fund.
We need stable solutions for sovereign debt.
We are at a time when confidence depends on the ability of the Commission and the Council to respond to citizens ’ real concerns, which are unemployment, growth and cohesion.
Mrs Berès and all those who collaborated to obtain the report that is under discussion today.

In the context of this report on the Commission's work, I drafted a thematic contribution in which I highlighted the crucial role of small and medium-sized enterprises as the driving force behind the recovery of the European Union and future growth and prosperity.
In fact, there are more than 20 million small and medium-sized enterprises in the European Union.
If each of them could create a job, that would mean an equivalent reduction in unemployment.
This document sets out recommendations as economic strategies for exiting the crisis. I would like to highlight the main ones.
Firstly, we need to strengthen the social market economy, avoiding restrictions on competition and ensuring that small and medium-sized enterprises have access to credit.
the granting of tax incentives and even subsidies to SMEs to maintain and create jobs.
the creation of a new Small Business Act with a stronger social dimension.
the establishment of a European network of senior advisers to disseminate their knowledge.
Innovation as the most powerful engine of economic growth and, as such, the importance of an organic link between industry and innovation.

the establishment of new partnerships between industry and academia, and, finally, the creation of an education system that meets the needs of the labour market, but also the need to create new qualifications for new jobs.
Mr President, intelligent growth, as promoted by the Europe 2020 strategy, can only be achieved if we intelligently mobilise the different potentialities hidden in European diversity.
The 25 million square kilometres of exclusive zone give the European Union the largest EEZ in the world.
320 000 km of coastline, where a third of our population lives, generating 40% of our gross domestic product and 95% of our external trade, are figures that should alert the Commission and
and the Council to act, and that they cannot do without an integrated European maritime policy in order to achieve intelligent growth, essential for the success of the Europe 2020 strategy.

It draws attention in particular to the potential of the genetic and mineral wealth that our deep sea and our seabeds contain, which are still poorly known, and to the need to increase our research and development efforts in these areas.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating Mr Barnier on presenting this important document.
As diagnosed, the impact of the crisis we are experiencing is being felt in all sectors of society and of our economy, causing our citizens to lose heart and to lose confidence in the single market.
But it is precisely this single market, one of Europe ’ s triumphs, that can be used to combat the current crisis.
We therefore warmly welcome this communication, which represents a new ambition for internal market policies, enabling us to restore confidence in our social market economy model.
In the current situation, the European Union must pay particular attention to its 20 million small and medium-sized enterprises.
In this respect, one of the Union's priorities should be the creation of mechanisms to solve the problem of access to credit for these companies.

We also highlight the Commission proposal to evaluate the Small Business Act by the end of 2010, in order to make life easier for these companies, particularly in terms of simplifying bureaucracy and taxation, as well as promoting social responsibility.
We hope that these 50 measures now presented will help to deepen the internal market, making full use of it, which could represent additional growth for the European economy.
Mr President, today we are discussing what has been presented as one of the European Commission's highest priorities, namely the innovation policy.
finally take shape, particularly through the European partnerships for innovation and with this pilot project that we are discussing today on active and healthy ageing.
However, there are many aspects that still need to be defined, particularly in terms of clarification, the definition of future projects and priorities.
Who will ultimately define the priorities and on the basis of what criteria?
I think that these responses are overdue.
However, I would like to make some specific comments, particularly on this first project on active and healthy ageing, which is the one that was presented to us.

And these notes are, first of all, a positive note, because I think it is positive that we are focusing on a sector of the population that is particularly vulnerable in a Europe that is increasingly ageing and is becoming increasingly disadvantaged.

However, I would like to add even more questions to these questions, because it seems contradictory to present a project in this area when, at the same time, we do not have anywhere to support this innovation.
How are we going to support them?
What is the basis?

Is there any viable innovation in a context such as the one we are experiencing, in which there is a persistent and systematic attack on the welfare state, a reduction in access to health, systematic cuts in services provided and continued disinvestment in this area?
