The title of the communication presented, 'An innovation union: more jobs, better lives, better society', is ambitious.

I would even say that I would have no problem signing it personally, but let us agree that it is contradictory to what has been happening in the Member States, with the cuts and the austerity measures.

I think that sustainable innovation is only possible if there are rigorous, sustainable and demanding public policies; otherwise, it is a match that goes out and we have little left and little response to give to these populations. Thank you very much.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we welcome the Commission ’ s efforts to place research and innovation at the top of the political agenda.
We are facing an integrated strategy to make the European innovation ecosystem more effective and profitable in the service of a sustainable economy.
The European Innovation Partnerships are emerging as an instrument, and the major challenges they are based on are broad enough not to be controversial.
However, it is not yet clear how the sub-thematic priorities will be defined, or how the weight of each discipline within them will be determined.
What level of transparency is expected from stakeholders when arbitrating conflicting interests?
What is the relative role of the Commission, the Member States and the regions?
How can we ensure accountability in the context of a complex structure?
What financial dimension will these partnerships have?
The Commission and the Council, Mr President, must ensure excellence in implementation, which is not guaranteed by the few months of pilot testing planned for the first partnership.

The Committee on International Trade is currently assessing the global banana agreement reached at the World Trade Organisation, and shortly the plenary of this House will be called upon to express itself on the reduction of customs tariffs on imports of bananas from Latin American countries.

Let it be made quite clear that none of the outermost regions, whether Madeira or the Azores, or any of the other eight, especially the Canary Islands, which are by far the most disadvantaged, are against the signing of this type of agreement.
What these regions want is simply that, on the one hand, the same hygiene, phytosanitary and respect for animal welfare standards be required of others,

workers, which are required of them in order to access the market, which is the most basic form of justice, and secondly, that they should be compensated in the exact amount of the damage caused to them.

Otherwise, if that is not the case, let us at least have the decency to tell them straight out that they will have to change their lives and look for another job, because it is certain that under the conditions that are expected in the future they will not be able to survive and will have to abandon their activity.
It is true that in these agreements there are winners and losers; let us therefore look after the losers, because the winners do not need it.
We are here discussing a very important programme.
concrete measures to help economic recovery, in this case in a central area, namely energy.
In the current context of crisis, proposals such as this one, which help to create jobs, are fundamental.
There is no possibility of economic recovery unless we make job creation our top priority.
This report sends out a very positive signal when it draws our attention to the way in which we should respond at local level, and to the need to respond concretely to local needs.
We are in a period in which we are discussing and will continue to discuss a number of legislative proposals in the energy field, and we often tend to
we focus too much on large-scale projects, on large networks, and forget about other scales of intervention where it is absolutely essential to act.

That is why I think that this report can help us to invest not only in energy efficiency and in renewable energies, which is very important, but also to bring tangible benefits to local populations, which will naturally translate into tangible benefits for the European population as a whole.
That is why I would like to conclude, Mr President, with two final points: we must never forget that energy efficiency is achieved largely through the energy reduction of projects.

micro-generation, as presented here, and the final point, which was a great achievement for Parliament, is that we managed to guarantee that there would be a specific definition of the amount of funding to be applied to these projects.
It is not what we wanted; we needed much more, and so I ask the Commissioner what he is trying to do and intends to do to ensure that these needs are met.
Mr President, Commissioner Dalli, ladies and gentlemen, patients should have the right to know as much as possible about their illness and about the medicines available to treat it.
But how can we distinguish information from publicity?
Where and how can we gather objective and unbiased information?
There are different interpretations of the current texts; they need to be harmonised, reinforcing the sovereignty of the citizen.
The information must be reliable, validated by independent authorities, accessible and aimed at the average, non-specialist public.
The aim is to create a more enlightened and responsible citizenship among end users.
The deadline given to the European Agency for tacit approval should be extended from 60 to 120 days.
The requirement for justification of the refusal strengthens the right of defence and balances the final result.
The EMA has an essential role as a privileged source of objective information.

I would like to conclude by congratulating the rapporteur, Mr Fjellner, on his work and by welcoming the fact that he has accepted almost all of the proposals that I tabled in the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection in my capacity as rapporteur for opinion.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, bee mortality is increasing and the number of beekeepers is decreasing, a situation that is problematic for agriculture and for biodiversity, as has already been said, given the enormous implications for food production and plant species that depend on their pollination.
The life of a worker bee is very short, about six weeks, and it is very sensitive to changes in the environment.

Therefore, honey production has also been affected, not only by the increasingly uncertain duration and stability of the seasons, but also by the increase in external attacks that may result from the use of pesticides, acaricides or other

nature, and on which a higher level of research could contribute to obtaining better answers, applied and more accurate reinforcement of measures in favour of biodiversity and reducing the effects of climate instability.
The current programmes, Commissioner, should undoubtedly be strengthened within the framework of the new CAP so that we can also obtain more effective responses at this level.
Mr President, let us be clear: the mechanism for solidarity for sovereign debt did not work and does not work. The price of Greek debt has not fallen, Ireland is in

economic turmoil, and the contagion has not been contained. The process that was created was created too late, it is intergovernmental, and it should have existed in a different format from the very beginning of the creation of the euro.
The Commission is now proposing to consolidate this system.
However, the third of the instruments proposed for use suggests involving the private sector.
We had already heard this proposal from Chancellor Merkel, and it was as a result of this premature and untimely announcement that the markets went wild.
Parliament will fully use its powers and, with the greatest responsibility and spirit of cooperation, in the co-decision process accompanying the energy package,
legislative work on economic governance, but it will not do so at the expense of haste and speed, putting quality second.

Therefore, let us be clear: Parliament will participate actively, but an issue as serious as resolving sovereign debt cannot be decided without the knowledge of European citizens and their representatives. The two elements go together.
Finally, we need, we Europeans, a clear European vision at this time of crisis.
We need a European debt consolidation mechanism, we need to issue Eurobonds and we need the euro area to be sustainably protected through European systems, not intergovernmental ones.

The European budget needs to be strengthened. We cannot continue with one percent of the level of the European budget, and we need to place real growth and convergence at the heart of European political priorities.
This is the agenda that the Commission and the new President must defend.
The President of the Commission cannot be an emanation of the Ecofin Council.
This is what we need to present to European citizens.
Mr President, Mr President-in-Office of the Council, Mr President of the Commission, I would like to say that the position of the EPP is clear. It is a position in the sense that
that the resolution of this crisis, this serious crisis that Europe and Europeans are living through, can only be achieved through a deepening of economic governance and of the Community method.

For us, there is no doubt that only with more Community method, only with economic governance, only with the proper instruments of a genuine single currency in the euro area is it possible to confront and tackle this crisis.
But this means that each of the institutions – the Council, the Commission and Parliament – must assume their responsibilities to Europeans.
It should be made clear that at least the largest groups in Parliament are clearly prepared to cooperate in increasing
the Community method, to introduce economic governance, to give the single currency the conditions necessary for it to triumph in the context of the European crisis.

It is also clear today that the same applies to the Commission and to the President of the Commission, who, contrary to what the socialists and Ms Ferreira say, have shown that they are not an emanation of the Council or of Parliament, but an emanation of European interests.
It is now up to the Council, in December, to assume its responsibilities.
That is what we count on, and on your clear commitment to the European ideal, President Van Rompuy.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating my fellow Members, Mr Luhan and Mr Marinescu, on the outstanding work they have done in their respective reports.
Both are important contributions to the ongoing discussion on the new cohesion policy for the 2014-2020 period.
Today more than ever, economic, social and territorial cohesion is essential for affirming the competitiveness of the European Union on a global scale.
We will only achieve this goal if we realise that only by strengthening and developing the regional dimension can we have a Europe that is cohesive internally and competitive externally.

In this respect, I would highlight three points that I consider to be crucial. First, decentralisation, so that local and regional authorities, especially those with legislative powers, can participate more and contribute to a better implementation of cohesion policy. Second,

Member States to invite regional and local authorities to participate in negotiations on the future of the Structural Funds on an equal footing with national authorities and representatives; and lastly, strengthening the role of regional authorities in the preparation, management and implementation of programmes.
Only in this way, with greater involvement of these authorities throughout the process, will the principle of subsidiarity be fulfilled.

Another aspect that I consider crucial is the call for a simpler architecture for the funds after 2013, not as a consequence of the economic crisis, but rather as a general principle of the future cohesion policy, so as to facilitate absorption of the funds.
The main concern underlying the Treaty of Lisbon was to bring Europe closer to its citizens.
Let us do not only that, but also make their lives easier by avoiding unnecessary administrative burdens, which are a deterrent to their participation.
Only then will we be able to achieve the objective of genuine territorial cohesion.
Only in this way will we reduce disparities within the European Union, which are today clearly marked and with a huge gap between rich and poor regions.
More participation also means more responsibility, and this responsibility must be assumed and shared by everyone, doing more and better in order to have a stronger and more competitive Europe.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating my fellow Members, Mrs Bauer and Mrs Hedh, on their excellent work on this report.
Human trafficking brings shame on our society and requires a strong, coordinated response from all of us.
Organised crime behind sexual exploitation, forced labour, trafficking in human organs and other despicable practices must be effectively combated, but above all it must be prevented.
What is at stake is the defence of human rights and the protection of the most vulnerable people, particularly women and children.
Experience has shown that the current legal framework is not sufficiently effective and that the European Union must do more.
Greater cross-border cooperation is essential, including effective sharing of information and best practices.
This proposal rightly highlights a victim-centred approach.
Policies in this area must cover aspects related to social affairs and social inclusion, such as the social rehabilitation of victims.
Mr President, the NATO Summit took place in Lisbon and so allow me to say a few words about what happened in my country in connection with this Summit.
At that time, the Portuguese authorities banned European citizens from entering the country for one reason only: they were carrying texts critical of the Alliance.
These days, the least we can say is that an arbitrary regime has come into force in Portugal, an authentic thought police.
a regime so special that it led the Portuguese Government to purchase riot control equipment worth EUR 5 million, which was not even delivered in time for the summit.
Are these the priorities of a government facing a huge social crisis?
In the Europe that we dream of, freedom of expression is a fundamental value.
The Europe we dream of does not bow down before military organisations that write their history in blood.
The NATO summit legitimised its military intervention throughout the planet.
It also emerged that the EU is subservient to the military strategy and interests of North America.
Thirdly, the militarisation of access to energy sources has been consolidated.
Finally, the military defence of the development model that generates environmental imbalances and exacerbates the social crisis was established.
For this reason, Madam President, in my opinion, with this summit, we lose in democracy, we lose in freedom, and we lose so that some can win with war.
Madam President, the first point I would like to make clear, and which is important for us to reinforce in this Parliament, is
Contrary to what is sometimes reported in the press and by some European leaders, the euro as a currency has been fundamental in responding to the current crisis.

Without the euro, we would be in a very difficult situation, with the currencies of the most fragile countries suffering a huge devaluation and with the German mark possibly undergoing a revaluation that would be impossible for the German economy and the European economy to withstand.
Therefore, the euro has been a stabilising factor, not only for the countries in the euro area, but also for the currencies of the countries that did not want to join the euro area.

That is why we must defend this Community, which has responded to the crisis better than any other, and which, in the future, we will look to as an example of what will happen to the dollar and to the United States of America. We will see that the euro has advantages.

We now have a responsibility in this Council to do everything to defend the euro, and in particular to set up a stabilisation fund based on the Community method, which is capable of introducing responsibility in the

countries in a more fragile situation, and solidarity from those that have fulfilled their obligations and have not always shown, at least through their external statements, that they are willing to be solidary in the context of the euro area.

Mr President, I would like to offer my sincere congratulations to Mr Surján on the excellent report produced, and I strongly urge the Commission to take it into account, particularly in those aspects where the complex nature of agricultural issues, which go far beyond the narrow scope of

agriculture, require the involvement of other areas, such as competition, the internal market and financial regulation, as well as being dealt with in other international fora, such as the G20 and the WTO.

The absolutely decisive issues for agriculture, for farmers and for the supply of sufficient food of adequate quality to the populations of Europe and the world can only be properly addressed if they are approached in all their dimensions.

The ambitious objectives of the CAP reform can only be achieved if the reform goes beyond the strict boundaries of agriculture and its objectives are also taken into account in the necessary interventions in other policy areas and in other global bodies.
This report makes this very clear, and I welcome it.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by thanking everyone for the words spoken here in relation to the report that Parliament is presenting on Alzheimer's and other dementias.
We all know that the situation we are in is substantially dramatic and still very much under-diagnosed, as has been said here.
I would just like to mention one issue that was common to all the speeches, but which was probably not stressed enough, and that is the issue of the dignity of patients and their rights.
not only of patients but also of their families and of those who care for them, whether formally or informally, and of the professionals who are involved in this type of illness.

I am pleased, frankly pleased, with the words of the Commissioner when he says that he shares the same concerns as the European Parliament and that the European Commission views very positively all the recommendations in the report presented by Parliament and that it will do everything within its power to put them into practice.
We know that every day that passes is a day too late in this matter, which is why we have to act urgently.
We also know that tomorrow Parliament will be voting on this matter.
We will await the result of the vote, but we know, Commissioner, that the citizens of Europe expect much more from us than solidarity and moral comfort.

expect concrete and appropriate action from us. I very much hope, Commissioner, that you will then put into practice, and that the Commission, which has the right of initiative, which unfortunately this Parliament does not have, will put into practice

All this priority that you are giving to this area and that you are turning into a legislative initiative so that citizens and their families see their rights respected and are treated with the equality and dignity that they deserve.

First of all, I would like to say that this Parliament, on a day when we are discussing the Hungarian Presidency, without prejudice to being able to discuss all the issues that it intends, should be concentrating on the problems of the European Union, which is not what it is doing here.

In order to focus on the problems of the European Union, we should have read carefully and discussed here whether or not we agree with the priorities of the Hungarian Presidency, instead of discussing points

or aspects that are aspects of Hungary ’ s internal life and that, at most, can be regulated in a healthy and normal relationship between the European Commission and the Hungarian Government and Parliament.

I would therefore like to say here that, apart from the priority that the Hungarian Presidency will naturally give to the economic and financial issues on the agenda, I am very pleased to see that it is
very clear and very ambitious Hungarian agenda for this semester, focusing on three issues that will be essential for the future of Europe.

food, which is entering a serious global crisis, energy, which is essential for our security and for our economic sustainability, and water, which few have spoken about, but which will be the next challenge for Europeans after food.
I hope you achieve your goals.

Madam President, I think that these two countries, like others in this region, show us how, in recent years, European leaders have, on many occasions, sided more with business than with democracy, more with dictators than with the poor.
I am frankly concerned about the situation in Egypt at the moment and about today ’ s developments, and that is why I think that we should not be calm, but that we should show solidarity.
solidarity with the millions of Egyptian citizens who are in Cairo and throughout the country, who are fed up with hunger, fed up with oppression, fed up with unemployment.

That is why, after Mubarak's speech today, after the regime's dogs have been released, after a clear and open path has been created for violence, we must say that we must stand alongside these people.
We cannot allow a strategy of fear to be created to make them go home.
In Portugal, 36 years ago, we would have gone home if we had followed those pieces of advice.
We are lucky that we did not go back, because then we would have had a democratic revolution.
Mr President, Commissioner, I would also like to say that I think this issue is important, but not so important that we should continue living in the illusion that we have lived in in Europe.

Either deception or false promise, as they have called it, because we have been operating, particularly in the area of collection of stem cells in umbilical cords, on the basis of information that is misleading.
We know, and it has already been mentioned, that in Europe, collection of stem cells through the umbilical cord does not reach 1%.
However, we also know that their possibilities for treating diseases may be endless: diseases such as those already mentioned, cancer, Alzheimer's disease, rare diseases, offer immense possibilities.

But what we allowed to happen was the creation of a set of private banks for the preservation of cells, telling parents that they could store their children's cells and, from then on, from the time of birth, cure them in the future if necessary.
I think we should be more responsible than that, because the probability of guaranteeing that this will happen is very low, as we know.
This false promise that has been made to parents costs money.
In Portugal, for example, this false promise costs three and a half minimum wages.
The solution clearly lies in creating public banks for stem cells, because only with public banks can we guarantee equal use and equal access.
and effective medical treatment and more research that is the same for everyone, rather than just using stem cells as a commodity or a business, which is growing, but which is only for some.
Therefore, I think that solidarity, common sense and impartial information are not, after all, things that can be classified as extra requirements.
We do not have the power to impose, but we do have the power to promote.
This is within our powers and I think we should do it in the name of equality.
Mr President, food prices have reached their highest level ever and this is the seventh consecutive month of increase.
There are several factors that can explain this increase, the first of which is a short-term factor.
It is due to the coincidence of adverse weather conditions in certain countries, such as Russia and Argentina.
However, there are two factors that are fundamental, and that is why I am raising these two factors here.
The first is the fact that some food producers and consumers who were exporters have become importers, as is the case of China.
The second point concerns the fact that the weight of some large multinationals enables them to manipulate prices in their favour.
The example of cereals is very clear at this level: it is dominated by six large multinationals.
This situation therefore places farmers in a state of great uncertainty regarding food prices.
Europe should therefore start doing what it has not done for some time, namely protecting its producers and consumers.
In a world where everyone resorts to protection methods and where multinationals manipulate prices, we cannot continue to be so naive.
I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs Matias, and all those who collaborated with her.
Unfortunately, over recent years, we have seen an increase in falsified medicines in Europe.
According to data from the European Union, one and a half million boxes of falsified medicines are sold annually through the legal supply chain.
This is a criminal activity that poses a serious threat to European patients and to European industry.
The objective of this directive is to prevent falsified medicines from entering the supply chain and thus protect public health.

I would like to highlight the following key points in the opinion I drafted: the introduction of the definition of falsified medicines, clearly focusing on consumer protection; the application of security features

security of prescription-only medicines, the responsibility of all those involved in the distribution chain and, finally, the application by Member States of effective penalties to deter this criminal activity.
Mr President, I obviously want to thank all the comments made by all my fellow Members.
I am not going to comment on them individually, as that is not the intention.
I think that if there were any doubts about what the word 'compromise' means, it has become very clear here what the word 'compromise' means.

We do not all have the perfect proposal, but much more important than knowing whether it is the perfect proposal is knowing that we have a tool in our hands that will allow us to stop delaying the protection of patients ’ health.
We also have an obligation not to delay its adaptation, we have an obligation not to delay real action, and we can only do this together.
I must also say personally, and the Commissioner mentioned the issue of inspections, that in my original proposal, I tabled an amendment for mandatory inspections in third countries.
That is what compromise is all about: you win some things and you lose others.
The Commission has explained to us that this would be impossible to obtain.
I would like to make one further point in relation to this compromise.
I think we are all committed, but I am also aware that we will all be demanding that it be fulfilled, because otherwise it will not be effective.

It is in this compromise that safeguards are guaranteed so that we can monitor the process, evaluate it and have systematic information that enables us to verify whether it is being properly implemented or not, and whether changes are needed.

I would therefore like to conclude by thanking the European Commission for its cooperation and, in particular, the Spanish and Belgian Presidencies, with which we have worked together over the past six months.
There are no perfect proposals, no perfect interventions, there are flaws, but I think we have a strong and robust commitment to allow us to fight and defend patients ’ health.
As my speech is also full of mistakes, I have failed to thank my colleagues, Mrs Bastos and Mrs Sartori, for the support from the other committees.
Thank you very much for your support.
Mr President, Commissioner, it is not worth repeating that the current moment is the most serious test for the survival of the euro and, therefore, of Europe.
The Poli Mata report opens up constructive solutions, and so I also want to congratulate our colleague on the ideas she has put forward.

The seriousness of the situation does not stem from the fact that the euro area as a whole has a deficit or excessive external debt; it stems rather from the fact that the economies that make it up are persistently and seriously diverging under the impact of the common policy.
We lack the instruments that will allow convergence between these economies, that will allow Europe to regain balance, that will allow Europe to grow.

Let us be clear: the eurozone and the European Union cannot survive with the current level of integration and the diversity of the economies that make it up, with a budget of 1% of the collective wealth.
In practice, in the face of the crisis, the only solution we have been able to build has been the austerity recipe, and it is workers and companies who are paying the price today.
the deficits generated by the speculative crisis in the financial sector, in the rescue of which 26% of the wealth of the European Union was committed.
The financial transaction tax, levied at a very low rate, for example, 0.05% on transactions in high-risk, highly speculative products, will indeed be paid by their consumers.
But why should they not pay if ordinary citizens pay VAT on bread, milk and other basic products?
The tax burden must be redistributed so as to harm workers and companies less and be shared more fairly by the financial sectors, particularly the speculative ones.
And, Commissioner, I think that the Commission cannot at this time refuse or run away from the clear message that Parliament is sending to it politically.
The public consultation cannot exclude the analysis of the financial transaction tax; on the contrary, it should be given the highest priority.
We know very well what happened with the A flu and that should have taught us all a lesson.
I do not know whether you remember how all this started, but the truth is that it started in one way and then the response was completely disproportionate to the scale of the problem.
That is why the report voted on today is so important: we must learn from what was done, so that what was done is not repeated.
We have the right to know who took the decisions, how the whole story unfolded and who decided that it should have unfolded in that way.
We have a right to know which countries responded in a certain way, why they responded, without transparency and without leaving us with a
minimum of doubts, because we have all the doubts if I go into business, we have to have the guarantee that no residue remains if I go into business related to this flu.
That is why I believe, Madam President, that all threats to public health must be tackled, but it is not indifferent how they are tackled or who pays for it.
Mr President, Commissioner, we are here talking about proposals on pay and proposals on retirement age, and the least we can say about these proposals is that they are frankly unfair.
They are unfair and they are insisting on recipes that have already been proven more than once to be failures.

What is happening in Ireland, Greece, Portugal, Spain, Italy and goodness knows how many other countries, because this is an endless list, are different situations in each of these countries, but there are some things in common.
One of the things we have in common is an unprecedented transfer of the value of labour to financial capital.
We cannot accept this.
Another thing we have in common is that it is the workers, pensioners and retirees who are paying for this crisis, as well as an entire generation of precarious workers who,
in the society in which we live, and I would like to conclude by saying, Mr President, Commissioner, we cannot continue to condemn people to poverty.
I sincerely hope.
Mr President, I would like to begin by saying that I fully support what my colleague said.

Prevention of natural and man-made disasters is of enormous importance and increasing importance, and the European Commission systematically presents us with a number of intentions, procedures and timetables.
However, as has already been said, there is a very complicated problem.
The Solidarity Fund is an instrument that normally responds to these situations, but it takes a very, very long time to respond: nine months to a year, and this is after the disaster.
As has already been said, and I repeat and will repeat as long as necessary, there is the case of Madeira, which suffered a huge disaster a year ago and still has not received any support.
I therefore ask, and I reiterate this question, whether it is not time for the Commission to review the procedure, the fund itself and the financing model?
How can we maintain an exceptional fund to deal with situations that are becoming increasingly chronic?
We do not know where it will happen, but we know that it will happen, and these climate changes are here to stay.
Therefore, an exception can never be the answer to a regular situation.
Mr President, the concerns raised here regarding disaster prevention and response are of great sensitivity and of the utmost importance to our populations, and must not be trivialised.

We are talking about very sensitive situations that affect people's safety and their property, and which often affect us all emotionally, due to the loss of lives, the destruction of families and the ruin of entire lives' work.

However, all our demonstrations in the face of tragedies that, in a media-driven way, impress and sensitize us all, are of little use if we then go to sleep on the work that we must do to ensure that those situations can be avoided, minimized or, better still, prevented.
Commissioner, my colleagues have already focused very well on the subject.

This is an area where the European Union can better demonstrate its presence among its citizens, helping them to prevent disasters and, above all, being at their side when disasters occur, at the very moments when they are most in need and most vulnerable.
I will answer with pleasure, although I would not like to turn a debate in the European Parliament into a debate on Portuguese politics.

In fact, if Ms Rita Estrela wants to debate Portuguese policy, there is a simple solution: we must have elections, candidates for the Portuguese Parliament, and she will have the opportunity, in opposition, to debate with the PSD Government the policies it intends for Portugal.
I am just saying the following: we can no longer stand a government that has failed four times in two years.
It is impossible, unsustainable, not credible to the markets, there are no more solutions with the socialist government.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating Mr Schwab on his negotiating tenacity, which led to this important compromise, and also to welcome all those who collaborated with him on this report.

We want not only to ensure a high level of protection for European consumers, but also for European companies, whatever their size, to be able to offer goods and services to consumers in the 27 Member States without unnecessary legal obstacles.

This new directive will put an end to the legislative fragmentation that has been hampering the participation of our companies in the cross-border market and will give a major boost to the internal market.
I would highlight the new provisions on information to be provided to consumers, requiring that this information be clear, intelligible and provided in good time.
It is also important that the burden of proof as regards compliance with information requirements is placed on the trader.
As regards contracts concluded at a distance, especially those concluded by telephone or via the internet, their rules should be defined and consumers protected by simple and effective mechanisms.
We are certain that, at the end of the institutional negotiations, the new directive will help to make the European Union more competitive and dynamic, as required in a global economy.

Madam President, President-in-Office of the Council, President of the Commission, first of all, I would like to congratulate you on the results of the Council of 24 and 25 March, particularly on the consolidation of the Semester

European, but much more than that, whether it is through the institutionalisation of the European Stability Mechanism or through the 'fleshing out', so to speak, of the Euro Plus Pact, which had already come from the Eurozone summit of 11 March.
I believe that these are very positive results for Europe and it is a pity that this process has not been fully concluded on 24 and 25 March.
I believe that if we had sent that signal, if the Member States had sent that signal in the Council, by completely closing this package of reforms of the Fund, we would today be in a position

stronger to face the next three or four months and, in part, we would have mitigated the effects of the crisis that my country, Portugal, is currently experiencing and on which I also do not want to miss the opportunity to say a few words.

to say that, in the case of the PSD, which is fully integrated into the EPP, there is an absolute and total willingness to comply with the requirements and obligations of integration into the European space.
That is what we have done, even now, at a time when there are difficulties with short-term financing.
In contrast to what the Socialist Government has done, the Social Democratic Party has shown total willingness to give Europe the guarantees that are necessary in the context of this crisis.

With this, I want to send this signal, without bringing internal politics into the European Parliament, of the commitment, of the pledge, of the total guarantee of the Social Democratic Party to the rigorous agenda that the country will have to have after the elections on 5 June.
Madam President, Commissioner Füle, ladies and gentlemen, the historic moment we are living through calls for firm and determined action.
Like many of you, I am watching the events unfolding in the Mediterranean basin with a mixture of hope, concern and expectation.

I am, however, satisfied with the proactive stance that Parliament is preparing to adopt in relation to the review of the European Neighbourhood Policy, which I hope will lead to a greater presence of the Union in this area, not only as a funder but also as a committed partner.
First of all, by affirming the absolute necessity of defending our founding values in our relations with neighbouring countries.
We can no longer compromise on the defence of democracy, nor on the defence of human rights, and even less on the defence of social justice.

We can no longer privilege short-term stability to the detriment of the higher interests of citizens and their intransigent defence of their individual and collective freedom, with particular attention to women's rights.
In future, the Union must give priority to a bottom-up approach in its development policy.
This is essential.
Only greater involvement by local communities and civil society will guarantee maximum effectiveness in its implementation.

However, I cannot refrain from expressing a frustration: I regret that this Parliament and the Commission do not want to take this opportunity to differentiate, once and for all, the Eastern Neighbourhood policy, with countries that could potentially become our partners in the Union in the future, from the Southern Neighbourhood policy.
I also challenge the Commission to show, in its 20 April review process, the ambition that the current situation demands: a neighbourhood policy
tailor-made, adapted to each country, with clear benchmarks and careful assessment, which will allow us to build a future Mediterranean economic area with the new democracies of the South.

To conclude, Commissioner, we trust that the good atmosphere and cooperation that have characterised the work on this subject will lead to permanent involvement of the European Parliament in the planning and assessment of this policy. Thank you very much.
Mr President, the reports we are debating, and for which I congratulate the rapporteurs, reinforce a set of initiatives whose ambition is to relaunch the internal market.
I would like to highlight the commitment of the European Commission, and in particular of Commissioner Barnier, whom I welcome, and the positive reception of this Parliament through the
the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection to give priority to competitiveness and dynamism of the market, a fundamental pillar of the Europe 2020 strategy.
Citizens and businesses expect effective stimuli to get Europe growing again economically, expect an increase in employment and aim to create more prosperity.

As the Commissioner pointed out, opening up markets to our companies, removing obstacles and difficulties, will give them a very special boost and an unequalled opportunity, enabling them to continue their mission as the driving force of our economy.

Measures aimed at reducing the administrative and bureaucratic burden on SMEs and at improving their access to credit and to the services market, the European patent and the recognition of qualifications are essential.
The relevance of developing e-commerce to strengthen consumer and business confidence in this type of commerce, the investment in innovation,
important for strong and lasting growth, and social entrepreneurship, which is essential for creating innovative business projects for social inclusion.
These are some of the measures that will ensure this ambitious project of completing, deepening and making the most of the single market for the benefit of European citizens.
Finally, an important recommendation that Member States redouble their efforts to improve the transposition and implementation of the rules of the internal market.
I would also like to add my voice to those who have expressed concern about the low incomes of fishermen, aggravated by the record prices of fuel, particularly in recent months.
As has been said here very well, fuel costs have a very high impact on the survival of our small and medium-sized shipowners, and are particularly worsening the already meagre fishing yields.
This dramatic increase in prices is also having serious negative effects: fewer boats going out to sea, fishermen earning poverty wages, worsened by the low prices paid at first sale.

increased imports of aquaculture and fisheries products from third countries into the European market, and the consequent abandonment of the activity due to financial difficulties, such as job losses.
In this context, in order to avoid the crisis getting worse, we need to act, and we need to act swiftly and thoroughly on the problems in fisheries.

The Commission should therefore give positive responses, with short- and long-term measures, to deal with the difficulties facing the sector and the serious income losses being suffered by our fishermen.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to congratulate, in particular, the work of our EPP colleague, Mário David, whom I represent today, for being fully

inconveniently prevented from attending this plenary, and I would like to say that we have always considered that support for the victims of the floods in Pakistan was an ethical and moral imperative.
We therefore strongly support all the measures taken in the direction of traditional humanitarian aid.
We believe that we should continue to work towards helping to stabilise the situation of the millions of people affected.
What we cannot accept is that the European Union's trade policy should now be considered an appropriate instrument for humanitarian aid.

The truth is that this has two disastrous consequences: one within the European Union, especially for southern countries and their textile and clothing industries, which are already highly weakened by an economic, social and financial crisis without parallel since

in the Union. Secondly, at global level, in the WTO negotiations, where this opening of the largest trading block in the world, the European Union, to these possible Pakistani exports, could completely upset the negotiations.
It is no coincidence that they are in the deadlock in which they find themselves.

It is therefore natural that, in a situation such as this, we should say something about the history of this regulation and about the difficulties it encountered during these eight months of intense negotiations.

we have collaborated with the most constructive proposals, and now, unfortunately, we have to take a position, which is a position in favour of the amendment that, in essence, provides for the end of this draft regulation as we know it.
We must do so now, not least out of solidarity with Pakistan, but because we believe that humanitarian aid must be delivered locally and in the appropriate manner.
Mr President, Commissioner, I would like to begin by saying that, of course, I think the agreement reached for Portugal is a balanced one.
It is a very tough agreement for the populations, but one which places its fundamental point on structural reforms to increase growth and competitiveness.
I think this is very positive.

I do not share the view of others who consider that countries such as Greece, Ireland and Portugal did everything right, did not make mistakes, did not exaggerate, did everything right and it was only the international crisis that caused the problems.

No, our governments made mistakes and had the wrong policies, but that does not mean that there is not now an opportunity, within the context of the Union, to have the possibility of returning to the right path.

It is therefore important to point out here that, in fact, we need to tell public opinion, which is against support for countries in difficulty, that they are not giving money to these countries, they are merely lending money to them.
They are lending them at very high interest rates and are therefore doing a deal that is positive for their budgets and for their populations.

But I also say here that perhaps it is time to have a global European response, with the admission of Eurobonds, with the possibility of selling on the primary market, in order to respond to international markets.

It might be time for the European Commission, the Fund and the IMF to review interest rates, so that by lowering them, we can give these countries, which are in difficulty today, the possibility of sustainable growth.
Mr President, Commissioner, rapporteur, this strategy is important for all European countries, especially for those whose economies lack competitiveness.
It is essential for the creation of small and medium-sized enterprises.
Today, it is more active in creating new jobs.
It is essential for universities and laboratories, whose relationship with the productive sectors determines part of their survival.
It is essential for the financial system, which will find in innovation a new market as the classic manufacturing model runs out.
Innovation is high on the European agenda and, as a pillar of the Europe 2020 strategy, it will need to be supported by new financial instruments so that it does not succumb to budgetary constraints.
Finally, I would like to welcome Judith Merkies for her important contribution to a more innovative Europe and an economy more based on knowledge.
Mr President, Madam Commissioner, I would like to begin by congratulating Mr Audy on his excellent report.

The mid-term evaluation, although late, enabled the critical aspects of the Seventh Framework Programme to be highlighted: excessive bureaucracy, lack of interest from industry, the number and complexity of the new instruments, and the slow pace of disbursement.
There is also a greater risk of transfers of funds from the current Framework Programme to large-scale programmes with questionable added value.

But it also allowed us to highlight positive aspects: greater international coordination and cooperation between scientific groups in joint projects, some success stories, including the European Research Council, and greater gender balance.
There are priorities that must be developed: first of all, simplification; secondly, promoting scientific excellence, not in some countries but throughout Europe; greater links between
framework programme, innovation, instruments for small and medium-sized enterprises and entrepreneurship, of which the most successful is the programme, which should be extended.

Madam President, Commissioner, I am going to address this proposal that has been presented to Parliament today, and I am going to say to you, quite frankly, that I think we have the right, some of us, to be disappointed with this proposal, because the proposal that is being presented is not only weak, but also has huge gaps.
I think this is a European issue, not an issue for the Member States that have nuclear power and those that do not, but for the whole of Europe, and Europe desperately needs good news.
I must honestly say that one of these good pieces of news is to guarantee to citizens that we are taking care of their security.

Now, when these tests are carried out in the terms presented by the Commissioner, that is, a first phase is carried out by the interested parties themselves, a second phase is carried out by the Member States,

those concerned, and a third phase is carried out by the peers who are truly interested. I wonder whether the final result will not be to say that all nuclear power plants are in very good health and that they are recommended.

We know that this is not the case, and so I very much hope that there will be serious consequences, and that we can say to our citizens that we are taking care of their safety and not just pretending that we are.
I hope that this European Council, especially given the climate that we have been experiencing in recent weeks, will take a decision of great responsibility.
and more responsibility than that shown at the last Council, in which an attempt was made to postpone a problem for three months simply because of electoral calendars.

I hope that this responsible decision will essentially make it clear that economic and financial issues are crucial and decisive for Europe, but that economic and financial issues are instrumental to what is Europe's main objective, which is to maintain peace.
if we approach the problems in such a way that it leads only to the States beginning to distrust each other, to instil distrust between the different States.
to create and fuel differences and rivalries between southern and northern countries, between central and peripheral countries. That is what will lead us to the decline of the European Union.

I believe that what is at stake in this European Council, regardless of technical solutions, regardless of political disagreements, is knowing whether or not we want the European project to continue to be a valid project for the peace strategy in Europe.
I think that is what is at stake.
What I notice in many speeches by leaders, both those from countries in difficulty and those from countries that are net contributors,
taxpayers, is an irresponsible discourse which often even appeals to certain characteristics of nationalities which are extremely negative.
I therefore appeal, and only appeal, to the sense of responsibility of the leaders of the European governments to find solutions that lead Europe to an exit and a solution.

Madam President, Commissioner, my first words are to praise the work done by the rapporteur, Mr Georgios Stravakakis, who has done an extraordinary job in identifying future synergies for greater effectiveness between the various structural funds.

Mr President, this report is also part of this joint debate on cohesion policy, a debate that is of greater importance, not only because of the substance of the subject, but also, and above all, because of the opportunity, in terms of the timing of its discussion.
Indeed, we are currently awaiting the Commission's proposal, not only on the new financial perspectives, but also on the future cohesion policy after 2013.
The cohesion policy is a fundamental policy for the European Union.

It is a horizontal policy which, through its main instrument, the Structural Funds, finances projects and programmes in the Member States, seeking to reduce disparities between the development levels of the various regions.
Experience has, however, shown us the difficulties in terms of efficiency and results resulting from the fragmentation and complexity of this policy.

I therefore believe that a single strategic framework for the various funds, with an integrated, results-oriented approach, is the starting point for greater and better synergies between the various instruments and for enhancing their effectiveness.
It is also essential for better coordination between the various sectoral policies and for better efficiency in the use of resources.
We must use this time of crisis to look for new alternatives.

It is true that all these reports that we are discussing today will pose enormous challenges, and we know that to meet these challenges we also need a good budget, but we need to make a clear distinction between budgetary aspects and political objectives.
The political objectives are well known, and it is only by affirming these political objectives that we will be able to offer a future to the regions.
If that is the case, we must then put the budget at the service of the future of the regions and of the citizens, and not the citizens and the regions at the service of the budget.
Mr President, cohesion policy is the key policy for the future of the European project.
The construction of a common space, with an internal market and a common currency, requires a certain level of convergence among its Member States and their regions in order to function.
The current competitiveness gaps and development imbalances have inevitably weakened the European space and led to the crisis affecting the euro area.
That is why this vital need for convergence absolutely requires a powerful cohesion budget that cannot be weakened to finance other policies.
The European Union must have more if it is to do more, and this can only be achieved by increasing its budget, not by reducing it, even if only slightly, as is the case with the cohesion budget.

I therefore ask the Commissioner whether the creation of the infrastructure fund and the new innovation policies, which will be linked to cohesion and regional development, which I support, will not become a Trojan horse in the cohesion budget without an increase in the Community budget.

Modernisation and renewal of energy infrastructure are essential factors for the objectives of smart, sustainable growth that we are pursuing under the Europe 2020 strategy and for the energy security that we need.
It is essential that all regions are involved in this challenge.

To this end, the incentives provided by regulatory systems need to be improved, particularly those applicable to higher-risk projects and more innovative projects, such as those being developed in the Azores islands in the field of renewable energies.

Regions should already be involved in the stages of project approval and implementation, in setting priorities and in their cooperation and execution, because this ensures better results and, therefore, better defence of European interests.
The current economic situation further accentuates the need to adopt an innovative and integrated approach to energy issues, taking into account all economic, environmental and social aspects.
It is essential not to lose sight of its medium- and long-term effects, while trying to ensure that all European citizens have access to secure, sustainable energy at reasonable prices.

Madam President, the report we are voting on is of particular importance, bearing in mind the influence that social services of general interest have on the daily lives of European citizens, as well as on social cohesion, job creation and economic growth.

The collaboration with the rapporteur, Mr De Rossa, was very beneficial and clarifying, as he himself has just said, allowing several compromises to be adopted in the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs.
I congratulate and compliment the rapporteur on the result we have here today and are debating.
In fact, our main concern was to make it clear that the powers in this area belong to the Member States.
The national authorities are responsible for defining, organising and financing social services of general interest.
Therefore, it is unnecessary to create harmonising legislation at European level, as the realities of these general interest social services are very diverse in the 27 Member States.

Just look at health services, social security, employment and social housing, which are examples where proximity and knowledge of the context are the cornerstone of the adequate and fair delivery of these services.

Mr President, as rapporteur for the opinion of the Committee on Regional Development on the future of transnational social services, I would like to congratulate Mr De Rossa, highlighting the fact that we are dealing with a pillar essential to the social market economy model that we defend for Europe, and in which the Cedefop plays a fundamental role in its cohesion.

Social cohesion at European Union level recommends not overlooking the existence of minimum protection levels in all Member States, without prejudice to the respect of the competences of their national, regional and local authorities.

European regions play a very important role in providing these services to the public, and their opinion should be taken into account, given the nature of their constraints, in order to guarantee that the services are provided.

At a time of crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing, the public services of the social sector constitute a social safety net for the most disadvantaged or for those whose income does not allow them to access private services.

It is therefore important to ensure that Member States that decentralise powers do so with the appropriate financial resources, so that regional and local authorities can provide a good service to the population.
Baroness Ashton, I would like to begin by saying that it is obvious that we must continue to support the democratic revolutions taking place in these countries.
I do not have much time, so I just want to make a few points.
Firstly, we must not forget that the conditions for negotiation, with a view to democratic transition rather than civil war, must be defined by the peoples of these countries.
The situation in Syria is serious, and this applies to Syria, but also to Yemen and Bahrain.

If we want to avoid civil war, the last thing these countries need is for Europe to meddle in their internal affairs, particularly and above all through the presentation of military or paramilitary solutions.
I say this because we must totally repudiate what is happening in Libya.
NATO and the United Nations have gone beyond all the resolutions, even those adopted by the United Nations Security Council.
It is this silence on Libya, this repudiation that we must make, and it is because it is not included in this resolution that I cannot support it.

Mr President, President of the Commission, Prime Minister, first of all, I would like to congratulate the Polish Presidency on the ambitious programme it has brought here and, in particular, on the fact that it has brought the debate on the future of the European Union to the fore.

neighbourhood policy, not only to the east, but also on the southern border, where I think we are facing a challenge that is perhaps more demanding than the one we have to the east, which is more long-term and less short-term.
That said, I would like to ask the Prime Minister and the Polish Presidency to pay particular attention to economic integration policies, economic governance and the supervision of financial markets.
I think that, at this time, no matter how ambitious our goals are in terms of defence, energy, food security, the environment, agriculture and the multiannual financial framework, if we do not

if we do not resolve the problem of the debt crisis in Europe in a clear and solid way in the face of the global markets, we will not have a Europe that is strong enough to deal with all the challenges that the Polish Presidency has set itself.

That is why I drew attention to the fact that it is essential to convince the remaining Heads of State or Government that more economic integration and more economic governance in the European Union are needed.

Without that, what will happen is that instead of being the drivers or, at least, the representatives of our peoples, whether in the Council, in Parliament or in the Commission, in the interest of Europe, we will be nothing more than
Mr President, as Chair of the Delegation to the ACP-EU Joint Parliamentary Assembly, I would like to say a few words about each of my four countries.
Starting with Syria, and reiterating what some of my fellow Members have already said, I regret that the international community seems to have double standards in the eyes of public opinion.

We need to denounce, if necessary, the countries in the Security Council – and I am obviously referring to Russia and China, as well as Brazil – which have the right of veto and have prevented the international community from taking a position more in line with the values we share.
With regard to Egypt, I think there is an urgent need to raise awareness among the Egyptian authorities of the need to postpone the elections, as has already been done in Tunisia.
As for Lebanon, I believe that a very clear message must emerge from this debate for the new Lebanese Government.
to make them aware that they will be judged on the basis of their actions in relation to the charges brought by the Special Tribunal for Lebanon.
Jordan is an example of how a peaceful transition is possible and at this stage, we perhaps only need to raise awareness and encourage the authorities to speed up the reform process.
We cannot forget, obviously, in a debate of this kind, the peace process in the Middle East, and we reiterate our hope that the meeting of the Quartet next Monday will be able to
actually be productive, and to reiterate that this House would certainly like to see much greater EU involvement in the whole process.

I have no doubt that there will be a new round of negotiations before the United Nations General Assembly in September, but I hope that this round of negotiations will be productive and conducted in good faith.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur and all those who contributed to this work.
There is no doubt that this report will be a reference point for recommendations and for solid, coordinated responses to the challenges of the crisis.
If there is one conclusion that can be drawn, it is that the crisis we are experiencing will only be overcome through deeper European integration.

In times of serious financial, economic and social difficulties and painful austerity measures in some Member States, such as my country, Portugal, there is a temptation to take refuge in protectionist and nationalist attitudes.
But this is a mistake.

The European response to the crisis must involve genuine European economic governance, which pursues sustainable growth, promotes job creation and develops the social market economy, providing long-term answers with defined objectives with which the Member States can identify.
The strategy for exiting the crisis needs to be financed with a sufficiently ambitious EU budget.
In order to avoid a new sovereign debt and euro crisis, we need closer coordination of budgetary policies, as well as the creation of a system for issuing public debt and eurobonds.
I will conclude by stressing that, in this difficult period, it is also necessary to maintain social protection levels, especially for our most vulnerable citizens.
Mr President, Mr President-in-Office of the Council, Commissioner, it is usual to say that crises have helped to strengthen Europe.
This has been the case historically, but today I am not so sure that this is still true.

The slow, improvised, reactive way in which the Union is reacting to the Greek crisis worsens the situation every day and spreads contagion and lack of confidence in the Union and its currency in a frightening way.
This, despite the problems in the euro area being due to obvious shortcomings in the construction of the single currency, which today are concentrated in its weakest links.

In order for the current crisis to result in a stronger Union, we need a strategic vision, as we had in the past, and which, unfortunately, neither the Commission nor the Council has been able to give to the European programme and project.
This Parliament report gives us this vision, however: more Europe and not less is the solution.
Parliament recalls that the single market and the single currency generate intolerable divergences between economies, which must be countered by specific policies.

It recognises that the euro requires a reinforced European budget, financed by own resources, and a European debt issuance and solidarity agency, capable of protecting Member States from market speculation.

to provide them with credit at reasonable prices and within reasonable timeframes. It also recognises that diplomas and the transferability of workers between countries require mutual recognition of social rights and pensions.
Parliament has united to send out clear messages. Let us hope that the Commission and the Council will listen.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear rapporteur, first of all, I would like to congratulate and congratulate the rapporteur, because I believe that this report is extremely useful and will allow us to take new steps in the area of mediation.
The report provides a broad overview, a range of solutions adopted by the various Member States in implementing the
Directive 2008 / 52 / EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 May 2008 on mediation in civil and commercial matters.
We are all aware of the advantages of mediation.
On the one hand, it is an extra-judicial method of resolving disputes, it contributes to the decongestion of courts, to the rationalisation of the justice system, while at the same time providing
providing the parties with the possibility of obtaining an amicable solution that is faster and less costly, less burdensome than the litigation in which they are involved.

It is therefore crucial to reiterate the objective of Article 1 of the directive, which calls for the adoption of incentive measures to encourage the use of alternative dispute resolution methods, and in particular mediation.
Promoting access to and use of out-of-court dispute resolution mechanisms contributes to improving, simplifying and making our justice more accessible.

This is therefore a very important challenge for the European Union and for the establishment of an area of freedom, security and justice, and I would say for the idea of the rule of law within the European Union.
That is why, in this context of crisis, I also believe it is very important that, as the report says, we raise public awareness of the use of operators
judicial cooperation in this area, and I would like to call on the Commission and Commissioner Reding to present a legislative proposal on alternative dispute resolution as soon as possible.
Mr President, Commissioner, I would also like to stress the fundamental importance of this report on transparency in the European energy market.
I would just like to say that this is a central area of cooperation at European level, if we safeguard some of the fundamental aspects, including avoiding
liberalisation does not result in higher energy prices for citizens, guarantee equal access and be careful in terms of penalties, as has already been mentioned by numerous colleagues.

And precisely because this is a central area of European cooperation, Mr President, Mr Commissioner, I cannot fail to mention it and to question it and to ask you, straight in the eye, Mr Commissioner,
I would like to take the opportunity to ask you to explain your recent statements on Greece, Ireland and Portugal, because these are also matters of European cooperation.
Symbolic measures, such as flying flags at half-mast, Commissioner, only serve to increase the shame felt by citizens who are worthy and who are fighting for a better Europe.

Mr President, Commissioner Šefčovič, representatives of the Council, the Polish Presidency, first of all, I would like to make it clear that I am speaking here in a dual capacity: on the one hand, replacing the author of the opinion of the Committee on Constitutional Affairs,

constitutional affairs, my colleague Mr Schmidt from Denmark, who is absent because the election process is taking place in Denmark at the moment, and so I am representing him as a member of the AFCO Committee, but also as a member of the JURI Committee, as shadow rapporteur in the JURI Committee on this report.

The first point I would like to make is that, in the crisis we are in, in the economic and financial crisis, the issue of intelligent regulation, of smart regulation, of better legislation, is a central issue.
There is no doubt that, in the so-called structural reforms that can kick-start the European area 's economy and finances, improving legislation and regulation is also absolutely essential.

It is clear that a Europe that wants to be dynamic and competitive on the one hand must have regulations that are simple and accessible to businesses and also, obviously, to citizens, and therefore must be oriented towards their needs.
The truth is that, in this respect, it is essential today that, first of all, the programme to reduce administrative burdens be achieved, that all efforts be made to

to promote consultations with market operators, with associations representing citizens and businesses, and to carry out impact assessments of the legislation itself, which is also extremely important, so that the

decision-making process can essentially be based on the opinion of those who are the recipients of the legislation and can be carried out without administrative burdens and with lower economic and financial costs.

It is important to say that, for the Committee on Constitutional Affairs in particular, the idea of the principle of proportionality and subsidiarity is relevant: the idea that European intervention should be appropriate and not promote the complexification of the system.

legal and regulatory framework. And so, in our opinion, we pay particular attention to the enhanced role of national parliaments in this process, precisely because of the principle of proportionality and the principle of subsidiarity.

That is why it is essential that, at the level of smart regulation and at the level of improving legislation, we now involve national parliaments, so that legislation and regulation are both at European level and at national level.

be more agile, more intelligent and more efficient. This is therefore a good time to put into practice what we announce every day, which is a more competitive Europe and a more agile Europe through smarter regulation.
Mr President, the number of homeless people, as well as the number of people living below the poverty line, has been increasing in recent years in several Member States.
The economic, financial and social crisis and the rise in unemployment have made this problem worse, as everyone has already diagnosed.
We also all agree that this phenomenon is a denial of important European Union values: respect for human dignity and respect for fundamental rights.
In my country, for example, which is subject to a strong austerity measure, the concept of homeless, according to the national plan, includes people who go to ask for help to have a meal.
The number of people seeking help to eat has increased by more than 20% since 2008.
It is particularly high in families and disadvantaged people whose living conditions have been altered by unemployment and excessive bank debt.
This is just one example showing how serious this situation is.
Last year, the European Parliament adopted a written declaration on a European Union strategy for the homeless.

With this declaration, Parliament has drawn attention to the need for the European Union to adopt a strategy supported by national and regional strategies with the long-term objective of ending situations of people sleeping rough and long-term homelessness.

National strategies should focus on preventive measures, but also on concrete actions to improve the quality of services provided to the homeless and the availability of accommodation at affordable prices.
It is also clear that this European strategy must be compatible with the social housing policies of the Member States.
We have taken good note of the Commissioner ’ s words and we have registered the point he has made.
I will conclude by urging the European Commission to give a strong impetus to coordinating the action of Member States and regional authorities in combating this social injustice.
Mr President, we are debating the peace process in the Middle East, but is there some process underway that I have missed?
Unfortunately, no.

The European Union has repeatedly spoken, as has the majority of the international community, in favour of two peoples and two states sharing the space between the River Jordan and the Mediterranean, who have the right to aspire to live in peace and security.
However, without dialogue between the parties, we are stuck in a sea of hatred and misunderstandings that has been going on for 63 years, and this only feeds extremism and frustration.

It is essential that Europe and the Arab League become much more involved in seeking a negotiated solution to the borders, based on those existing in 1967, with exchanges that will allow the majority of the Jewish settlements to remain in Israel.

that the issue of Jerusalem, the holy city, the future capital of the two states, be clarified and mutually accepted as a symbol of peaceful coexistence between two peoples who have the duty to fulfil the prophecy of Isaiah that it is the land of milk and honey.

that all EU countries participate in resolving the issue of Palestinian refugees and that the international community, and in particular the Arab nations, participate in the necessary financial compensation.

It is also absolutely essential that a military force under the aegis of the United Nations, with the participation of American and Islamic forces among others, be deployed on the ground as a guarantor of peace.
Let us therefore have the courage and the political will.
Mr President, Commissioner, tourism needs a clear, coordinated European approach, with a close eye on the potential and challenges offered by the different types of tourism.
The tourist diversity of the European Union is so rich that it allows for exponential growth in this sector and in the competitiveness of the European Union itself.

Investing in this key sector, which represents 10% of the EU's GDP and around 12% of employment in the European Union, will not only create European added value, but also help us to deal with the economic crisis we are experiencing.
I come from an ultra-peripheral region, Madeira, where tourism is the main economic activity.

Therefore, the creation of a specific heading for the sector in the 2014-2020 financial framework is essential for the sustainable and cohesive growth of tourism, which is made up essentially of small and medium-sized enterprises.

The achievement of a genuine European policy for tourism that is sustainable and diversified requires a concrete strategic plan, with a clearly defined schedule, actions and funding, capable of enhancing the visibility and quality of the tourism sector in the European Union.
Mr President, the absence of an ongoing peace process is jeopardising basic rights and has led to unilateral measures being taken by the parties, which are not
not contributed in any way to easing tensions in the region, let alone to the effective search for a life for the two peoples who have the right to live in harmony, in peace, security and dignity.

It is time for the international community to say 'enough is enough'. It is time for Europe and the Arab League to drive the search for a lasting solution, sensibly, presenting viable solutions without delay and with deadlines.
It is clear that the United States and Russia are no longer part of the solution.
Let us start with Jerusalem: why not recover the original idea of a separate corpus separatum administered by the United Nations and which obviously functions as the capital of both States?
Borders: let us start with those of 1967.
Land parcels should be swapped where possible and logical.
Take care of the defence of Israel, an issue obviously vital for the Jewish people, for example by placing 'blue helmets' with Americans, Europeans and Islamic forces along the Jordan.
There are about 4.8 million refugees, and they have the right to their dignity and to a future linked to a land, preferably their own.

But it is not exclusively an Israeli or Palestinian problem: it is also ours, the problem of the neighbouring Arab countries, of the Arab community in general, but also of Europe and the rest of the international community.
Colonialism must end and its expansion must be brought to a halt, and this is non-negotiable.
The announcement made today is shameful and shows hypocrisy and bad faith in announcing its willingness to enter into immediate negotiations.

Above all, we want good-faith, committed negotiations with a deadline for their conclusion, after which the international community, in order to maintain its credibility, will have to adopt a different attitude.

Madam President, Commissioner, the shameful situation in the refugee camps in Tunisia has already been mentioned, and we all share it, and I very much agree with what my fellow Members who have just spoken have said.
I will not refer to that, but rather to another situation that is more overlooked, namely, that of the citizens who were taken in by families in Tunisia.

This is one of the situations that should make us even more ashamed, because Tunisia, in a situation of crisis, knows how to be solidary, whereas Europe does not know how to be solidary and is not in a transition process towards democracy, and does not know how to be solidary, does not know how to receive.
I think it is truly shameful that we can count the number of refugees received in Europe in hundreds.
Many of these people, contrary to what has been said, cannot go back to their homes.
There are children who have enrolled in public schools, which has implications in terms of infrastructure and in terms of reception in these countries.
That is why, Madam Commissioner, I am very sensitive to your appeal, and I am not saying this ironically, I am saying it seriously, but please, the Commission,
And Europe?
What can you do?
How is it that Europe is so happy with this double standard?
We are so quick to repatriate and so slow to help, Commissioner.
This cannot go on.

Mr President, I would like to congratulate you on this boost of hope that you have given to all Members of the European Parliament and, in particular, to the citizens of the European Union whom we represent here.
That said, I would like to say that we now expect the Commission to take very, very determined action with the Council to resolve this sovereign debt crisis in the short term.
