We know that it is a marathon, but immediate signals are needed.

Therefore, without prejudice to our all starting to think about a treaty amendment that would give political expression to the whole project that we have brought here today, we need to do something now.

This set of measures and, possibly, an agreement between the Member States, what I have called a kind of social pact, which goes beyond the Treaties and which could, from now on, put all these ideas into practice.
Many congratulations on your courage and determination.
I believe we are on the right track and we leave here today with another determination.

Mr President, the crisis that the European Union is going through has had serious consequences in all Member States, and thousands of workers have been affected by dismissals caused by globalisation and the crisis.
We cannot fail to express our solidarity with these workers who have lost their jobs and to affirm that Europe should help Member States to maintain employment levels and combat unemployment.
A rapid reintegration of workers into the labour market is a priority objective.

An example of the usefulness of this fund is my country, Portugal, which has used it four times, although we are aware that access to this fund could be improved, especially in the countries most affected by the crisis.

Thanks to the existence of this Globalisation Adjustment Fund, it has been possible to help thousands of workers through measures such as vocational training, help with setting up businesses and incentives for recruitment.
This proposal is positive because it will allow Member States to request the intervention of this fund to support workers dismissed as a direct consequence of the economic and financial crisis,
We support the extension of the deadline and believe that the Member States most affected by the crisis should have greater and easier access to this Globalisation Adjustment Fund.
Mr President, I would like to greet the Polish Presidency and also Commissioner Rehn. I just want to say that I believe that the

Today, in the sequence and in the development of what was done in the State of the Union address, a very, very big step was taken towards being able to solve the problems of the crisis in the euro area.
because the Commission has here assumed its commitment to use its right of initiative and to work with the Council to resolve this crisis.
I believe that this is an extremely important step, and it is the only way that we have at this moment of resolving the crisis in the euro area.
There is no possibility of solving this in the short term with intergovernmental approximation alone, and therefore it is necessary that the states, the governments of the states, give
the Commission the tools and the mandate to lead this process of resolution, to implement, execute and develop this roadmap that you have presented here.
However, I would like to say that this is not incompatible with our developing, at the same time, our ability to amend the Treaties.
I am also convinced that, in the medium term, we need to amend the Treaties, but even then we must do so in a way that strengthens the Commission, giving it more power and a better position.

It is through strengthening the Community method, now in the short term and then through a change in the Treaties in the long term, that we will achieve greater integration and a solution for the 17 countries in the euro area and for the 27 countries in the European Union.

Mr President, Mr Barroso came here today to announce that one of the measures will be that banks that do not have the capital requirements must submit plans to obtain them and, if they are unable to do so, they must then suspend their dividends and bonuses.
I could not fail to agree with the presentation made by the President.
This week we saw what happened with Dexia Bank and, a few months ago, an administrator of this bank received a EUR 600 000 prize for his wonderful management, as we know.
But, seriously, Mr President, the Commission has to go to the governments and present the complete package, and the complete package is what has the essentials.
The main thing is that taxpayers' money should be matched by a credit policy for growth.
The main thing is that banks start paying taxes on their profits, which does not happen in many countries, particularly in my country, Portugal.
In two years, no, in three years, this is the second time that we have been asked to save the banks with taxpayers ' money and the unemployed ' money.
The banks that save also save the economy, helping to create jobs.
Madam President, Baroness Ashton, there are times when I think we need to stop and take stock, and this seems to be one of those times.
And I think this is because we are always running behind events, and responding to events one after the other, and we lose sight of the big picture.

It is true that each case is a case and each country we are talking about here is a specific case, but, if you will allow me to say so, Baroness Ashton, it seems to me that it is difficult to scrutinise what criteria the European Union has used to act in each of these cases.
I fear that we are often held hostage by the interests of the countries of the European Union, by some of these countries, not all of them.
That is why we use so often in this House the expression 'double standards', and we continue to use it.

Sometimes it is sanctions, sometimes it is not sanctions; sometimes interference goes as far as bombing, as in the case of Libya, and sometimes it is total silence, as in the case of the invasion of Bahrain by the forces of

Saudi Arabia. Baroness Ashton, let me tell you, I have studied all the files carefully and I have not yet seen a single declaration by the European institutions condemning the invasion of Bahrain by the Saudi military forces.
Death sentences, endless violence. Doctors and nurses are sentenced to up to 30 years in prison by military courts for supposedly being against the regime and not helping the injured who needed them.
With regard to the dictatorial monarchy of Bahrain, with regard to the dictatorial regime of Yemen, the Saleh regime, we have had much more silence than action.

I am not in favour of interference, I never have been, but I am afraid that we are putting ourselves in an uncomfortable position, a position seen from the outside, Baroness, which is that instead of helping them to develop, to achieve their aspirations,

democratic and revolutionary peoples in the Arab countries, instead of helping the Arab Spring, what we are doing is preventing it from spreading, either by excess or by default, and I do not think we should carry this sin.
I hope that awarding the Nobel Prize to a H'mong activist can be a sign that our side is the side of the peoples who are in revolt. Thank you very much.
Madam President, Commissioner, this is a good report by Mr Kazak.
First of all, we have to ask why we should revise VAT in the European Union.
Firstly, there is a need to make it compatible with the single market.
The more regimes and varieties there are, the less fluid the market will be.
A VAT reform that favours e-commerce will focus competition on the value and base price of goods and services, not on tax differences.
It combats fraud and ensures the confidence that is so necessary, particularly in cross-border transactions, improves the economy of SMEs and reduces administrative burdens.
What other supporting measures are already under way in the single market that could be associated with VAT?
Firstly, a simple, clear and transparent digital support mechanism, in line with the Single Market initiative.
Measure five on services, the need to revise legislation, on standardisation and the implementation of the services directive.
Measure nine aims to remove fiscal obstacles and explicitly review the VAT system and VAT equalisation in online and face-to-face trade, which we all feel when ordering books.
There are two fundamental reasons for the pressure to reform VAT.
Firstly, the economic crisis.
More revenue is needed, including probably in some countries a reduction in social security contributions to promote economic growth, shifting the burden to VAT.

Secondly, the second major source of pressure is population ageing, demographic ageing, and the trend towards moving from indirect taxes to direct taxes, due to the reduction in the population base on which they are levied.
We need to reconsider the balance between direct and indirect taxation.
Finally, in these reforms, we have to reconcile subsidiarity with harmonisation.
The range of variation in the standard VAT rate needs to be restricted so that we can all understand each other better.
Greater transparency is needed in the definition of reduced VAT rates and their scope, with a clear and widely publicised list of reduced rates.
We need to study thresholds for exemptions for SMEs, knowing full well that every exemption brings with it a risk of small or large fraud.
Finally, I would like to welcome the Commission's recent proposal to create a European VAT.
Madam President, I would like to thank Ms Rühle for the excellent report she has prepared.

The advantages of public procurement are certainly well known, but the obstacles are also evident: conflicts of interest between European legislation and the objectives of Member States harm competitiveness.
The dichotomy between quality and prices, and the complexity of the laws and procedures, are evident.
It is essential to look at existing best practices, particularly in Portugal, where electronic procurement has worked well and has facilitated public procurement.
Efficient European public procurement is a privileged way of controlling the public deficit and achieving more growth.
It will need a stable and predictable legal framework, less bureaucratic procedures to attract SMEs and to defend social and environmental values.

That is why I welcome the intentions that Commissioner Barnier has presented to us here for the revision of public procurement, which is still this year one of the twelve priorities of the Single Market Act.
I would like to congratulate Mrs Rühle and all those who collaborated with her.
An important issue in the field of public procurement is electronic public procurement.
Unfortunately, the objectives proposed by the European Union of 50% of adjudications by electronic means by 2010 failed, with average values of only 5%.
Only a few countries, such as my own, Portugal, have been successful in this area.

The advantages of electronic public procurement are obvious and have already been mentioned here: it allows the replacement of costly access procedures with a simple consultation of the electronic platform, in other words, it greatly reduces costs for businesses, especially for those

small and medium-sized enterprises, the publication on the internet and the ease of accessing the documents, which increases the number of suppliers, thereby increasing competition and enabling better purchasing, makes the award of public contracts

easier and more flexible, simplifies access to European Union public contracts, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises, thus facilitating the participation of these important economic drivers in the public procurement market.
In this respect, we welcome and encourage the European Commission to strengthen the mechanisms to make electronic public procurement an increasingly widespread reality.

We are living through decisive hours for the future of Europe, and although the situation has rapidly become, for everyone to see, not a problem for the Greeks or the Portuguese but a major European problem, the truth is that the solutions proposed and their delay have not produced any effect in halting the rapid deterioration of the situation.

The search for solutions aimed at individual Member States, rather than truly European solutions, has allowed markets to continue speculating on sovereign debt and has imposed deadlines and timetables
absolutely impossible goals that some Member States are trying to achieve with measures of a brutality previously unimaginable, applied to people, companies and the public sector.

A common solution, already found for the risk of European sovereign debt, is absolutely essential, as it would remove pressure from the markets and speculation and give time for the necessary adjustments to be made.
Without this, what we will end up with are countries that will continue to have no money to pay their creditors, economies in ruins and a great deal of suffering.

I would like to thank my fellow Members, the shadow rapporteurs, with whom I have had a fruitful collaboration, and also the rapporteurs for opinion of the four committees involved. These opinions are a valuable contribution to this report.
We received 400 amendments and drafted 47 compromises, all of which were adopted.
This shows the importance and interest generated by this issue of new skills and jobs.
Ultimately, it is a question of preparing Europe ’ s human resources, anticipating the needs of the labour market in the next decade.
The key strategy in drawing up the report was to rephrase common ideas to avoid repetition and ensure the report ' s coherence.
I think that this objective has been achieved.
We all know that the crisis currently affecting us has highlighted the fragilities of the European economic and social model, as well as cancelling out several of the advances made in the past.
Since the first effects of the crisis began to be felt in the labour markets in 2008, Europe has lost more than five million jobs.
Today, almost 23 million people are employed.
Youth unemployment is even more alarming: the youth unemployment rate is close to 21% and is more than double the general unemployment rate.
In all Member States, unemployment rates vary according to levels of qualification.
This means that people with higher qualifications find jobs more easily.
We also know that in the near future, the needs in terms of aptitudes, skills and qualifications will increase in all types and levels of profession.
In this context, Europe faces four challenges: first, job creation; second, the need to develop and remain competitive;
then the sustainability of social security systems and finally the difficulty in finding people with the right skills to fill job vacancies.
This report has a dual objective: first, to underline the need to act quickly to close the skills gaps in Europe; second, to better anticipate the skills needs of the labour market.

To this end, several measures are highlighted. First, in order to ensure the availability of skilled labour, it is necessary to improve basic skills, as well as skills in science, innovation, information and communication technologies and foreign languages.
As I have already said, skills needs must be identified early on, over a time horizon of at least ten years.
We now know the potential of a new sustainable economy, the green economy, and the need to invest in ecological skills.
Then, participation in lifelong learning and in professional retraining must be increased, and school drop-out reduced.
Another important matter is the mutual recognition of diplomas and professional qualifications and the urgency of moving forward with this recognition.
Fourthly, we have already drawn attention to the link that must exist between educational establishments and the needs of the labour market.
The internships must be of high quality and supervised by mentors, so as to lead to employment and put an end to any abuse of interns' situation.
The role of social partners and social dialogue is also highlighted.
The report also proposes measures to promote job creation and job seeking.
We have the future ahead of us and we need to create jobs.
SMEs play a decisive role in achieving this objective.

The single market also needs to be strengthened in order to facilitate the free movement of workers, and therefore there is a need to coordinate social security systems so that workers can have their acquired rights guaranteed, especially the portability of pensions.

Finally, the issue of labour market flexibility: in order to improve the functioning of the labour market, the rules of flexicurity must be adapted to the socio-economic contexts of each Member State.
Madam President, I would like to start by thanking all the Members who have taken part in this lively debate.
All the colleagues made very useful speeches and contributed to a very constructive discussion on an ambitious agenda at a time when we are experiencing a serious crisis.

Indeed, this debate is taking place on the eve of a European Council that will show whether or not there is political will to overcome the difficulties we are experiencing and whether or not there is political will
policy for a deepening of our economic coordination and whether there is really a harmonised and united will to carry forward the ambition that is in this agenda, that is, to have

new jobs in the next decade, new jobs that will give hope to young people who are currently unemployed and to those who are being trained, hope in the labour market, hope in an inclusive labour market that will respond to their needs.

ambition of dignity through work and that they also have the opportunity to be in a permanent dynamism to meet the challenges of a globalised economy and with a dynamic that has no parallel in recent generations.
Our ambition is therefore to create jobs and economic growth, not only to respond to those who are unemployed as a result of the crisis, but also to those who will enter the labour market.

response to the major challenges that are out there, such as the green economy, health services, transport and accommodation services, which require all of us to have a different, intelligent and inclusive vision of the labour market.
Thank you all.

Madam President, what we have been told today in the Council statement is that, for technical reasons, democracy would have to be sacrificed in the European Semester. But what we are talking about is a political issue, and the question I am asking is whether political coordination can exist without democracy.
Where are the European Parliament's powers in the end?
Where is the scrutiny by national parliaments of their major budgetary choices?

However, if this were a technical issue, as has been said here, then we are talking about a variable technique, because we know that the Council substantially eased the conditions for the four countries on the waiting list: France, Italy, Hungary and Spain. It did not do the same for the countries that are being rescued.
So, this is the model of intergovernmentalism that allows Germany to give opinions on Portugal, but does not allow Portugal to give opinions on Germany.
Commissioner, please just pay attention to this: no democratic legitimacy problem is solved by asking Members to go and consult information on the Internet.
This is a problem of false inclusion, not a problem of democratic exclusion, which is what we are talking about here. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr Daerden, on the quality and timeliness of this report, and also Commissioner Andor for the initiatives announced.
Poverty is a very serious problem that requires effective and rapid action to halt its increase, which currently affects more than 80 million Europeans.
The serious crisis we are going through has made this situation worse and has made the times in which we live dramatic, as Commissioner Andor said.
The number of unemployed people continues to rise, and we all know that lack of employment is a passport to poverty and social exclusion.
This situation requires resources, will and a great deal of effort from the European Commission and the Member States.
If this does not happen, the objective of taking 20 million Europeans out of poverty during this decade will not be achieved.
I therefore warmly welcome the initiative of the Platform against Poverty, which is a very important instrument for ensuring social justice.
However, no one doubts that the best antidotes to poverty and social exclusion are job creation and the promotion of economic growth.
Mr President, access to the Internet should today be considered as a new fundamental right of citizens and not merely as a commercial object, because this right
essential things such as freedom of expression, access to and sharing of information, access to and sharing of culture and knowledge, as well as democratic and media pluralism.

That is why this access cannot be subject to speculative tariffs or to contracts that restrict free choice and the possibility of easily changing supplier, particularly in the world of mobile telecommunications, as we also know.
Active citizenship therefore requires that full and transparent information be provided.
Blocking the use of the Internet is not just the preserve of political regimes that are afraid of their people.
It may also happen as a result of economic choices, offering privileges to special clients and less egalitarian treatment to clients who are less profitable.
That is why I support Parliament in clearly standing by Internet neutrality and defending its total openness.
Let us be honest.
We have been taking medicine to get out of the crisis for three years now and the situation is worsening day by day.
Instead of healing, we have created two fatal vicious circles.
The first is between banks and states.
Today, it is easier to discuss the failure of a State than of a large bank, but we still expect banks to finance States and States to save their banks.
This spiral must be broken.
The Commission is trying to move forward with European legislation to protect taxpayers from bank crises.
It is also vital, however, to protect Member States ’ debt from attacks by speculators.

To this end, it is urgent and long overdue to prepare coordinated issues of sovereign debt, whether called Eurobonds or Stability Bonds, and guarantee them through robust instruments of a Community, rather than intergovernmental, nature.
In short, we also need to review the involvement of the European Central Bank.
But the second vicious circle results from the argument that some States are rich because they are virtuous, while others are poor because they have sinned.
This narrative is politically explosive and technically wrong.

It ignores the essential fact that a market and a single currency built by political choice and between unequal partners necessarily aggravates competitiveness imbalances between the centre and the periphery.
This is what needs to be tackled in economic governance.
No, Mr Van Rompuy, not through sanctions that promote the crushing of wages and incomes, with one country controlling another.
before, through involvement, Commissioner, of both the countries with surpluses and the countries with deficits, so as to make productivity converge through investment, education and science.
The extensions have only generated unemployment, disillusionment and a downward spiral from which we cannot escape.

Madam President, Commissioner, integrated maritime policy advocates a coordinated approach to the management and governance of oceans, seas and coasts, from a global and coherent perspective of all European policies related to the sea.

A global perspective and an integrated approach require interaction between European policies linked to the sea and demand better governance through greater involvement in the European decision-making process.
The success and relevance of this policy have been demonstrated over recent years through the various initiatives financed in the form of preparatory actions and pilot projects.
The integrated maritime policy should therefore continue to benefit from adequate funding in the coming years of the current financial programming period.
In this respect, I support adequate funding until 2013, not only to consolidate previous projects, but also to help define a stable framework for the objectives of this programme.
Issues of policy, governance, sustainability and maritime surveillance are, for obvious reasons, issues of particular interest to certain regions of the European Union, such as the outermost regions.

The outermost regions, with the exception of French Guiana, are small and isolated, with a limited number of economic activities, and in which maritime and fishing activities are particularly important.
Moreover, the outermost regions contribute to the European Union having the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.

The outermost regions represent more than a third of the EU's exclusive economic zone, about 15 to 25 million square kilometres, and together with coastal regions, are fundamental to the economic growth of the EU and its Member States.

A programme such as the one provided by the Integrated Maritime Policy should benefit these regions, giving priority to transnational coordination of maritime policies and relevant interregional cooperation.
It is therefore necessary to guarantee continuous and sufficient funding, not only for the next two years, but also and above all for the next financial framework from 2014.
not to take the opportunity, when we talk about integrated maritime policy, to highlight the enormous potential that the Atlantic and island dimension holds for the European Union.

The Atlantic, as the maritime border of Western Europe, also links it to North and South America and to Africa, giving Europe a strategic dimension that is essential in the framework of a maritime policy drawn up with an eye to the future.

It is with the future in mind that the integrated maritime policy, in addition to promoting the coordination of maritime activities, should place at the top of its priorities the three-dimensional potential of the Atlantic, based on science and technology.

The exploration of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, the deep sea and the seabed constitutes a new frontier whose potential lies in biotechnology and mineral resources, and which is of considerable value to the knowledge economy.
The Union should therefore take advantage of the Azores ’ privileged position and the excellence of its oceanographic department to have a deep-sea observatory installed there.
Enough of this path.
When the diagnosis is wrong, the remedies do not work.
In the case of the euro area, the proposed remedies have led us to the brink of death.
The successive miraculous solutions that have been proposed have also been equally discredited by the markets.
the Financial Stabilisation Fund, successive tinkering with the Stability and Growth Pact, the revenues from the Troika in the countries under the programme.
Blind discipline without growth has generated a recessionary wave among economies that are totally interlinked and which have therefore contaminated each other with bankruptcies and unemployment.
Governments are falling one after the other at the hands of the markets, that is, of the rating agencies and speculators, and solvent countries are turning into insolvent ones.

Changing the Treaties now is just another step on this disastrous path, with an aggravating factor: we risk destroying what remains of national sovereignty by putting countries in the hands of the European Court of Justice.
No, we do not need to amend the Treaties.
In this emergency situation we are in, we need to take two decisions.
Firstly, we need to set out a real agenda for growth and sustainable cohesion.
Where is the 2020 agenda?
Where is the recovery budget?
Where are those projects, those 'project bonds', for Europe's structural investments?
Secondly, we need to protect sovereign debt.
Naturally, eurobonds are necessary, but in this emergency situation we are in, it is urgent that the European Central Bank directly or indirectly defend sovereign debt against speculators.
not in a shamefaced way, but with a firm, definite, resolute position, as befits any central bank defending its currency and the economy that supports it.
This does not require treaty changes; it requires political will and European conviction.
We will move the Treaty when we have drawn all the lessons from the present crisis, and there are many lessons to be drawn, but this is certainly not the time.
Mr President, Commissioner Dalli, the first Single Market Forum, an idea arising from the Grech report, took place in Krakow.
was rich in content and fruitful in the debate carried out by the Polish Presidency with European, national, regional and local authorities and civil society.
We have a roadmap drawn up in the Commission communication of 12 April with the twelve levers.
It needs to be passed into law, just like the strategy and the other 38 priorities identified in the Single Market Act.
For a market to exist, confidence is necessary.
Citizens' and consumers' confidence cannot be taken for granted, but needs to be encouraged and nurtured.

The single market is not just a buying and selling operation: it involves horizontal policies such as health, social protection and consumer protection, workers' rights, the environment and sustainable development.
The Single Market Forum should therefore become an emblematic event for the European Union, an annual event with a diversified and increasingly participative audience.

Mr President, I think we are at a point where we need to take stock of these crisis policies, these austerity policies, which have affected so many countries, and also the so-called structural reforms.
It is in this context that I think we cannot fail to see how they have generated so much social unrest.
It has generated social unrest not only in the countries with the greatest difficulties, in the southern countries, not only in the so-called PIIGS, but also in other contexts in Europe.
Just last week, Portugal experienced the biggest general strike ever, involving both the public and private sectors, a strike that took to the streets.

However, if we look at what happened today in the United Kingdom, we see that we are experiencing the biggest strike ever, especially if we take into account that people took to the streets, just as they did in Portugal.
We hope for a large demonstration in Brussels on Friday.
What this proves, Mr President, and what the strikes and people taking to the streets prove, is that the causes and problems we are facing are common, and that is why the reaction is common too.
The crisis in the European Union is spreading to all the Member States, reaching incredible proportions.

We are witnessing chaos in the financial markets and an increasing inability to provide the essential funding for the activity of our small and medium-sized enterprises, even those that are healthy, driving them to despair, bankruptcy and unemployment.
It is a spiral that feeds the recession and is destroying the economy and society.

Nobody denies the need for strict budgetary discipline, deep restructuring and better economic governance, but these are necessary conditions that are no longer sufficient in the short term to counteract the degradation currently taking place in the Union.

If it is true that we need policies to stimulate employment and growth, without which we will never be able to control sovereign debts, which are central today, we also need to guarantee the financing of our companies and our economies, without which the Union will collapse.
Therefore, more than ever, more than treaty revision, we need strong and secure intervention by the European Central Bank today.
Madam President, Commissioner, we are all here today united around a resolution with a set of very important requests in terms of intervention.
We are talking about World AIDS Day, and this assessment of the 2009 resolution and communication is fundamental.
It is essential, precisely because of the things that have already been mentioned,
We repeat them because, unfortunately, there is still room for repetition, which means that there is still much to be done.
There are many new cases diagnosed every year in Europe and in the European Economic Area: a 2010 report mentions more than 25 000 new cases.
If we consider neighbouring countries, we are talking about more than 100 000 new cases.
If we think that there are things that cannot get worse, they can always get worse.

One of the issues I would like to make very clear here has to do with the investment that has been made in the health sector as a result of the economic and financial crisis that we are currently experiencing.
This underinvestment has seriously affected areas that are fundamental to the fight against HIV/AIDS.
That is why, Commissioner, I believe that the Commission has a fundamental role to play in asking the Member States to fulfil their obligations in these areas.

A second issue is also linked to the increase in discrimination, which has also been highlighted by the economic and social crisis: discrimination against workers who have lost their jobs because they are importers of these goods.
That is why prevention, treatment and care should be equal for everyone, regardless of gender, age or region of residence.
I hope that we will be able to implement these measures with the support of the Commissioner.
Welcome to this Parliament, Mr Draghi.
We are living through special and dramatic circumstances, and they require determined and urgent measures.

The spiral between the debt of the states and the debt of the banks has to be broken, but at the moment, the priority is to defend the states, to ensure that the countries of the euro area and the euro itself are not destroyed by the rating agencies and by the speculators.
In the report that we will adopt, the Tremosa and Băsescu report, we have proposals to rethink the architecture of the euro area, to create eurobonds and a European treasury, but all this takes time.
At this moment, we are talking about an emergency and, in this context, the only solution is in the hands of the European Central Bank.
Technically, the European Central Bank knows what needs to be done.
Today, you should not be inhibited in your intervention and in your action by limited interpretations of your mandate.
Your mandate is to safeguard the euro, not to deal with inflation.
There is no doubt that, at a time like this, saving the euro and doing what needs to be done is what this Parliament and the citizens expect of the European Central Bank.
Good luck and courage, Mr Draghi.
European neighbourhood policy, the positions that the European Parliament has been taking were already clearly stated.
We were therefore very pleased to see a large number of the principles and proposals that we have been defending in this House included in the Commission and Council communications.
I would also like to thank the excellent and constant cooperation and coordination that we have enjoyed throughout this time with Vice-President Ashton and Commissioner Füle and their cabinets.
Firstly, because of the improvement in our relations with our neighbours.

By favouring relations with the general population and with civil society organisations in particular, we will give greater objectivity and greater effectiveness to our neighbourhood policy, which is intended to be based exclusively on the universal values that we defend and share.
I would like to stress, however, that the European Union does not intend to impose a pre-defined model or formula for political reforms in third countries.
However, when it comes to our taxpayers ’ money, we should give more to those who are doing more to bring us closer to the values we believe in.
This principle means that the faster a country progresses in its internal reforms, the more support it will receive from the European Union.
The 'more for more' principle is therefore correct, fair and consistent.
This obviously means that we also anticipate the possibility of there being cases or countries where we will have less for less.
Neighbourhood policy is now based on the defence of common values and on a commitment to democracy, human rights, the rule of law and good governance.
But this search involves greater responsibility.
Our partners are called upon to take more responsibility in the process. For this reason, we defend the principles of greater differentiation, greater conditionality and 'more for more'.
By adopting a bottom-up approach to our partners and by implementing tailor-made policies, we make development and institutional capacity more effective.

institutional building that we so much want to defend, and which lies at the heart of the rule of law and the democratic process, which we want to see implemented as quickly as possible in the societies, economies and communities of our neighbours.

The idea is to give the floor to citizens, as well as to all civil society movements that identify with our values, supporting organised movements that strive to achieve positive and democratic changes in their countries.
countries, improving the rights of women and children, building and deepening the principles of freedom, whether of expression, of association, of religious belief, of the press, of choice, etc.
etc., with fair elections, the fight against corruption, the rule of law, greater social justice, respect for minorities, environmental protection and efficient use of resources.
The serious economic and social problems faced by our neighbours also need to be addressed immediately.
It is clear that, without our concrete help, the democratic processes underway could fail.
These are the ideas that we have always defended for the future European youth policy.
These are the proposals in the report we are bringing to the vote.
Now it is time to act.
On our part, we will remain vigilant and exercise the new powers of monitoring and supervision now granted to the European Parliament.

Finally, I would like to thank my fellow rapporteur and friend, Marek Siwiec, to whom I would like to extend a very special word of thanks for his outstanding work on the eastern dimension of the neighbourhood policy.
I would also like to thank the shadow rapporteurs and the members of the other committees for the many suggestions they made in the amendments they tabled, which I believe have enriched our work.
And one final word for the Commission staff and for the political advisers, for the excellent support work that I have received.
European neighbourhood policy without including outermost regions as active frontiers of Europe in different parts of the world.
As external borders, the outermost regions transmit our values of democracy, peace, respect for human rights and fundamental rights, together with the third countries in those regions.
Regions such as the outermost French regions have an absolute need for greater insertion in their geographical areas.
Far from the EU's internal market, this integration is vital for their development and constitutes enormous potential for deepening the neighbourhood policy with the neighbouring countries.

Neighbourhood policy needs a new impetus, an impetus that also takes advantage of the privileged historical and cultural relations maintained by the outermost regions of the Azores, Madeira and the Canary Islands with North America and South America.
These regions can and should play a more active role in favour of the European Union within the framework of a new European cohesion policy.

Mr President, last September marked one year since a thorough debate, in which I was also involved as the Socialist Group's rapporteur on this matter, promoted meetings and debates with European, national and regional organisations linked to the

retailers, the industry and farmers, Parliament adopted the Bové report, containing a comprehensive set of recommendations to the European Commission, with the aim of achieving fair income for farmers and better functioning of the food supply chain.

Since 2008, the Commission and Parliament have been producing communications, reports and recommendations and setting up high-level forums and groups to resolve this serious problem, but, apart from raising awareness, nothing has changed in practice.

Let us return to the subject, and it must be said without hesitation that a significant number of the problems that farmers are experiencing stem from highly penalising practices imposed mainly by the modern distribution sector.
The problems have been more than identified.
What is lacking from the Commission and the Member States is a real political will to implement solutions.
Until then, farmers and consumers will continue to suffer.
This treaty is useless.
Unfortunately, it is especially dangerous.
Firstly, because it will reinforce with more punishments and rigid rules a recipe that does not work.
The recessionary spiral has begun.
Who can trust a doctor who does not distinguish between weight loss and anorexia?
A doctor who, the more the patient weakens, the more he insists on the medicine, while openly discussing the probability of his death.
How many countries have to go into recession before the Commission and the Council realise that the diagnosis is wrong?
But it is also dangerous, because we here represent the citizens of Europe.
23 700 000 people are unemployed.
Almost six million young people have no hope of finding a job.
Will the Council and the Commission not realise that ignoring this fact and failing to provide an adequate response reveals an arrogance and insensitivity that are politically explosive?
Let us be clear.
The Great Recession is not inevitable: it is an ideological obsession.
There is an alternative agenda, but it must be one of action, not words.
Where are the bond projects to finance European investment?
Where is the increase in the European budget with the tax on speculative financial products?
Where was the barrier that should have protected countries from the ruinous interest rates imposed on them by the markets, leading them to ruin?
Citizens want options, not words, but competent options.
Madam President, the Commission and the governments are now promising to reprogramme the Structural Funds to support SMEs and create jobs.

However, it is worth remembering that, at the end of last year, the Commission also decided on the suspension of Structural Fund allocations to countries that do not meet or fail to meet the objectives in terms of deficits or debt.
You do not need to be a genius to understand that either there is a promise or there is punishment, but you cannot have both at the same time.
That is why, Commissioner, I would like to ask you a very direct question: has the decision to suspend the allocation of Structural Funds been maintained, frozen or cancelled?

In other words, Greece, Portugal, Ireland and other countries will still be able to access the funds to which they are entitled if they do not meet the objectives imposed on them by the Commission and by you.
Please answer, Commissioner, because in Portugal we are very tired of promises.
Mr President, Commissioner, the proposal made by President Barroso to redirect available Structural Funds to support our SMEs
stimulating employment, especially youth employment, could not be more welcome, particularly for economies that are undergoing tough austerity measures, such as the Portuguese economy.

With the external intervention agreement and the great difficulties in financing the economy and, in particular, our small and medium-sized enterprises by the banking system, this proposal is of particular importance for regions such as the Azores, which have high absorption and good use of the funds, but which are located in a recessionary context.

national level, can here achieve a real possibility of stimulating their economy through small and medium-sized enterprises and the indispensable contribution of our best-prepared generation of young people ever, in the best response to growth and employment that these funds
we could choose.
What we need to know, Commissioner, is how we will actually access these funds and how our regions will participate in their reprogramming.

Mr President, Commissioner, the milk package presented here is an instrument for the organisation of the sector, and contains important positive elements, such as the reinforcement of the role of producers' organisations and of interbranch organisations in contractual matters, which in some Member States may be contrary to the rules.

However, it is still far from the instruments that the sector needs in order to prosper in the coming years, within an agriculture that we want to see in Europe with fair distribution of value in the food chain, and which does not turn into an agriculture of intensive large farms concentrated in a few Member States.

While improving the self-regulation of relations between the sector's stakeholders is desirable, to think that this alone is sufficient is to commit a serious error that could drive away thousands of milk producers, dozens of regions and even some milk-producing countries.
The debate on the regulation of the fisheries sector in the perspective of a sector that is economically, socially, environmentally and territorially sustainable is still to be had.
We cannot abandon the milk quota system without having real supply management instruments in place.
Mr President, Commissioner, since May 2010, we have seen how a problem that seemed manageable has spiralled out of control.

It is true that the fight against the crisis triggered by the financial markets has increased the indebtedness of the countries in the euro area and that the most fragile have become particularly vulnerable.
However, can countries sharing the same currency be left to the mercy of attacks by financial operators and those who profit from betting on the probability of euro area countries going bankrupt?
The terrible consequences of the Council and Commission's inability to respond have created problems that are now unquestionable.
Speculative interest rates on individual countries have made any strategy to exit the crisis unworkable.
The consequent aggravation of the country's economic problem justifies the further raising of interest rates until the total cut in credit kills the country's economy and the contagion does the rest.
Parliament has long been in favour of joint management of sovereign debt, subject to strict but clear rules.
We welcome the fact that the Commission has finally produced a Green Paper on the subject, which it has called 'the

Today it is clear that without some kind of obligations of this kind, properly linked to the European stabilisation mechanism, the so-called protection barriers, such firewalls for sovereign debt, do not exist, and as long as they do not exist, the euro is in danger and Europe is in danger too.

In the resolution that this Parliament adopted by a large majority on the new treaty, it was made absolutely clear that it is not acceptable to continue imposing more rules on fiscal stability, budgetary discipline and sanctions on eurozone countries without taking urgent, concrete steps towards joint management of sovereign debt.
The two processes will have to evolve in parallel.
That is what we expect from the Commission, and it is too late now.

I would like to think that this House of European democracy will unanimously repudiate the behaviour of President Al-Assad and the Syrian authorities in massacring their own people, which we are helplessly witnessing on a daily basis.
It is important that Europe speaks with one voice on the meaning of the values of respect for human life, which are being massacred indiscriminately and with such a high degree of orchestration.
The entire international community must maintain pressure on the Syrian regime through the application of new restrictive measures.
The killings have to stop, and this is non-negotiable.
We must insist on dialogue, without a doubt.

A democratic Syria needs a transitional period and guarantees for all its minorities. However, the application of more restrictive measures against the Syrian regime is pointless if all countries do not move in the same direction, and this has clearly and unfortunately not happened.
Apart from the thousands of dead, two resolutions were shamefully rejected by the United Nations Security Council.
We continue to condemn the positions of China and Russia, which are increasingly isolated in their incredible support for the Syrian regime.
Everything else remains the same: a rotten, desperate and cornered regime continues to govern a country, and we, the international community, remain powerless to stop the genocide of a people.
We are already late in toughening the European Union and international community ' s action.
We need to give more tangible support to the Arab League in its interventions and in its intention to send a peacekeeping force to Syria.
We must unreservedly support the work of the Red Crescent in its efforts to minimise the suffering of the populations.
The International Criminal Court must immediately take action to punish those responsible for the crimes being committed against humanity.

And as a form of pressure on the countries that have continued to support Assad, the European Union should also prohibit trade exchanges with all Russian or Chinese companies that have commercial relations with Syria.
and start yesterday.
Mr President, welcome, Commissioner, to this important debate.
We are in fact talking about central issues relating to data protection, particularly in a context where there may be conflicts between two different jurisdictions.
Data protection is indeed one of the sensitive areas that has generated so much controversy and, in my opinion, should continue to do so, because we are talking about what could be the clash between

rights and guarantees, the safeguarding of European citizens, who are protected by European legislation and then see this legislation overridden when legislation from other countries takes precedence over European legislation.
It is sensitive for several reasons.
It is sensitive because there is a break and a doubt in what is, for all intents and purposes, a contract that exists between European citizens and the European institutions.
When they hand over their personal data, they do so in the knowledge that it will be protected by European bodies and institutions and will not be used for other purposes.

So, there is a breakdown of confidence here, and this is data that is difficult to manage. On the other hand, it is also a serious and alarming situation, because the European Union ends up transferring personal data to third countries, thus breaking the seal of confidence that is generated

between European citizens and European institutions. So, if citizens accept that European institutions can be guardians of their personal data in so many different areas, from health issues to other issues, as was mentioned, such as companies,

who require the data – and I am talking specifically, for example, about the United States, which systematically requires data from the European Union – what we are doing when citizens assume that they are handing over their data, and, as you said, that the European Union will safeguard their data, is undermining trust.
So, Commissioner, I would also like to say that these issues have often been problematic for different reasons, but I will always be on the side of citizens' demonstrations, which

want to safeguard the protection of their personal data. If there are demonstrations that are held because European institutions are not complying with this, I think we should be on the side of the citizens, not on the other side.

It is almost like a kind of betrayal. That is why we are asking you these questions, Commissioner, so that you can clarify them once and for all, because no community can be built on the destruction of another community, on the destruction of the rights of others, on the destruction of the environment, on the destruction of the future.

the weakening of the bonds of trust between their citizens and their institutions. In this respect, Commissioner, please answer the following question: to what extent can we guarantee that European legislation overrides national legislation?
Because while it is true that European legislation overrides the legislation of the 27 Member States, and citizens want this,
Sovereignty is a space that, at many times, we cannot accept that other countries, such as the United States, then superimpose on European legislation, which superimposes on national legislation.
This needs to be clarified, because it is indeed a very sensitive matter, and a serious one, and one that we must take into account.

And finally, Commissioner, I would like to ask you, please, to tell us what the Commission is doing to ensure that this does not happen, that it is not repeated, and that the personal data of all citizens and

of companies and of various sectors that are referred to in these issues, are indeed data that people can trust, that are protected from what we are protecting them from. And by this I mean that I do not even agree that all information that is stored should be stored, but that is another debate.
As this is a serious matter and as it is a pressing matter in terms of citizens' rights, I would like you to give us these clarifications so that we can all rest easier. Thank you very much.

Mr President, I would essentially like to say that we expect a major economic decision from the next Council for the countries that are receiving aid, very much linked to the issue of the Structural Funds.
The Structural Funds need to be made more flexible.
There is a lot that has not been used; a large part of it has been used only and solely for major infrastructure projects.
They have to be turned towards competitiveness, towards small and medium-sized enterprises, and towards the relaunching of the economy.
The only investment that countries such as Greece, Portugal, Ireland and others can make is that which results at this time from the Structural Funds.
I would also like to make a political point to all the politicians in the Union, to those who are in good faith in the European process.
When talking about these issues, we need to be very careful to respect national identities and the Member States.
politicians, many of whom have good intentions, but not all of them, have risen to the challenge of strictly respecting the national identity of each Member State.
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to congratulate the rapporteurs, but I would like to say that enough with pious speeches on employment and growth.

Today, with the first results on macroeconomic imbalances in Europe, the veil is beginning to be lifted as to why in the European Union and particularly in the euro area, some countries are thriving while others are falling apart.

Europe is losing competitiveness in external markets, and this agenda needs to be reviewed, because this is where much of the insufficient internal growth lies. However, focusing on the internal market,
Four countries, including Germany, have accumulated surpluses year after year, while 20 other countries – I repeat, 20 – have accumulated deficits year after year.
As the deficits accumulate, the external debt of these countries increases unsustainably.
Of course, this process must be monitored.
Of course, discipline is needed.
But today it is important to ask ourselves: in this context, is the solution of crushing the most vulnerable with recessionary policies the only possible solution?
Will the tax competition of some winners within the internal market continue to be tolerated?

Can the largest European economy continue to develop common policies that favour it and internal economic policies that, while being right for its economy, capture for itself the lion's share of the gains from the internal market?
Can the Commission and the Council continue to impose recessionary policies that squeeze the weakest economies, discussing
openly the possibility of sovereign States in the euro area failing, when they know that the firebreaks have never left the dossier of intentions?
Do the Commission and the Council fully realise the explosive political and economic risk that this discourse poses to Europe?
Yes, we need to radically review our revenues.
But for this, political will is needed.
Until this will is there, out of respect for our citizens, let us stop saying that we defend growth and employment, because it is not true.
Citizens cannot continue to be deceived.
Mr President, I would like to start by congratulating the rapporteurs.
To say that we are living through the worst financial, economic and social crisis since the creation of our modern international financial model is already a cliché.
It is never superfluous to stress that unemployment rates in the European Union have reached record highs, and to demand that combating unemployment be the top priority.
Youth unemployment is dramatic: in my country, Portugal, more than one in every three young people is looking for a job and has no prospect of finding one.
Our citizens are disenchanted.
We urgently need to restore their confidence.
After years of imprudent management of public finances, it is time to adopt budgetary consolidation reforms and to put our accounts in order.
But let us not kid ourselves: austerity without economic growth is nothing more than a partial solution.
Creating more and better jobs is Europe ’ s great challenge.
Measures to ensure that young people do not go more than four months without receiving a job or training offer, as well as reducing the risk of poverty and social exclusion, must become a reality.
Madam President, a year ago, we witnessed the tragic combination of an earthquake, a tsunami and a nuclear industrial accident, all in sequence.
Today, we pay a heartfelt tribute to the many thousands of victims of Fukushima.
Honouring the victims means telling them that we have learned our lesson: we must learn to deal with natural disasters, but we have an obligation to avoid nuclear ones.
Unfortunately, that does not seem to be the case.
One year after Fukushima and new nuclear power plants are still being built.
One year after Fukushima and new nuclear power plants are still being planned.
Japan unfortunately has the memory of tragedies that last: Hiroshima, Nagasaki, and we Europeans have the memory of a tragedy that lasts: Chernobyl.
Fukushima forces us to reopen a debate that should never have been closed: the debate on nuclear energy.
A year ago, nuclear energy was unquestionable for the Japanese; today, two thirds of the Japanese do not want it.
They know that it costs lives, they know that this cost is priceless, and that, on the contrary, it is a cost that increases from generation to generation.
The stubborn policy in favour of nuclear power is the policy of arrogance.
Here in Europe, we also have a majority of public opinion against nuclear power and, despite this, nothing seems to be changing.
There are now 320 000 refugees in Japan, of whom 100 000 will never be able to return home.
What was once familiar is now dangerous; what was once comfort is now ruin.
It is the ruin of a building or a house with radioactive vegetation; it is the ruin of soil or water contaminated.

Fukushima cannot be visited as other ruins are visited, for example, a Mayan ruin or a Roman ruin, but, like them, Fukushima also reflects a civilisation, and the ruin of Fukushima reflects our civilisation.
The one that chose to include in its development model a lethal technology.
An earthquake and a tsunami have no culprits, but a nuclear disaster has those responsible.
In Japan, as in Europe, many citizens have decided to create anti-nuclear movements.
They know better than anyone that safe nuclear energy does not exist, and they know better than anyone that there are no stress tests that can
could simulate what happened in Fukushima. That is why we know that energy policies are national, but nuclear safety is not.
Madam President, as has already been said, diabetes affects more than 30 million people in Europe.
These are indeed the figures of an epidemic.
That is why I believe that it is also justified to adopt a specific resolution on diabetes, especially as this is the first time that Parliament has addressed this issue.
It is the result of collective work, and it is work that does not cancel out the work done so far on chronic diseases, but rather reinforces our ability to act together.
We have around 325 000 deaths per year associated with diabetes, because we know that diabetes is the main cause of, for example, heart attacks, blindness, amputations, and failure of vital organs.
In the light of these figures, we have two options, in my opinion.
The first is to say that it is not that serious, that we can live with it and that each government should deal with it.
The second is to say that the absence of a European strategy in this area is part of the problem.
I understand that we have a lot to do in this respect.
We have a lot to do in terms of early diagnosis, coordination and investment in research.
That is why this resolution is a beginning and a joint effort in this respect.
Mr President, I basically wanted to congratulate the Council on its results.
Not because the measures are ideal, but because they are the only ones possible.
There is no doubt that today we have come much further and we can be more optimistic than we were three months ago and six months ago.
Many Member States have been making a huge effort to stabilise and consolidate their public finances, and this effort has only been made in some Member States due to pressure from the European Union.
It is essential for them to be able to regain their credibility in international markets and it is essential for them to be able to grow in the long term.
With these guarantees that the Member States have given, among which I would highlight the Portuguese State, in terms of fiscal consolidation and budget consolidation, I am
I am also convinced that, with the new treaty signed by the 27, we will be in a position to seriously relaunch growth in the coming European Council meetings.
However, it is only with this confidence, which is being built up little by little, that this growth can be achieved.
That is why I say that solidarity from the richest towards the poorest is important, but solidarity from the poorest towards the richest is also important, by providing these guarantees.
Mr President, I basically wanted to say that we are delighted to see that steps have been taken to integrate Serbia into the European Union.

This is very important because the experience of countries such as Portugal, Spain or Greece, which were countries that lived under dictatorships and which had a set of very serious problems, is the experience that European integration was the way to consolidate democracy.
Serbia therefore also deserves this opportunity.
I would like, in any case, to make a slightly different point from that made by my colleagues.

I am very aware of and I welcome the concern for minorities, but we must not forget the Serbian majority. It is essential that the Serbs themselves have a place in the European Union, a place with full rights.
This must not be forgotten.

There will be no peace in the Balkans without Serbia. Therefore, as much as we stress the rights of minorities, which must be respected, we must not forget the Serbian majority, its identity and its full right to be European citizens, equal to all others, with its own state and its own national pride.
Mr President, last week, Portugal voted for the treaty, or rather the budget ultimatum, which is not, incidentally, a European treaty, as it is worth mentioning.

The favourable vote by the governing parties reflects the accumulation of decades and decades of stupidity and is a typical attitude of the good, grovelling student who does not think and obeys the worst teachers.
The position of the Socialist Group and its leader was to move from a very dangerous violent abstention to a crawling vote in favour.

This alliance makes the Portuguese Assembly of the Republic the first parliament to accept the humiliation of being deprived of one of its most important functions, which the people entrusted to it: deciding on the country ’ s budget annually.
This is yet another coup by the European Union, and it is a coup in which the power of money is increasingly beyond the reach of the citizens ’ vote.
And I ask you, Mr President, is it democratic for someone to vote so that we can no longer vote?
Budget consolidation is imposing almost unbearable demands on some European citizens.
However, every day there are fewer of those who have to carry a heavier burden.
The tax burden falls on workers, small and medium-sized enterprises and consumers, while at the same time there is completely uncontrolled tax evasion and tax avoidance.
The group of European tax experts wanted to know how much this tax evasion and tax fraud was worth in the 27 countries, and it is worth one million million euros, in English, one trillion euros.
This is more than the EU countries spend on health.
This means that if we spent just one fifth of this amount on investment, investment in Europe would rise from 2.7% of GDP to 3.5%. We are therefore talking about an exceptionally high amount.
The resolution we are going to adopt, initiated by this Parliament, is precisely a very clear demand on the Commission and the Council to speed up concrete action to combat tax evasion and fraud.

This means, in particular, concrete initiatives for tax cooperation within the European Union and tax cooperation with our neighbouring countries, in particular Switzerland, but negotiated by the Commission and not on a country-by-country, bilateral basis.
This means reforming and harmonising accounting rules, increasing transparency and reporting.
This clearly means reactivating the fundamental issue of combating fraud and tax evasion through tax havens.
Europe is not a minor partner.
Europe is a major partner in the international context.
Europe has an obligation, within the G20, to clearly advocate a common fight against tax fraud and tax evasion.

Without this, we will lose credibility with our citizens, and so my final words in this debate are a call for the Commission and the Council to launch an effective fight against fraud and tax evasion.

Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating my colleague, Mrs Niebler, on her excellent work and to point out the coincidence that on Europe Day, which we are celebrating today, we are debating and will tomorrow be adopting measures that meet the legitimate demands of our citizens.
What is at stake in this regulation is to impose limits on the mobile telephony tariffs charged to European consumers when roaming, with the main objective of ending roaming in Europe from 2016.
Our citizens will benefit from a sharp fall in prices for calls, SMS and data.
If today there are operators who charge EUR 3 per megabyte, from July the maximum price they can charge will be 70 cents per megabyte, falling to 20 cents in 2014.
As regards incoming calls, the maximum price that can be charged will be eight cents this year and five cents in 2014.
In July 2012, a family with two mobile phone users will save, as the Commissioner said, EUR 200 on their bill.
Second benefit: better quality information for consumers.
From summer onwards, tour operators will be required to inform consumers of the prices charged when they travel outside the European Union.
Until now, the obligation applied only within the European area.
Third advantage: the possibility of more operators entering the market, which means more competition, which will stimulate better services and better prices.

Finally, in the near future, consumers will be able to have two contracts, one for services in the national territory and another for services abroad, while keeping the same mobile number and receiving only one bill.
Consumers, our citizens, will also be able to contract with any European operator that offers them the best price.
This report is a perfect example of the Europe of citizens, the Europe of freedom of movement and freedom to provide services. Thank you very much.
Mr President, in February 2009, this Parliament adopted a report in which it supported the European Commission proposal to relaunch the European economy.
There were projects for clean energy, transport, new technologies and innovation, and the role of the European Investment Bank and the Structural Funds was already being questioned.
More than three years on, we have managed to produce the Six Pack, the Euro Plus Pact, the budgetary pact, the Two Pack, all of them full of reinforced discipline and sanctions.
But meanwhile, while unemployment, especially youth unemployment, reaches alarming levels, the European economy is entering recession and more than seven of its Member States have negative growth.
Meanwhile, while Greece is nothing more than the first stone threatening to bring down the whole house, the recovery agenda seems to have been forgotten.
At this moment, this agenda is clear.
What matters at this point is no longer to give it an urgent character, but an emergency character, in a situation that is threatening to explode.
We urgently need to intervene in a number of areas.
Firstly, we need to establish this European Marshall Plan, with investment promoting growth, employment and convergence.
Project bonds are one of the possible sources.
Secondly, we need to stabilise the interest rates at which countries finance themselves.
Let us at least prepare to coordinate sovereign debt issues and jointly manage the debt that has accumulated.
Thirdly, the European Stability Mechanism and the European Central Bank must be prepared to function as real firebreaks in the defence of European sovereign debt.
Fourthly, the credit that was transferred en masse by the European Central Bank to commercial banks must reach companies.
One billion euros is tied up in the banking system, while thousands of viable companies die every day for lack of funding.
Fifthly, investment makes no sense without demand.
The weakest economies are being crushed by austerity plans.
It is the strongest economies that must boost domestic demand.

is that almost 80% of exchanges are made within Europe, and it is up to the Commission, Commissioner Olli Rehn, to make recommendations not only to the more fragile economies, but also to the more dynamic ones.
Finally, Mr President, we need to put a limit on the revenues imposed by the troikas.

We must put a stop to the punitive style, without dialogue and without accountability, with which senior officials determine the fate of millions of European citizens and of the democratic institutions of Member States.

Mr President, Minister, Commissioners, what I want to say is very clear. Naturally, the Portuguese Members of the Group of the European People's Party (Christian Democrats) support wholeheartedly all measures that can contribute to growth. There is no possibility of

out of this crisis without promoting growth, particularly in those Member States most affected by the financial crisis. But sometimes, when I listen to some of my fellow Members, I get the impression that they think that growth can continue with excessive deficits and with more public debt.
We must continue to insist on cutting spending and on structural reforms. We can do everything to promote growth, but we cannot abandon the path of containing and reducing public spending and

structural reforms. More growth cannot mean more relaxation, more rest. More growth and more growth policies must go hand in hand with great rigour in public accounts and with great rigour in structural reforms.
If we start talking about growth and thinking that there are magical solutions to growth, we will end up once again with an unsustainable public debt and we will continue without structural reforms.

That said, I would like to draw attention to the fact that, at tomorrow's summit, it will not be just this long-term or medium-term issue of growth that will be at stake: the urgent issue of Greece will also be at stake, and I think it is fundamental that the European states somehow stand by Greece.
We are responsible for finding a solution to the Greek problem. I would like to repudiate here the statement made by the British Member, who spoke of the ' Portugalisation of Europe '. Perhaps the
Europeanisation of Europe, at least in terms of values, were not a negative thing. Therefore, I reject this idea of trying to humiliate or trying to reduce any peoples.
We are all Europe and we all have a place in it.
Mr President, Commissioner, you keep telling us that every euro spent on debt is a euro less for job creation. So, please explain why you are still obsessed with this?

He has also come here to tell us that one of the priorities of the summit is to keep Greece in the euro area, and I ask you, Commissioner, do we believe you or do we believe Mr Barroso or Mr Trichet, who were saying the exact opposite a few days ago?
We have talked about maintaining the euro area, but I ask: which euro?

in the euro that we are currently living in, in this area that we are currently living in, or in the euro that Deutsche Bank announced yesterday in a study in which it referred to a 'G-euro', a second-rate euro, in the event that the left wins the elections in June in Greece.
This is a trap, Commissioner, a trap of a Europe held hostage by governments and a trap of a Europe held hostage by financial interests.
No, let us not allow people to live in fear, because that is no way to live. Europe can be something else. Mr Sabi, there is no lack of money, there is a lack of will.

Mr President, Minister Várhelyi, the first thing I would like to say is that it is regrettable that, in a debate of this importance, the Vice-President of the Commission and High Representative, Baroness Ashton, is not present.
In any case, we are pleased to work once again with Minister Vălean, who made a statement here that I fully endorse.

First of all, I would like to offer a word of comfort and solidarity to the Guinean people, who have basically been the people of Guinea-Bissau, a people systematically massacred by democratic instability, political instability and great insecurity.
Poverty was no longer enough: the disadvantaged have systematically been affected by these evils.

I would also like to say that the EPP, during its Presidency, has followed this issue from the beginning, and it is also regrettable that Parliament has only been able to have a debate on this matter at the end of a month.
This is due to the invisibility of the Guinean people and the Guinean issue.

This invisibility is due to the fact that, unlike other very serious matters, the interests of major economic powers are not at stake here, and this is what motivates the European, American and world powers to debate the situation of the Guinean people.

But I must say that if an external factor is needed, if misery and political instability are not enough, for us to take global humanitarian and political action, then there is one: the risk of the spread of terrorism.
least that the situation of drug trafficking that extends into Guinea and that directly affects the peoples of Europe and the peoples of America should be a reason for us to get involved in this.
I therefore call on the European Union to be stronger.

There is no doubt that Resolution 2048, and the decisions of the European Union, the Community, the Lusophone countries and the African Union, but perhaps not the Community of West African States, are going in the right direction.
However, resolutions are not enough: we need action.
We must be firmer and more effective with the States that have the capacity to intervene in Guinea-Bissau.
That is our obligation.

I believe that the European Union is currently responding well in terms of principles, but is not responding as it should in terms of practical tools and on-the-ground operations, and that is what I am asking of the Council and the Commission.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, globalisation has brought with it disproportionate growth in the financial sector, whose transactions are now, as we know, seventy times greater than global wealth.

Opacity, very frequent transactions, high salaries, or very high risk have ended up not only characterising the world we live in today, but also being fundamental causes of the crisis we are going through.
However, this crisis has demanded much greater sacrifices from ordinary citizens than from the sector that caused it.
Today, this moment is important.

It is important, and I would personally like to see a situation in which the tax was generalised in all the countries of the European Union and subsequently extended to the rest of the world, through the active participation of the Commission and the European Union in general in the context of the G20.
I would like all products to be covered, without exceptions.
I would like this to be the basis for the necessary reinforcement of the EU ’ s own resources.
In the short term, we must accept compromises, but we must not lose sight of where we are heading.
This can only be achieved through a clear identification of these objectives by Parliament and by the Commission, and also through a great spirit of compromise on the part of the Council.

This is what European citizens expect of us today, and I can only thank here and now not only Ana Palacio, who was tireless in this process, but also all those who managed to overcome their differences in order to reach a common position.

Mr President, Mrs Podimata, today we are discussing, for the first time, the financial transaction tax, and its adoption would be the first sign that speculation will not emerge totally unscathed from this crisis in which we find ourselves.
This House and the European institutions must respond to the people, not to the financial markets.
For decades, the financial markets lobbies have managed to govern the world without opposition and without any problems.
It is obvious to us that, between 2008 and 2012, the crisis in the financial and speculative markets was at the root of most of the problems we have experienced.

And yet, these speculative financial markets managed not only to ensure that nothing was done, but also to ensure that money was transferred directly from the pockets of citizens to these very speculative markets.
Mr President, we have been fighting for this for many years, but it was only when people throughout Europe began to say 'enough' that the EU institutions finally took some action.
That is the answer we must give to citizens; it is the first signal we must send.
The citizens of Europe, who have paid everything, do not deserve to be disrespected.
This proposal is therefore not complete, not perfect, and will not solve all the problems of financial speculation, but it is an important signal.
We have been... No, the European leaders have been apologising to the financial markets.
They should have apologised to the people they have affected.
To conclude, Mr President, quoting a now famous politician, we are talking about those markets, those markets that govern us without ever having been elected.

I sincerely hope that the governments will send a signal of confidence to people, and I sincerely hope that, with this approval and with the positive vote of my fellow Members, we will begin to talk about justice and solidarity in Europe.
Colleagues, summon up your courage and let us get this project through.
