Mr President, the financial transaction tax should function as a way of controlling and discouraging high-risk speculative activities in the financial sector.
It aims to reduce speculation and increase healthy competition, the basis of the single market, and also to generate revenue.
Mr Imaz's report attempted to bring the European Commission's initial proposal closer to the UK's so-called 'stamp tax'. This made the proposal more attractive to reluctant Member States.
We hope that the Council understands the message too.

Whereas, in the Commission proposal, the tax applied to transactions by financial institutions located in the European Union, but now it is intended that transactions in European assets, even if carried out by non-Europeans, should also be taxed.
This measure will broaden the tax base and reduce tax evasion.
It is hoped that tax revenues will be considered as a source of own resources for the Union or for the Member States, thus reducing the taxes of the Member States, a matter still to be decided.
I will conclude, Mr President, by congratulating the rapporteur on the quality of her work. I hope that this symbolic text
Madam President, this weekend we had yet another request to rescue the private banks.

They continue to use the same argument, which is to avoid contagion in the euro area, and I ask: have we not learned anything? Greece, Portugal, Ireland and now Spain, and yet the contagion continues.
But what containment radius of contagion is this that is destroying us?

This policy does not work, and we should have known that already. It is the same politicians who say that there is no money for culture, education, health and public goods who, when the banks or the markets ask for it, make it appear almost miraculously.
This economic policy is not a remedy, it is a deadly poison for growth, for jobs, for the European economy and for the social Europe.
Markets dictate, governments obey and blackmail the peoples, and that is what governments are doing to Greece at this time, during an election period.

I sincerely hope that, for all our sakes, the Greek people do not allow themselves to be blackmailed next Sunday and that they teach us a lesson, because this recipe, Madam President, is a recipe for disaster.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, unfortunately, the proposals we are discussing here today confirm the ban on anti-crisis policies in the euro area and further entrench the logic of the budgetary treaty.

There are some declarations of intent, but they are very timid. I refer, for example, to the idea that one day we might start to think about ways of defining possible legislation for the hypothetical introduction of Eurobonds.
The problem is that this day will only come when all the problems in the euro area have been solved, and it is taking a very long time to arrive.
We support certain aspects of this proposal, but at this rate everything will be too little and too late.
Putting a country under legal protection means maintaining the rates and the amounts, as well as giving priority to new loans, in other words, there is a need to
Member States that honour their debts, but all the instruments that could allow them to grow and find the resources to do so are being taken away from them.

Let us be frank with each other: the only legal protection that exists in these proposals and that is provided for is for creditors, but even that will not be resolved, because it is not effective even for them.

As we have seen in Greece, Ireland, Portugal and now Spain, this means condemning countries to recession, unemployment and insolvency. I will conclude, Mr President, by saying that the worsening of these situations and their spillover into other economies brings us closer to a point of no return.

In the face of the complete failure of austerity, the European Commission or the German Government are proposing the same policy to us, but in overdose: it is neoliberal fanaticism that is killing the euro.
Mr President, given the extremely limited time we have, I would just like to say to the Commission that it can count on this Parliament, as it always has,
Parliament, in a work that is effective and in favour of the solidity of the euro area and of Europe, but this can only be achieved when the initiatives that focus on discipline, we

we have managed to add conditions so that there is a balanced sharing of gains and sacrifices between the different Member States and between the different citizens, and a hope, a hope that we will come out of the crisis that we are going through.
For this hope to exist, other initiatives are needed, and these are the ones that Parliament is trying to fill: an initiative aimed at investment, growth and control
of sovereign debt, which has been the Achilles ’ heel of those, clearly, which reflects the fragility of the economic systems of some Member States.
Commissioner, you can count on us, Council, Madam President, you can count on us, but the agenda must be an inclusive one. It cannot be an agenda that makes
discipline the condition for everything else to follow, nor does it make discipline the be-all and end-all of our European proposals.

Our citizens expect this of us, they can count on Parliament, and I would like to thank once again my fellow Members, the rapporteurs, Mr Gauzès, who is the other rapporteur, and all the fellow Members who, as shadow rapporteurs or simply as active elements in the construction of this project, were able to contribute
for a Parliament project that is more balanced, more solid, and which I hope will merit the respect, the capacity for dialogue and the spirit of cooperation and compromise that we have shown so far.
Thank you very much, Mr President, Madam President-in-Office of the Council, Commissioner.
Madam President, apart from thanking everyone who helped to achieve this result, I would like to ask that the final vote be postponed so that we can immediately negotiate at trilogue level.
Europe is now faced with three agendas: the growth agenda, the agenda relating to bank management and the agenda relating to sovereign debt management.
Dealing with the European Semester means including these three dimensions.
On the subject of growth, I think that at this moment it is important to stress that the Commission recommendations cannot focus only on
deficits, but also on countries with surpluses, and if some have to absorb their internal demand, others have to release it.

I also think that, in terms of growth, it is essential that the Commission monitors the recommendations of the so-called troika, because when a country goes into recession, it cannot continue to crush its public investment, to crush public spending as if nothing had happened.
The Troika needs to revise and adjust its recipe according to the results. And I would like to say to you, Commissioner, that today we are approving here in Parliament
Parliament, the need to immediately launch an instrument for growth and for countercyclical activity of the order of 1% of the EU ’ s GDP over a period of ten years.
A second dimension is that of what is now called the banking union. We asked in Parliament that this be done two years ago, in the June 2010 report, and it is only now being implemented.

A third dimension is the dimension of sovereign debt: without it, there is no stability in Europe. I also want to say that today we are approving the two-pack here, but we are introducing into the two-pack, beyond austerity,

There is another dimension that has been missing, and that is the immediate creation of a redemption fund – using the German term – so that the sovereign debt, which exceeds 60%, can be managed jointly.

This is a legislative text and we await the Commission ’ s full support for this initiative by Parliament so that we can control the completely threatening spiral that is currently destroying the European Union.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, now that we intend to discuss a growth pact, it is essential that we do not forget citizens and consumers, and, fundamentally, the human and social dimension.
that gives it substance, and which was the subject of one of the three reports on the Single Market Act of 2011. Any growth pact must take into account the social sacrifices and benefits involved.

Just as important as an internal market test will be a social impact test of all the measures taken to deliver the single market: a test, yes, an examination that will allow us to measure the progress made for the benefit of the citizens of the European Union.
Since 2008, Europe has already lost more than six million jobs.
In my country, Portugal, the economy is suffering a contraction without precedent and unemployment among men, women and young people is reaching record levels.
According to forecasts by the Bank of Portugal, the situation will worsen over the next two years.
It is in this context that we support and welcome the new employment package and congratulate Commissioner Andor.
Insisting, now and always, on the basic premise of the need for economic growth, the Member States will really have to concentrate their efforts on creating jobs.

The means to this end should include subsidies for new jobs, tax reductions on labour and support for the creation of new businesses and for entrepreneurship, but also for small and medium-sized enterprises that maintain jobs.
National employment policies need to take into account that the areas with the greatest potential for job creation are health, information and communication technologies and the green economy.
As stated in the communication, we urgently need to reduce the gap between the qualifications provided by education and the skills demanded by the labour market.
On the other hand, one of the solutions to combat unemployment is worker mobility.
It is also crucial that the Structural Funds are used to create jobs and that the EURES portal is used as the prime instrument for job recruitment and offers in Europe.
Finally, social governance must be on the agenda, because our citizens are eagerly awaiting tangible results.
Madam President, Commissioner, it is true that six months have passed since the PIP scandal broke, and the most incredible thing is that, for ten years, a private company has been able to
on the market and had introduced silicone implants that were supposed to be used for the construction and manufacture of cushions and were used in women in 71 countries.
This affected several hundred thousand women, as has already been mentioned, and it is estimated that around 500 000.

We are talking about estimates because there have been serious failures in this process. We know that what happened with this company is a criminal case, it is fraud, it is a case for the courts, but we also know

that there is fraud here, which should be controlled by the courts, and the courts will deal with it. But there is a legal loophole, a legal vacuum, and that is something we must deal with. We have responsibilities in this matter.

For ten years, we were unable to identify what the criminals managed to identify, and so there was a gap in terms of control, in terms of traceability, in terms of the archives, in terms of the records: everything failed. But these gentlemen from this company managed to put into practice what we were unable to control, and that is why

In this process, it is not just the lack of information, which is very important, the fact that there is a lack of information on all matters relating to breast implants, but also the humiliation of thousands of women, and the particular humiliation of those women who had to undergo tests to find out whether they had been given the right implants.

We are talking about a public health issue and we cannot be lax when it comes to public health. We regulate so many unnecessary things. We even regulate the authenticity of spare parts for motor vehicles. Not long ago, we adopted a directive on this.

We have to control what is essential for people's lives. The revision of the directive on medical devices is good news. The best news would have been if we had not had to do it, but now that we are doing it, let us apply it with

conditions, with traceability, transparency, control and penalties, because unfortunately, even if penalties are applied, they will not be applied to the women who have suffered from this problem, and the Member States must assume full responsibility.
Mr President, I would like to make it very clear that the Council meeting at the end of June is vital for us and that what is essential is that we look for a European solution to this crisis.
From our point of view, the problem so far is that we have dealt with each of the issues that have arisen in isolation and individually.

It is time for Europe to have a global solution, a solution that, as Mr Daul very clearly stated, involves greater integration, greater cooperation, greater convergence of European policies and a revaluation of the Community method.
It is essential that alongside stability there is growth, but we must not abandon our concerns about stability.
We therefore need a global European response, not a fragmented response for each State or region in difficulty.

greater productivity and more production possibilities have led to considerable increases in the amount of milk produced, creating critical situations of surpluses and low prices in the milk and dairy market.

In many regions with high productive potential, these surpluses have been sold at dumping prices in the markets of other countries, creating even greater downward pressure on the prices paid to producers in those countries.

This happened, for example, with French milk, which was sold in France for twice the price it was sold in Galicia, and producers who have been hit by the rise in the cost of raw materials and production factors have seen their ability to withstand low prices become extremely limited.

This is a bad omen for many sectors and many productive areas of Europe and seems to be a clear indication of what the future may hold without quotas and without appropriate supply regulation instruments.
Therefore, a thorough audit is needed into the territorial impacts of the abolition of these supply-regulating instruments in thousands of operations throughout the European Union.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur on this report.
This is an extremely important issue for Europe.
At a time when we are experiencing a serious economic, financial and social crisis, protecting intellectual property rights by combating counterfeit products is vital for the European economy.
This protection must strengthen research and promote innovation, and must safeguard scientific and technological knowledge, contributing to the development of new products and services.
All of this in order to generate growth and create quality jobs.

Unfortunately, in recent years we have seen a significant increase in intellectual property rights infringements in Europe, which, as has already been mentioned, represent annual losses of EUR 250 billion for businesses and the destruction of 185 000 jobs in the European area.

However, beyond these negative effects on the European economy, the violation of these rights also constitutes a serious threat to the health and safety of European consumers, since many counterfeit products, such as foodstuffs, cosmetics, medicines, etc.,
etc. are produced in unregulated environments.
The EU customs authorities therefore play a crucial role in preventing counterfeit products from entering the internal market.
The revision of the present regulation aims to strengthen control of the application of the legislation in question, strengthen the legitimacy of its intervention, increase the
legal certainty by adapting the provisions of the regulation to the new realities and developments, thus contributing to better protection of the economy and of European consumers.
Intellectual property rights are therefore a crucial priority for achieving the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy, in the sense of achieving smart, sustainable and inclusive growth.

Mr President, the election of a new Egyptian President in the post-Mubarak era is good news, but we must not delude ourselves: these presidential elections do not in any way close the transition process taking place in Egypt.

It has been rightly said that dissolving parliament just days before the presidential elections raises several risks, and in this case, the power that is concentrated in the Superior Council of Elections
armed forces, was excessive at best. It has legislative power, it reduces the power of the President and, furthermore, it still has in its hands the definition of changes to the Constitution.

All this raises some concerns for us, and should raise some concerns, because the Egyptian people run the risk of becoming hostages of the military power and of a return to the past, with the lack of the fundamental element, which is a democratically elected parliament.
It is not up to us to bring about change in Egypt; it is up to the Egyptians.
but I think that we Europeans must support all changes that propose the restoration of democratic legitimacy and of a democratically elected Parliament.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to begin by congratulating the rapporteur, Lara Comi, on the excellent work she has done on this report, which is of vital importance for the future of Europe.
In a world and a society undergoing accelerated change, Europe needs a comprehensive, integrated, effective and updated standardisation system.
A system that guarantees the proper functioning of the internal market, improves consumer protection and their lives, but also protects the environment, is essential.

Innovation and social inclusion are also very important, as are the interaction and communication of networks and systems, especially in the field of information and communication technologies, as has already been stressed here.
As Professor Monti said in his report on a new strategy for the single market, European standardisation is essential for the governance of the internal market, and it is particularly important
need to revise the European standardisation process, so as to maintain the advantages of the current system and to find a fair balance between the European and national dimensions.

In this respect, the report we are discussing now follows and supports these objectives, which are the right and appropriate ones: to put an end to the slowness and fragmentation that exist and which cause companies to incur costs
transaction costs and higher unit costs, strengthen the participation of SMEs in standardisation activities, particularly at national level, and facilitate their access to these standards, strengthen

on the other hand, the participation of parties representing the interests of society, such as consumers, environmentalists, workers and people with disabilities, and maintaining financial support for European organisations created to represent public interests.
I will conclude by saying that the adoption of this report will contribute to relaunching the European project, to European innovation and to strengthening the competitiveness of our companies.
Mr President, Commissioner, the future common fisheries policy should give priority to its environmental, economic and social dimensions.
in a synergistic and non-hierarchical manner, in favour of sustainable development and in line with the objectives of smart, sustainable and inclusive growth of the Europe 2020 strategy.
As rapporteur for the Regi programme, I would like to highlight the particularities of certain regions, such as outermost regions, whose economies are heavily dependent on the sector.

In these regions, the reform of the common fisheries policy should aim to ensure the survival and future prosperity of their small-scale and artisanal fleets, thus safeguarding and defending the employment that depends on them.

I agree with the Commission's position on the need to adjust this policy and on specific dates, which should take into account the disparate realities of the fisheries and aquaculture sectors in the various coastal regions.

I am in favour of this policy placing emphasis on regionalisation as a form of governance, through enhanced participation by the various stakeholders, so that in the future it will be possible to respond more adequately to the needs and sensitivities of each maritime basin.
We are reaching the decisive moments in which we will shape the new fisheries policy in Europe, and we cannot fail.

The need and urgency to achieve sustainable levels in our fish stocks, but also in revitalising our coastal fishing communities and promoting their socioeconomic level, modernising the sector and supplying quality food, require more than ever boldness and courageous measures.
We therefore have to make choices and prioritise objectives.

I proposed protection for the fragility of my region, I proposed that the exploitation of its resources be carried out taking into account scientific data and giving priority to our coastal communities in their exploitation and restricting external access to beyond 100 miles to the fishing banks there.
The proposal safeguarded the fishing capacity of our important fishing communities, rejecting the proposals for transferable catch quotas.

I proposed that our communities should have better input into managing their fisheries affairs, through the creation of a regional advisory council, more appropriate to outermost regions. I hope that this will allow us to have a better future.

Mr President, taking into account what we know about the application of the third package, and part of this issue has already been discussed today, with regard to the delay in transposing its directives, or what seems to be the latest information I have, it is the case that

eighteen Member States have been notified, probably this information is already out of date, of failure to transpose. My question to you is: how do you view the possibility that the legislation being prepared, particularly the networks
trans-European networks, to include an incentive device that makes the submission of PCIs conditional, or that is

condition the compliance by Member States of the obligations arising from the third package, or whether, on the contrary, you consider this to be an extravagant mechanism that should be left to the regular competition mechanisms, Article 101,
101 and its successors, all the intervention in this matter.
of the Commission and the tolerance, the spirit of tolerance that has existed in this matter. But the question is whether, with legislation being prepared that could
introduce additional and much stronger conditionality, do you think that this conditionality is an abusive instrument or do you think that this conditionality would be acceptable?
I must confess that I share the concerns initially raised about the democratic legitimacy and democratic scrutiny related to this proposal.
banking union and banking supervision, because it is necessary to clarify and resolve the essential issue of the role of Parliament, the only institution directly elected by the citizens.

But I have another concern, which is a fundamental one, and it is as follows: monetary policy instruments are controlled by the European Union. Monetary policy is the responsibility of the European Union.

Bank supervision is also becoming the responsibility of the European Union, yet if things go wrong, if the banks go bankrupt, the responsibility for paying for that bankruptcy will continue to be the taxpayers of the Member States, it will continue to be a national responsibility.

This is not a problem of minor importance; it is a fundamental problem that remains unresolved. Finally, I would just like to mention that the fact that we continue to tie monetary policy to what is a secondary issue in the context in which we live, namely inflation, and not to job creation and growth, as it should be, also remains unresolved.
Mr President, European leaders continue to insist on budgetary discipline, and I say: 'budgetary discipline', so let us look at it. What exactly do we mean when we talk about budgetary discipline?

In my opinion, budgetary discipline means respecting contracts with pensioners and not abandoning the State's commitments halfway through. In my opinion, budgetary discipline means respecting contracts with workers and not inventing extraordinary taxes or cutting allowances. Budgetary discipline

budgetary discipline means respecting commitments in health, education, culture, and not closing services or cancelling projects in midstream. Discipline means putting the banks in order, and not going and taking from taxpayers to put in the hands of speculators to save their banks.
This is the budgetary discipline that I would like to see implemented in the European Union, rather than our continuing with this party of the markets.

If an example is needed, the budget being discussed in Portugal under the Troika is a perfect example: it violates all the commitments that any State should have with its citizens, in order not to violate the agreements made behind closed doors between Europe and its countries.

Mr President, President-in-Office of the Council, President of the Commission, first of all, I would like to congratulate myself on the results of the Council, even though it seems to me that they are a little delayed in time, the final decision is coming a little late.
The truth is that there is progress.
Now, precisely because I agree with everything that my colleague Mr Liides has said, I would just like to ask the President of the Council a question: the countries that are under assistance,
The countries in difficulty have fought hard to ensure that there can be a retroactive application of bank debts in their separation from State debt.
This was apparently not accepted.
However, yesterday we heard statements from the highest German authorities and the highest French authorities saying that they would treat Ireland in a special way.
What I am asking is whether the Council has approved any discriminatory treatment for Ireland, different from that reserved for countries such as Portugal, Greece, Spain and others.
Mr President, Commissioner, it is a privilege to present the report on the 20 concerns of citizens and businesses
on the functioning of the single market, precisely in this debate with Commissioner Barnier, in which we are marking the twentieth anniversary of this market.

First of all, I would like to thank colleagues from all political groups and, in particular, the rapporteurs for opinion and the shadow rapporteurs for their very constructive contributions and their very positive attitude.
It was a pleasure to work with everyone and the report has greatly benefited from our joint efforts.

We are well aware of and proud of the fact that, over these twenty years, the single market has been the driving force behind major economic and employment opportunities in Europe, which have transformed the lives of European citizens and European companies.
Travelling, residing and working in the EU are freedoms available to all citizens of the 27 Member States.
For European consumers, the single market means more choice and lower prices.
For our businesses, the single market means more business opportunities, with access to 500 million consumers, which means more economic growth and more job creation.
To illustrate the progress that the single market has made in Europe since its creation in 1992 until the beginning of the crisis in
2008, it is enough to look at the 2.7 million jobs that were created and the additional 2.13% growth in GDP.
Today, making or receiving a call when travelling in Europe is 13% cheaper than in 2005.
Freedom of movement for people is most eloquently expressed in the Erasmus programme.
Over two and a half million students have benefited from this European exchange programme for education and mobility.
Over the past five years, companies have also benefited from a 25% cut in administrative charges due to single market legislation.
But one question arises: is everything going well in the single market?
No, not everything works well.
The economic and financial crisis that is affecting us has had negative repercussions on the functioning of this market.
Our citizens, in some countries living through the tragedy of unemployment and having to face austerity measures, have become sceptical about Europe and have a negative view of the internal market.
People still do not know or do not fully understand their rights, nor do they know where to find information or help.
On the other hand, many Member States are unjustifiably slow in transposing European legislation directives on the internal market, thus undermining its full potential.

In certain areas of citizens' everyday lives, things as simple as the reimbursement of medical expenses after a temporary stay in another country in the Union, opening a bank account for a student on the programme

Erasmus, changing energy suppliers, transferring pensions and retirement benefits, recognition of qualifications and registering a vehicle in another Member State are still very complex, difficult or costly.
We need to respond to these problems.
That was my concern as rapporteur.
The message in the report is therefore very clear: we need more political will, much more political will from the Member States and from the European institutions.

Council, and above all the Council and the Commission, to govern better and together, we need to win back people ’ s and companies ’ confidence, and we need to make it easier for them to exercise their rights.

Finally, I would like to congratulate the Commission, and Mr Barnier in particular, on the presentation of the second internal market act, the twelve priority actions for new growth, which marks, I am sure, a new stage for the future.
Madam President, Commissioner Barnier, Ms Bastos, congratulations on your efforts, which have already been so successful.
When Jacques Delors was President of the Commission, the EU single market was created in 1992.
Delors then summarised the three basic principles of the single market: competition, which stimulates; cooperation, which strengthens; and solidarity, which unites.
Twenty years after, the economic and financial crisis has rekindled the threat of national protectionism and it has become clear that the internal market is not a finished work.
The celebration of these twenty years has to be used to relaunch the single market, with the aim of putting the market at the service of the citizen, not the citizen at the service of the market.
To do this, we need to define priorities, and I would like to talk about one priority in particular.
It is essential to have a common policy for producing and distributing energy at prices that are affordable for consumers, with a high level of energy efficiency
through the European energy market, served by cross-border infrastructure, with the possibility of Community investment leverage, which represents added European value.
The common market will also be judged on energy.
one of the greatest achievements of the European Union, and it is certainly one of the strongest pillars of the development of the Union, of its Member States and of its regions.

However, in order to be effective and fair, it is necessary to strengthen its policies in the direction of equal opportunities for citizens and all regions, promoting internal cohesion within the Union.

In this context, it is essential to acknowledge that we can only achieve greater development in our most remote regions, such as outermost regions, through their greater integration into the single market.

Regions such as the Azores need to continue to increase their competitiveness by improving their conditions for competition, but we will never succeed in this unless we resolve our accessibility problems in the area of transport.
We can achieve this through specific instruments that better respond to the problems raised by our remoteness.
We therefore believe that the Commission should consider a specific type of ' POSEI transport programme ' as an essential instrument for integrating these types of regions into the internal market.
Madam President, I would like to begin by thanking you for this excellent debate.
It was a very constructive debate and one that showed that the single market is truly a great achievement of the European project.

I welcome the presence of Mr Mavroyiannis and his participation in this debate. I thank all my fellow Members for their constructive approach, and for the sceptical approach of some, which is understandable.

I would like to thank Commissioner Barnier for the commitment and enthusiasm he brings to the discussion of these issues, these problems of the internal market, and for his political will to resolve them.
We still have a lot to answer to the citizens.
Citizens are dissatisfied, they are disillusioned and they expect more from us, they expect more from the Council, they expect more from the Commission, they expect more from the Member States.
This assessment is very useful, this 20-year assessment is important, but, as many people have said here, it is important to open the door to looking to the future.
Looking to the future, we need to consider many of the issues raised here and many of the measures already announced by the European Commission.
The digital single market, citizens ’ mobility, the reform of the EUR-lex portal and the European energy market are examples
eloquent that we still have a long way to go in the future and that this path is very important and vital in responding to this time of crisis that we are living through.
Citizens are suffering, and they expect a great deal from politicians and from the answers that politicians can give to the adverse conditions they are suffering.
That is why it is important that the single market be one of the responses to the major challenge of competitiveness, employment and economic growth.
I am sure that, following this celebration of twenty years, we are all more determined to give the answers that our citizens need.

Mr President, the Gauzès report, together with the report we will be drawing up on the Union ’ s annual growth prospects at the end of this year, and which will precede the Spring Council, are two essential components of the so-called European Semester.
This report deals with the recommendations made to each of the Member States by the Council on the Commission's proposal, and these recommendations, as has just been said, are in line with the
the Six Pack and the future Two Pack, an extraordinarily strong body that strengthens European control over the economic and budgetary policies of Member States.

Finding the balance between this control and national legitimacy and autonomy is not easy, and it has declined to a level of imposition, especially of discipline, and in the final instance may even give rise to a serious package of sanctions.

With regard to the two-pack, I would like to say briefly that it is important that the Council, which is absent here today, gives its negotiators a negotiating mandate compatible with the ambition to conclude this package, this two-pack, quickly.

But more European coordination, returning to the subject, means less control by countries over their own destiny, which requires a subtle balance, but also a greater requirement for the quality of the recommendations made to countries in at least three or four dimensions.

Firstly, it is important to ensure consistency between medium- and long-term objectives, including growth, employment and real convergence, and short-term recommendations, which focus mainly on discipline and nominal convergence. Secondly, consistency is needed

with the conclusions that should be drawn from the analysis of macroeconomic imbalances, particularly regarding the asymmetric impact of common policies and the negative externalities and spillover effects of certain national policies on other countries.

Thirdly, we need to agree that there is a minimum threshold of social, economic and labour rights that cannot be breached by these recommendations. I am just about to finish, Mr President, I just wanted to thank Mr Gauzès for his spirit of cooperation and to hope that he will take one more step in this spirit, so as to widen the base of consensus around his report and that this spirit will also remain in relation to the next report.
It is true, and this has already been mentioned many times, that there are many things we do not know about shale gas extraction.
However, there are also many things that we already know, and we should not forget them.
For example, we know that each shale gas extraction operation uses more than 9 million litres of drinking water, which is enough to supply 1 000 European citizens for a year.
We also know that every shale gas extraction operation involves pumping 30 to 300 tonnes of chemicals into the ground, and that around 80% of these remain in the ground and do not come back up.

We also know that every extraction of shale gas releases radioactive elements and heavy metals into the subsoil, which, together with the evaporation of toxic waste resulting from the process, constitutes a real threat to public health.
This is a risk of contaminating drinking water resources, and it is a real and imminent contamination.

Knowing this, we know that shale gas extraction is also an apparently easy response that only thinks about the immediate future, industry and profit, ignoring the populations, the future and the environment.

But this is also the Europe that knows, in this House, that water is an essential, vital, important resource, that knows how to protect citizens' health and should protect it, that seeks to make agreements to avoid emissions and to promote energy efficiency, because we know that environmental standards are important, environmental standards and health.
Therefore, when we do not know everything, caution is the minimum.
They may even want to do it, but they should not do it in our name or in the name of the citizens whom we represent here.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the last few years have shown us how the Economic and Monetary Union has not withstood the crisis.
We also showed that we had a European institutional structure full of holes, particularly the institutional structure of the euro.
That is why the report and the proposal presented here are so far above what is proposed by the governments.
However, as the rapporteur said, we are making political choices here, and that is why I would like to clarify the reasons why we have some problems and will not be able to support this report.
The first has to do with issues relating to conditionality.

What we see is that we continue to have a narrative according to which solidarity between the different countries will only be possible when all the Stability and Growth Pact requirements have been met, when the intergovernmental agreement, the Euro Plus Pact,

Euro Plus and a set of measures that do not impose anything other than massive doses of austerity in the peripheries, and also a set of regressive measures that, as we have already seen, are not working.
The second reason is linked to the banking union.
The report very well presents the banking supervision mechanism, but it is so necessary that we cannot avoid the essential, and the essential is being avoided in that we continue to add

of national systems, which means that we are all together in banking policy, we are all together in banking supervision, but we are all separate when something goes wrong, and so taxpayers will continue to pay.
My third point concerns the issue of joint debt and the issue of Eurobonds.
Here, once again, the possibility of issuing joint debt is considered, but only when it is too late.

We need the debt as it is now, not just when the excessive deficits and debts have been corrected, because with the policies we are following, they will never be corrected, but on the contrary, they will become worse and worse, to the despair of entire populations.

I would therefore like to conclude, Mr President, by saying that despite all the efforts, I think we have here a very clear example of the fact that together we can still rise, but divided we will certainly fall, and that is why it was important to have even more
There is always room for improvement, but the support from the European Union has been crucial for Portugal in recent years.
Portugal has transformed itself from a country whose competitiveness was based on cheap labour into a country with highly trained, highly sophisticated labour.
But when the country exports, it needs to have conditions as to where.

That is why it is important that some countries with surpluses open their doors to the requalified exports of countries that have moved away from a profile of specialisation that was absolutely unsustainable.

One last point: immigration cannot be the solution. The retrained labour force, for which the EU has provided support, must not now be lost through the mass export of the most qualified people to countries that have the capacity to accommodate them.
This is dramatic and completely jeopardises our future.

Mr President, small-scale fishing should receive special protection under the common fisheries policy, not only because it promotes socio-economic cohesion and provides a livelihood for many families, but also because it is a factor in the settlement of communities along coastal areas.
Its economic and social sustainability must be a goal, guaranteeing a differentiated fishing regime that gives priority access to resources
It allows for local management and protects the fishing rights of the fishing professionals in these communities against transferable fishing rights.
It is also important for its sustainability to pay attention to access to water, especially in areas with biogeographical basins
maritime zones with a reduced continental shelf and scarce resources concentrated in submarine mountains and fishing banks that should have protected access.
In a context of liberalisation and crisis weighing on fragile regions, it must also be ensured that products from international trade meet the same requirements as those imported by the Union.
